body:
I think that's a terminal emulator issue, the shell has nothing to do with the rendering. For what it's worth, GNOME Terminal has the same problem ([looks like libvte should handle that](https://github.com/elementary/terminal/issues/558#issuecomment-756467348) in this case).

Looks like for `Terminal.app` you should [submit the bug report here](https://discussions.apple.com/welcome) (not a bug tracker per se though).

For iTerm2 looks like the output is handled correctly, but not when pasted into the command line:

![Screenshot_macOS_2022-01-01_19:01:24](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/147857184-f39b2f22-757d-4586-a420-ec24e6313c1a.png)

The latter might be due to Zsh's formatting, you should ask in the [mailing list](https://zsh.org/mla).

You can specify Unicode codepoints with `$'\U<codepoint>'`, so you can more easily copy-paste your emoji example like this:
```zsh
echo $'\U1F469\U200D\U1F52C'
```

Since this is not an Oh My Zsh bug I'm going to close this, but feel free to post an update to this issue if you find a solution / submit a bug report elsewhere.

Happy 2022!
author: mcornella

body: Yeah the rendered output works in Terminal.app and iTerm2, just checked. I think the only problem is in the command line, where it detects `$'\U200D'` as a special character and renders it like that. So it is a zsh issue.

author: mcornella

body:
A quick review of [current git packages](https://pkgs.org/download/git) shows that the only distributed git version older than v2.4.11 is v1.8.3 in Centos 7 and v1.8.4 in Slackware 14.1. The rest are all in the v2.20+ range. This is only on currently supported distributions; others will have older git versions.

The reason for a suggested minimum version though is that things might break on older versions. I'm not sure why v2.4.11 was chosen, I can't find the reasoning in the PR that changed it, but the previous minimum recommended version [was v1.7.2](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/498cd722b39215be04468f291a23b6a76b75b095) because the installer broke without it.

In fact, if there's nothing in the project requiring a higher version, we could go back to changing that back, though this could give the wrong impression that we need to support ancient versions.

The truth is that I don't have an answer here. Maybe we could maintain a Requirements section in the wiki for the most common commands, with minimum versions associated to used `--arguments` and behavior.

body:
Same issue for me on WSL2 Ubuntu(20.04). Can we reopen the issue? 

Edit: I was able to fix this issue by changing kubectl.plugin.zsh file:

```shell

if (( ! $+commands[kubectl.exe])); then
  return
fi

# If the completion file doesn't exist yet, we need to autoload it and
# bind it to `kubectl`. Otherwise, compinit will have already done that.
if [ true ]; then
  typeset -g -A _comps
  autoload -Uz _kubectl
  _comps[kubectl.exe]=_kubectl
fi

kubectl.exe completion zsh 2> /dev/null >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_kubectl" &|

# This command is used a LOT both below and in daily life
alias k=kubectl.exe

``` 

I had to change kubectl to kubectl.exe as I am working on minikube cluster so for me minikube and minikube.exe are in different locations.
Also, I had to change the second 'if 'condition but I think it was addressed in: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/10581#issuecomment-1214919336
author: michal-tomczak

body:
Unfortunately we are following the command structure as documented in [1] (also in cheatsheet [2]). The fact that it is not working is an upstream bug that should be reported in [the official repository](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubectl).

It is also not possible for us to change the alias into a form that allows completion of namespaces, as moving the `--namespace` flag would require using a function and completion would not work either.

[1]: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubectl/kubectl-commands#-em-set-context-em-

[2]: https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubectl/cheatsheet/#kubectl-context-and-configuration

author: jondoe1337

body:
> I read about that - the thing is that if I comment out `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` then the ssh autocompletion is not working. That's why I thought that it must have some connection to Oh My Zsh.

This happens because `zsh` doesn't load completion as a default. If you add `autoload -U compinit && compinit` to your `.zshrc` (instead of sourcing `.oh-my-zsh.sh`, that does this in a more sophisticated way) you'll have it.
If the performance is the same than sourcing ohmyzsh, then the issue will not be related to ohmyzsh and will be related to the `ssh` completion.

Sure! You can look in the [`.oh-my-zsh.sh` part](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/4f2d8b4d4cbc51e609f4b568e87907883422ab41/oh-my-zsh.sh#L106) that initializes the completion. You will see many functions that maybe you don't understand, in `man zshcompsys` you will get the answers!
Basically the `.zcompdump` file is the basis of completion, and the `compinit` function to load the completions defined in the `.zcompdump` file. I don't know so much about it, but it's really interesting :)

body:
Thanks for the report, but this is not ohmyzsh issue. The function that defines completion for `tcpdump` is defined by `zsh` itself. I attach it here to try to debug it. After I will email `zsh` developers to implement it. @mcornella could you revise it, please? Thanks!

`_tcpdump` function defined in `/usr/share/zsh/functions/Completion/Unix/_tcpdump` (latest commit in `zsh` master branch for this file was 9/5/2019 (european date), commit 09c6acf7b):
```zsh
#compdef tcpdump

local args ret=1
local root
(( EUID )) && root='!'

_tcpdump_interfaces() {
  local disp expl sep interfaces
  [[ $OSTYPE != openbsd* ]] &&
      interfaces=( ${${${${(f)"$(_call_program interfaces tcpdump -D)"}#<->.}//[()]/}/ /:} )
  if (( $#interfaces )); then
    _describe -t interfaces 'network interface' interfaces
  else
    _description interfaces expl 'network interface'
    _net_interfaces "$expl[@]"
    if zstyle -t ":completion:${curcontext}:interfaces" verbose; then
      zstyle -s ":completion:${curcontext}:interfaces" list-separator sep || sep=--
      disp=( "any $sep capture on all interfaces" )
      compadd "$expl[@]" -ld disp any
    else
      compadd "$expl[@]" any
    fi
  fi
}

_esp_secrets () {
  if [[ $OSTYPE = openbsd* ]]; then
    _values -S : 'ESP algorithm' \
      aes128:secret \
      aes128-hmac96:secret \
      blowfish:secret \
      blowfish-hmac96:secret \
      cast:secret \
      cast-hmac96:secret \
      des3:secret \
      des3-hmac96:secret \
      des:secret \
      des-hmac96:secret
  else
    _values -S : 'ESP algorithm' \
      'des-cbc:secret' \
      '3des-cbc:secret' \
      'blowfish-cbc:secret' \
      'rc3-cbc:secret' \
      'cast128-cbc:secret' \
      none
  fi
}

_packet_types () {
  local -a types
  types=(
    'cnfp:Cisco NetFlow protocol'
    'rpc:Remote Procedure Call'
    'rtp:Real-Time Applications protocol'
    'rtcp:Real-Time Applications control protocol'
    'vat:Visual Audio Tool'
    'wb:distributed White Board'
  )
  if [[ $OSTYPE = openbsd* ]]; then
    types+=(
      'sack:RFC 2018 TCP Selective Acknowledgements Options'
      'vrrp:Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol'
      'tcp:Transmission Control Protocol'
    )
  else
    types+=(
      'aodv:Ad-hoc On-demand Distance Vector protocol'
      'carp:Common Address Redundancy Protocol'
      'radius:RADIUS'
      'snmp:Simple Network Management Protocol'
      'tftp:Trivial File Transfer Protocol'
      'vxlan:Virtual eXtensible Local Area Network'
      'zmtpl:ZeroMQ Message Transport Protocol'
    )
  fi
  _describe -t packet-types 'packet type' types
}

_time_stamp_types () {
  local vals
  vals=( ${${${(ps:\n  :)"$(_call_program time-stamp-types tcpdump -J ${(kv)opt_args[(i)-i|--interface]} 2>&1)"}[2,-1]:#*cannot be set*}/ /:} )
  _describe -t time-stamp-types "time stamp type" vals
}

_data_link_types () {
  local vals expl
  if (( $+opt_args[(i)-i|--interface] )); then
    vals=( ${${${(s:  :)"$(_call_program data-link-types tcpdump -L ${(kv)opt_args[(i)-i|--interface]} 2>&1)"}[2,-1]}/ /:} )
    _describe -t data-link-types "data link type (${(v)opt_args[(i)-i|--interface]})" vals
  else
    _wanted data-link-types expl "data link type (general)" \
        compadd EN10MB LINUX_SLL
  fi
}

_bpf_filter () {
}

args=(
  '-A[print each packet in ASCII]'
  '-c+[exit after receiving specified number of packets]:number of packets'
  '(-ddd)-d[dump the compiled packet-matching code in a human readable form]'
  '(-ddd)-dd[dump packet-matching code as a C program fragment]'
  '(-d -dd)-ddd[dump packet-matching code as decimal numbers (preceded with a count)]'
  "-E[decrypting IPsec ESP packets]:spi@ipaddr::algo\:secret:_esp_secrets"
  '-e[print the link-level header on each dump line]'
  '-F+[specify input file for the filter expression]:filter expression file:_files'
  "-f[print 'foreign' IPv4 addresses numerically]"
  '-l[make stdout line buffered]'
  "-N[don't print domain name qualification of host names]"
  "(-nn)-n[don't convert addresses to names]"
  "-O[don't run the packet-matching code optimizer]"
  '(-p --no-promiscuous-mode)'{-p,--no-promiscuous-mode}"[don't put the interface into promiscuous mode]"
  '-q[quick (quiet?) output]'
  '-r+[read packets from file]:input file:_files'
  '(-S --absolute-tcp-sequence-numbers)'{-S,--absolute-tcp-sequence-numbers}'[print absolute TCP sequence numbers]'
  '-T+[interpret captured packets as specified type]:packet type:_packet_types'
  "(-tt -ttt -tttt -ttttt)-t[don't print a timestamp on each dump line]"
  '(-t -ttt -tttt -ttttt)-tt[print an unformatted timestamp on each dump line]'
  '(-vv -vvv)-v[slightly more verbose output]'
  '(-v -vvv)-vv[more verbose output]'
  '-w+[write the raw packets to file]:output file:_files'
  '-X[print each packet (minus its link level header) in hex and ASCII]'
  '-x[print each packet (minus its link level header) in hex]'
  '(-y --linktype)'{-y+,--linktype=}'[set the data link type to use while capturing packets]: :_data_link_types'
)

if [[ $OSTYPE = openbsd* ]]; then
  args=(
    '-i+[specify interface]:interface:_tcpdump_interfaces'
    - listd
    '-L[list data link types for the interface]'
    - capture
    ${(R)args:#(|\*)(|\(*\))--*} # removes any long-options
    '(-n)-a[attempt to convert network and broadcast addresses to names]'
    '-B+[specify drop action to be used when filter expression matches a packet]:drop action:(pass capture drop)'
    '-D[select packet flowing in specified direction]:direction:(in out)'
    '-I[print the interface on each dump line]'
    '-o[print a guess of the possible operating system(s)]'
    '-s+[specify amount of data to snarf from each packet]:length (bytes) [116]'
    '(-t -tt -tttt -ttttt)-ttt[print day and month in timestamp]'
    '(-t -tt -ttt -ttttt)-tttt[print timestamp difference between packets]'
    '(-t -tt -ttt -tttt)-ttttt[print timestamp difference since the first packet]'
  )
else
  args=(
    '(-i --interface -D --list-interfaces)'{-i+,--interface=}'[specify interface]:interface:_tcpdump_interfaces'
    - listt
    '(-J --list-time-stamp-types)'{-J,--list-time-stamp-types}'[list supported time stamp types]'
    - listd
    '(-L --list-data-link-types)'{-L,--list-data-link-types}'[list data link types for the interface]'
    - capture
    $args
    '(-B --buffer-size)'{-B+,--buffer-size=}'[set the operating system capture buffer size]:size (kiB)'
    '-b[print the AS number in BGP packets in ASDOT notation]'
    '-C+[specify output file size]:output file size (MB)'
    '(-)'{-D,--list-interfaces}'[print the list of the network interfaces available on the system]'
    '-G+[rotate dump file specified with -w at specified interval]:interval (seconds)'
    '-H[attempt to detect 802.11s draft mesh headers]'
    '(- *)'{-h,--help}'[display help information]'
    '(- *)--version[display version information]'
    '(-I --monitor-mode)'{-I,--monitor-mode}'[put the Wi-Fi interface in monitor mode]'
    '--immediate-mode[deliver packets to tcpdump as soon as they arrive without buffering]'
    '-I[put the interface in monitor mode]'
    '(-j --time-stamp-type)'{-j+,--time-stamp-type=}'[set the time stamp type for the capture]: :_time_stamp_types'
    '--time-stamp-precision=[set the time stamp precision for the capture]:precision [micro]:(micro nano)'
    '(-K --dont-verify-checksums)'{-K,--dont-verify-checksums}"[don't verify IP, TCP, or UDP checksums]"
    '*-m+[load SMI MIB module definitions]:SMI MIB module definition:_files'
    "(-n)-nn[don't convert protocol and port numbers to names]"
    '-M+[specify shared secret for validating the digests in TCP segments with the TCP-MD5 option]:secret'
    '(-# --number)'{-\#,--number}'[print an optional packet number at the beginning of the line]'
    '(-O --no-optimize)'{-O,--no-optimize}"[don't run the packet-matching code optimizer]"
    '(-s --snapshot-length)'{-s+,--snapshot-length=}'[specify amount of data to snarf from each packet]:length (bytes) [65535]'
    '(-t -tt -tttt -ttttt)-ttt[print a delta (in micro-seconds) on each line since previous line]'
    '(-t -tt -ttt -ttttt)-tttt[print a timestamp in default format preceded by date on each dump line]'
    '(-t -tt -ttt -tttt)-ttttt[print a delta (in micro-seconds) on each line since first line]'
    '(-U --packet-buffered)'{-U,--packet-buffered}'[make output packet-buffered when saving to file (-w)]'
    '-u[print undecoded NFS handles]'
    '-V+[read a list of filenames from specified file]:file:_files'
    '(-v -vv)-vvv[most verbose output]'
    '-W+[limit the number of created files (-C)]:number of files'
    '(-X)-XX[print each packet, including its link level header, in hex and ASCII]'
    '(-x)-xx[print each packet, including its link level header, in hex]'
    "${root}(-Z --relinquish-privileges)"{-Z+,--relinquish-privileges=}'[drop privileges and run as specified user]:user:_users'
    '-z+[specify command to run on files (with -C or -G)]:command:_command_names -e'
  )
fi
[[ $OSTYPE = freebsd* ]] && args+=(
  '-R[assume ESP/AH packets to be based on old specification (RFC1825 to RFC1829)]'
)

_arguments -s $args \
  '*::BPF filter:= _bpf_filters'
```
author: carlosala

body:
This is a bug in the `_bpf_filters` completion function. I can reproduce the same in 5.8. See the `:operators:operator:(not ()` part
```sh
  +_bpf_filters:65> _regex_arguments _bpf $'/[^\C-@]#\C-@/' '(' $'/(not[ \C-@]#|![ \C-@]#|(\\\\|)\\([ \C-@]#)/' ':operators:operator:(not ()' '#' '(' '(' '(' $'/(0x[0-9a-f]##|[0-9]##|tcp-fin|tcp-syn|tcp-rst|tcp-push|tcp-ack|tcp-urg|icmp-echoreply|icmp-unreach|icmp-sourcequench|icmp-redirect|icmp-echo|icmp-routeradvert|icmp-routersolicit|icmp-timxceed|icmp-paramprob|icmp-tstamp|icmp-tstampreply|icmp-ireq|icmp-ireqreply|icmp-maskreq|icmp-maskreply|len)[ \C-@]#/' ...
  ...
  # after a long code trace
  ...
    +_alternative:46> eval 'ws=(' 'not (' ')'
(eval):1: parse error near `( )'
```

This is fixed in https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/commit/2c000ee7bab65f950fd4bec2077332d90ef5417a, which hasn't been published yet.

A workaround is fixing the `_bpf_filters` function yourself, with this code that you can put in your zshrc file:
```sh
autoload -Uz +X _bpf_filters
functions[_bpf_filters]="${functions[_bpf_filters]//:operator:\(not \(\)/:operator:(not \()}"
```

body:
You need to make sure that `^[[B` and `^[[A` are the keyboard sequences for Down and Up arrow keys. You can test that by pressing <kbd>CTRL-V</kbd> and then pressing Down or Up arrow, which will display the keyboard sequence in the command line.

For me, Up arrow is `^[OA` and Down arrow is `^[OB`. That's going to depend on your terminal (it might be different in Terminal.app and VS Code terminal).

`$$` is not supposed to change in the same zsh session, so when you run `source ~/.zshrc` again you'll write the results of bindkey on the same file.

body:
You confuse the terms `up` / `down` with `backward` / `forward`. The proper widgets use `up` / `down` terms so if you use `history-substring-search-forward` or `history-substring-search-backward` that is not going to work.

This should be easily set up by the `history-substring-search` plugin, so you can enable that by adding `history-substring-search` to the `plugins` array in your zshrc file. **After that you don't need to add any `bindkey` lines to the zshrc file**, so you can remove those.

Let me know if after this change you get everything working again.

body:
BTW for the specific case of `vi-mode`, you can define your key bindings for all 3 different keymaps as follows:
```sh
bindkey -M emacs '^[[A' history-substring-search-up
bindkey -M viins '^[[A' history-substring-search-up
bindkey -M vicmd '^[[A' history-substring-search-up

bindkey -M emacs '^[[B' history-substring-search-down
bindkey -M viins '^[[B' history-substring-search-down
bindkey -M vicmd '^[[B' history-substring-search-down
```

I found that when I used ^[[A, .cshrc wont work automatically, but other keys like ESC(^[) worked well.  I guess this problem is associated with ASIIC detecting. 
There is an option in csh,
`-k  "key is interpreted as a symbolic arrow key name, which may be one of 'down', 'up', 'left' or 'right'."`
and finally it works with

body:
Found this issue because I had the same problem with [zsh-vi-mode](https://github.com/jeffreytse/zsh-vi-mode) and [Atuin](https://github.com/atuinsh/atuin), where Atuin's `^R` shortcut was overwritten by vi-mode.

Calling `bindkey '^R' atuin-search` in my `.zshrc` didn't help because by default vi-mode's shortcuts are bound [lazily](https://github.com/jeffreytse/zsh-vi-mode?tab=readme-ov-file#lazy-keybindings), so after custom and Atuin's bindings.

Since I use [zinit](https://github.com/zdharma-continuum/zinit) my solution was to delay loading the `atuin` plugin

```zsh
zinit light jeffreytse/zsh-vi-mode
zinit ice wait silent; zinit light atuinsh/atuin
```

Another solution would maybe be to disable vi-mode's lazy keybindings via `ZVM_LAZY_KEYBINDINGS=false`.

body:
@carlosala 1 suggestion I would make is to use `brew --prefix` to determine the install folder/postgres location, this returns for me:
- on Apple Silicon: `/opt/homebrew`
- on Intel: `/usr/local`

and then append `/var/postgres`

That way in the future if ever things change again, the plugin will likely keep working if the defaults change along.

Sample:
`alias startpost="pg_ctl -D $(brew --prefix)/var/postgres -l $(brew --prefix)/var/postgres/server.log start"`

Seems that taking [this approach](https://github.com/brgmnn/fish-docker-compose/blob/44a9a5ad593f05ac77e4c5024cfe4a8c55db31e3/completions/docker-compose.fish#L42) fixed it for me.

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/11a87eac47c579366620f995330532f47dc651f2/plugins/docker-compose/_docker-compose#L21-L26

**Change from `ps` to `config`**

Perhaps this could be released?
author: adamwojt

body:
We bundled [the upstream completion](https://github.com/docker/compose/blob/master/contrib/completion/zsh/_docker-compose), but it looks like [they removed it in v2 and it's still not ready](https://github.com/docker/compose/issues/8550). That is all to say that I'm ok with a unilateral change, as long as it doesn't introduce breaking changes.

Are you willing to make a PR?
author: mcornella

body:
> We bundled [the upstream completion](https://github.com/docker/compose/blob/master/contrib/completion/zsh/_docker-compose), but it looks like [they removed it in v2 and it's still not ready](https://github.com/docker/compose/issues/8550). That is all to say that I'm ok with a unilateral change, as long as it doesn't introduce breaking changes.
> 
> Are you willing to make a PR?

@mcornella I could but it's not as easy as it looks actually. Having had second look on this we have some parts that rely on `ps --filter`, for example:

https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/11a87eac47c579366620f995330532f47dc651f2/plugins/docker-compose/_docker-compose#L122
https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/11a87eac47c579366620f995330532f47dc651f2/plugins/docker-compose/_docker-compose#L40-L44

Which is impossible to filter with `config` but in current situation it would not complete properly anyway if services don't have their container. Also I've noticed that for v2.2.3 compose doesn't have filtering on `ps` via `source=build` anyway. 
```
$ docker-compose ps --services --filter source=build
> not implemented
```

So I could make PR but we need to decide if we want to go [this direction](https://github.com/brgmnn/fish-docker-compose/blob/44a9a5ad593f05ac77e4c5024cfe4a8c55db31e3/completions/docker-compose.fish#L92-L96) where all service based completion is taken from `docker-compose config --services` solely. This might be way to go, unless we want to seperate some like `stop` to take from `ps` - which would make sense as they have to be running first.
author: adamwojt

body: @mcornella Submitted what worked for me: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/10638
author: adamwojt

body:
A downside of only use the services from `docker-compose config --services` for certain commands is that for people who like to chain commands it is not possible to autocomplete. Example:

```zsh
docker-compose down && docker-compose up --build -d && docker-compose logs -f service-1
```

For the final part, I would be unable to auto-complete it, because at the moment of writing the command, the service `service-1` will not be up yet.

Is it possible to change this behaviour with configuration?
author: HanKruiger

body:
> A downside of only use the services from `docker-compose config --services` for certain commands is that for people who like to chain commands it is not possible to autocomplete. Example:
> 
> ```shell
> docker-compose down && docker-compose up --build -d && docker-compose logs -f service-1
> ```
> 
> For the final part, I would be unable to auto-complete it, because at the moment of writing the command, the service `service-1` will not be up yet.
> 
> Is it possible to change this behaviour with configuration?

This is not true. Even if using `docker-compose config --services` you would get autocomplete for all services in the file. Have a look on my PR, I describe how I resolved this. 
Also in your presented situation, exactly in current state of the plugin you would not get autocomplete as it is taken from `docker-compose ps` which need to have container created. Maybe you mixed `ps` with `config` ?

author: SimenB

body:
I'd prefer the setting to be `zstyle`-based so we don't pollute the global variable namespace. Something like
```zsh
zstyle ':omz:plugins:yarn' global-path false
```

In the code, we can test for that setting with
```sh
zstyle -T ':omz:plugins:yarn' global-path
```
Here `-T` returns true if the setting is not defined, but in the future we can reverse that to `-t` which will by default be interpreted as false.

This can be tested or chained with the normal true/false semantics:
```sh
if zstyle -T ':omz:plugins:yarn' global-path; then

# or

zstyle -T ':omz:plugins:yarn' global-path && ...
```

author: carlosala

body:
I like the granularity of the approach. I considered an extension for per-alias granularity but am not sure whether the additional complexity justifies the potential use cases. However, I share my thoughts for the sake of discussion.

Potential extension for per-alias granularity:

```
zstyle ':omz:plugins:<nameofplugin>:<nameofalias>' alias <off/on>
```

Use Cases:

#### 1. Enabling a subset of aliases of a plugin

- Issue: Some plugins define a lot of aliases that clutter the command namespace
- Solutions:
  - Removing all other aliases manually via `unalias` after loading `ohmyzsh`
    - Issue: Very cumbersome, newly added aliases have to be added manually to the list of excluded aliases
  - (with per-plugin granularity) Disabling all aliases for the plugin and manually declaring a subset of aliases
    - Issue: Manually declared aliases are not updated if their counterparts in the plugin code are
  - (with per-alias granularity) Disabling the alias for all plugins where it should not be set

#### 2. Disabling a subset of aliases of a plugin

- Issue: The aliases of certain plugins may conflict with commands or aliases of other programs
- Solutions:
  - Removing the aliases manually via `unalias` after loading `ohmyzsh`
    - Acceptable solution (in my opinion)
  - (with per-plugin granularity) Disabling all aliases for a plugin with conflicting aliases
    - Issue: Other aliases cannot be used unless they are manually declared
  - (with per-alias granularity) Each unwanted alias can be disabled without affecting other aliases of the plugin

#### 3. Duplicate aliases (e.g. `acs` in the `aliases` and `debian` plugins)

- Issue: When multiple plugins specify the same alias, the plugin instantiated last "wins"  and its definition of the alias is used while another definition may be desired
- Solutions:
  - Changing the order of conflicting plugins
    - Issue: I prefer the list of plugins to be sorted alphabetically
  - Manually overwriting the alias via re-declaration after loading `ohmyzsh`
    - Issue: Manually declared aliases are not updated if their counterparts in the plugin code are
  - (with per-plugin granularity) Disabling all plugins that overwrite the desired alias
    - Issue: All aliases of plugins with conflicting aliases are disabled
  - (with per-alias granularity) Disabling the alias for all plugins where it should not be set

body:
You can append the plugins to `fpath`.

```zsh
# You probably declared this variable somewhere
ZSH="$XDG_DATA_HOME/oh-my-zsh"

# …

# I placed this before sourcing oh-my-zsh.sh, but I don’t think it really matters
fpath+="$ZSH/plugins/adb"
fpath+="$ZSH/plugins/docker"
fpath+="$ZSH/plugins/pip"
fpath+="$ZSH/plugins/yarn"

# …

source "$ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh"
```

author: rwmitchell

body:
I believe the function was there to fix cases where a folder is used from a non-UNIX share, like when copying a folder from a Windows directory. In that case the permissions are all at 777, so just using `chmod -R go-w` doesn't fix the execution bits on regular files.

I do agree that the function is poorly named and it's not properly documented when run, though the README kinda clearly says that it will use `set755` on folders and `set644` on files.

I think a rename and a clearer message when asking for confirmation are enough to keep the feature.
author: mcornella

That's what I said and recommended renaming it msfixperms as that also removes it out of the fix[TAB] namespace.

> I do agree that the function is poorly named and it's not properly documented when run, though the README kinda clearly says that it will use `set755` on folders and `set644` on files.

You honestly believe someone new is going to understand the implications of 755 and 644 while also asking how to set their directory path?  

> I think a rename and a clearer message when asking for confirmation are enough to keep the feature.

Adding a fixperms aliased to chmod og-w would be generically useful and unlikely to harm anyone in the home single user environment.  The current fixperms is very dangerous except in the single use case of fixing files copied from an OS that lacks even the bare basics of user security.

author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala I get that for the `.zshrc.pre` and `.zshrc.post`, but why not add the `.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh` and `.oh-my-zsh.post.sh`? You are arguing the files aren't standard zsh spec... but the `.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh` and `.oh-my-zsh.post.sh` files are specific to ohmyzsh. And addint them allow all users to keep their customizations for ohmyzsh in ohmyzsh specific files, which has the benefit of keeping the `.zshrc` file clean of the majority of ohmyzsh.

Obviously still the decision of you and the project team, just trying to make sure I understand the rationale and we make the appropriate arguments!
author: jasonlyle88

body:
Don't worry, thanks for your contribution! It's always nice to discuss things rationaly!
I think that it's more granular and easy to understand to just source your own files. For example, in your `.zshrc`
```zsh
[...]

source ~/.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh # here you source your pre files
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh # here omz loads everything
source ~/.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh # here you source your post files

[...]
```

There are people that sources lots of files in different points of `.zshrc` and there are many others that just have everything in the same file. Also some people use `$ZDOTDIR`, so it's kind a mess.
I think that giving the user the option to choose where and how source it's config files is better than default names as a project-wide config. What do you think about?
author: carlosala

body:
I completely agree that it should be up to the user, but I think having 1 defined/blessed way would be helpful.

Since I recommended optionally sourcing the files based on their existence, I see that as low-risk.

So, on initial setup and modification of .zshrc instead of adding
```
[...]

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh # here omz loads everything

[...]
```

Just modify it to be 
```
[...]

[[ -f "${HOME}/.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh" ]] && source "${HOME}/.oh-my-zsh.pre.sh"
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh # here omz loads everything
[[ -f "${HOME}/.oh-my-zsh.post.sh" ]] && source "${HOME}/.oh-my-zsh.post.sh"

[...]
```

This allows for the optional use of the files but also for a optionally project dedicated place to do this
author: jasonlyle88

body: I wouldn't be in favor of forcing those files into the home directory.  I try to keep as much of that out as possible, with most of them in a separate repo directory - with symlinks  to just the essential main stuff (.zshrc, .vimrc, etc).

body: @rwmitchell But this wouldn't be forcing it into the home directory. They would not be created by default. It would just be code that is ready to source the files are there. If they are not there, then nothing changes (so this won't be a breaking change at all). If people don't want to use the files, great, they don't have to use them! If they do, then also great because there is a built in way for them to easily encapsulate their ohmyzsh configurations!

body:
Thanks for the additional link, that's actually what I wanted to link to! 😅 

This is what I added to my dotfiles, as I want to be able to use them in various environments:

```sh
if [[ ! "$LANG" == *UTF-8 ]]; then                     # if $LANG does not already end in UTF-8, try something
  if locale -a | grep "en_US.UTF-8" >/dev/null; then   # if en_US.UTF-8 is available, use it
    export LANG="en_US.UTF-8"
  elif locale -a | grep "C.UTF-8" >/dev/null; then     # else if C.UTF-8 is available, use it
    export LANG="C.UTF-8"
  else                                                 # worst case scenario, tell the user there might be a problem
    echo '! Could not find a UTF-8 locale available, please double check !'
  fi
fi
```

(This won't work for everyone, but it'll work for me until I start using a system where it doesn't. 😬 )

body: This: is not a directory path, but the path to the shell you want to run. /bin/zsh is the current default and a good choice. Changing that entry to a path will result in the error you are getting. From quick testing with Terminal, if a Terminal window is already open, a new window still start at the same directory as the previous window. If all windows are closed, it'll revert back to your home directory. May I suggest that if you'll be switching back to the terminal often, just leave it open. You can even leave it up on another Desktop that you switch to when needing to work in the terminal. If that is not possible for you, then add this line to the end of your .zshrc file: ```chdir '/Users/smitpatel/cs 214'``` well, maybe not exactly that. Upper/lower case probably matters and believe earlier you referred to it as "CS214". That space in between 'cs' and '214' is also troubling. While the filesystem can handle it, it will cause you no end of trouble at the command line.. If it is really there, I recommend renaming the directory name to remove it.

body:
The way that I was able to fix this in my machine was downloading the script and changing the fisrt line from `#!bin/sh` to `#!bin/bash`.

When the installation it's finished the script will try to source the `~/.zshrc` file but it has a problem, the fifth line in the `~/.zshrc` has a bug:

`export ZSH="$HOME/{your_path_to_home}/.oh-my-zsh"`

change it to `export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"` and run the command `source ~/.zshrc` and you will be okay

body:
PopOS! uses `dash` for it's `sh` binary:
```console
➜  ~ cat /etc/os-release
NAME="Pop!_OS"
VERSION="20.04 LTS"
ID=pop
ID_LIKE="ubuntu debian"
PRETTY_NAME="Pop!_OS 20.04 LTS"
VERSION_ID="20.04"
HOME_URL="https://pop.system76.com"
SUPPORT_URL="https://support.system76.com"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://github.com/pop-os/pop/issues"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://system76.com/privacy"
VERSION_CODENAME=focal
UBUNTU_CODENAME=focal
LOGO=distributor-logo-pop-os
➜  ~ ls -l /bin/sh      
lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 4 Jul 18  2019 /bin/sh -> dash
```

And it does support `~` to reference `$HOME`, which is a POSIX standard (see [Tilde expansion](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/utilities/V3_chap02.html#:~:text=If%20the%20login%20name%20is%20null%20(that%20is%2C%20the%20tilde%2Dprefix%20contains%20only%20the%20tilde)%2C%20the%20tilde%2Dprefix%20is%20replaced%20by%20the%20value%20of%20the%20variable%20HOME.)).

Can you provide more information as to how you're running the script? Does the issue appear when running the installer with `sh install.sh`?

author: trolliama

body:
Did you maybe change the `ZSH` variable when calling the script? It's possible that if you used `~` inside quotes that would create a literal `~` directory. An example of how this would be incorrectly called:
```sh
ZSH="~/.zsh" ./install.sh
```

and the problem that I saw was with `ZSH=${ZSH:-~/.oh-my-zsh}` which was creating a literal `'~'` directory wherever I executed the script
author: trolliama

body:
The script: 


```shell
#!/bin/sh
#
# This script should be run via curl:
#   sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
# or via wget:
#   sh -c "$(wget -qO- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
# or via fetch:
#   sh -c "$(fetch -o - https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)"
#
# As an alternative, you can first download the install script and run it afterwards:
#   wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh
#   sh install.sh
#
# You can tweak the install behavior by setting variables when running the script. For
# example, to change the path to the Oh My Zsh repository:
#   ZSH=~/.zsh sh install.sh
#
# Respects the following environment variables:
#   ZSH     - path to the Oh My Zsh repository folder (default: $HOME/.oh-my-zsh)
#   REPO    - name of the GitHub repo to install from (default: ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh)
#   REMOTE  - full remote URL of the git repo to install (default: GitHub via HTTPS)
#   BRANCH  - branch to check out immediately after install (default: master)
#
# Other options:
#   CHSH       - 'no' means the installer will not change the default shell (default: yes)
#   RUNZSH     - 'no' means the installer will not run zsh after the install (default: yes)
#   KEEP_ZSHRC - 'yes' means the installer will not replace an existing .zshrc (default: no)
#
# You can also pass some arguments to the install script to set some these options:
#   --skip-chsh: has the same behavior as setting CHSH to 'no'
#   --unattended: sets both CHSH and RUNZSH to 'no'
#   --keep-zshrc: sets KEEP_ZSHRC to 'yes'
# For example:
#   sh install.sh --unattended
# or:
#   sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)" "" --unattended
#
set -e

# Make sure important variables exist if not already defined
#
# $USER is defined by login(1) which is not always executed (e.g. containers)
# POSIX: https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/009695299/utilities/id.html
USER=${USER:-$(id -u -n)}


# Track if $ZSH was provided
custom_zsh=${ZSH:+yes}

# Default settings
ZSH=${ZSH:-~/.oh-my-zsh}
REPO=${REPO:-ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh}
REMOTE=${REMOTE:-https://github.com/${REPO}.git}
BRANCH=${BRANCH:-master}

# Other options
CHSH=${CHSH:-yes}
RUNZSH=${RUNZSH:-yes}
KEEP_ZSHRC=${KEEP_ZSHRC:-no}


command_exists() {
  command -v "$@" >/dev/null 2>&1
}

user_can_sudo() {
  # Check if sudo is installed
  command_exists sudo || return 1
  # The following command has 3 parts:
  #
  # 1. Run `sudo` with `-v`. Does the following:
  #    • with privilege: asks for a password immediately.
  #    • without privilege: exits with error code 1 and prints the message:
  #      Sorry, user <username> may not run sudo on <hostname>
  #
  # 2. Pass `-n` to `sudo` to tell it to not ask for a password. If the
  #    password is not required, the command will finish with exit code 0.
  #    If one is required, sudo will exit with error code 1 and print the
  #    message:
  #    sudo: a password is required
  #
  # 3. Check for the words "may not run sudo" in the output to really tell
  #    whether the user has privileges or not. For that we have to make sure
  #    to run `sudo` in the default locale (with `LANG=`) so that the message
  #    stays consistent regardless of the user's locale.
  #
  ! LANG= sudo -n -v 2>&1 | grep -q "may not run sudo"
}

# The [ -t 1 ] check only works when the function is not called from
# a subshell (like in `$(...)` or `(...)`, so this hack redefines the
# function at the top level to always return false when stdout is not
# a tty.
if [ -t 1 ]; then
  is_tty() {
    true
  }
else
  is_tty() {
    false
  }
fi

# This function uses the logic from supports-hyperlinks[1][2], which is
# made by Kat Marchán (@zkat) and licensed under the Apache License 2.0.
# [1] https://github.com/zkat/supports-hyperlinks
# [2] https://crates.io/crates/supports-hyperlinks
#
# Copyright (c) 2021 Kat Marchán
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
supports_hyperlinks() {
  # $FORCE_HYPERLINK must be set and be non-zero (this acts as a logic bypass)
  if [ -n "$FORCE_HYPERLINK" ]; then
    [ "$FORCE_HYPERLINK" != 0 ]
    return $?
  fi

  # If stdout is not a tty, it doesn't support hyperlinks
  is_tty || return 1

  # DomTerm terminal emulator (domterm.org)
  if [ -n "$DOMTERM" ]; then
    return 0
  fi

  # VTE-based terminals above v0.50 (Gnome Terminal, Guake, ROXTerm, etc)
  if [ -n "$VTE_VERSION" ]; then
    [ $VTE_VERSION -ge 5000 ]
    return $?
  fi

  # If $TERM_PROGRAM is set, these terminals support hyperlinks
  case "$TERM_PROGRAM" in
  Hyper|iTerm.app|terminology|WezTerm) return 0 ;;
  esac

  # kitty supports hyperlinks
  if [ "$TERM" = xterm-kitty ]; then
    return 0
  fi

  # Windows Terminal or Konsole also support hyperlinks
  if [ -n "$WT_SESSION" ] || [ -n "$KONSOLE_VERSION" ]; then
    return 0
  fi

  return 1
}

# Adapted from code and information by Anton Kochkov (@XVilka)
# Source: https://gist.github.com/XVilka/8346728
supports_truecolor() {
  case "$COLORTERM" in
  truecolor|24bit) return 0 ;;
  esac

  case "$TERM" in
  iterm           |\
  tmux-truecolor  |\
  linux-truecolor |\
  xterm-truecolor |\
  screen-truecolor) return 0 ;;
  esac

  return 1
}

fmt_link() {
  # $1: text, $2: url, $3: fallback mode
  if supports_hyperlinks; then
    printf '\033]8;;%s\a%s\033]8;;\a\n' "$2" "$1"
    return
  fi

  case "$3" in
  --text) printf '%s\n' "$1" ;;
  --url|*) fmt_underline "$2" ;;
  esac
}

fmt_underline() {
  is_tty && printf '\033[4m%s\033[24m\n' "$*" || printf '%s\n' "$*"
}

# shellcheck disable=SC2016 # backtick in single-quote
fmt_code() {
  is_tty && printf '`\033[2m%s\033[22m`\n' "$*" || printf '`%s`\n' "$*"
}

fmt_error() {
  printf '%sError: %s%s\n' "${FMT_BOLD}${FMT_RED}" "$*" "$FMT_RESET" >&2
}

setup_color() {
  # Only use colors if connected to a terminal
  if ! is_tty; then
    FMT_RAINBOW=""
    FMT_RED=""
    FMT_GREEN=""
    FMT_YELLOW=""
    FMT_BLUE=""
    FMT_BOLD=""
    FMT_RESET=""
    return
  fi

  if supports_truecolor; then
    FMT_RAINBOW="
      $(printf '\033[38;2;255;0;0m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;255;97;0m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;247;255;0m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;0;255;30m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;77;0;255m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;168;0;255m')
      $(printf '\033[38;2;245;0;172m')
    "
  else
    FMT_RAINBOW="
      $(printf '\033[38;5;196m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;202m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;226m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;082m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;021m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;093m')
      $(printf '\033[38;5;163m')
    "
  fi

  FMT_RED=$(printf '\033[31m')
  FMT_GREEN=$(printf '\033[32m')
  FMT_YELLOW=$(printf '\033[33m')
  FMT_BLUE=$(printf '\033[34m')
  FMT_BOLD=$(printf '\033[1m')
  FMT_RESET=$(printf '\033[0m')
}

setup_ohmyzsh() {
  # Prevent the cloned repository from having insecure permissions. Failing to do
  # so causes compinit() calls to fail with "command not found: compdef" errors
  # for users with insecure umasks (e.g., "002", allowing group writability). Note
  # that this will be ignored under Cygwin by default, as Windows ACLs take
  # precedence over umasks except for filesystems mounted with option "noacl".
  umask g-w,o-w

  echo "${FMT_BLUE}Cloning Oh My Zsh...${FMT_RESET}"

  command_exists git || {
    fmt_error "git is not installed"
    exit 1
  }

  ostype=$(uname)
  if [ -z "${ostype%CYGWIN*}" ] && git --version | grep -q msysgit; then
    fmt_error "Windows/MSYS Git is not supported on Cygwin"
    fmt_error "Make sure the Cygwin git package is installed and is first on the \$PATH"
    exit 1
  fi

  # Manual clone with git config options to support git < v1.7.2
  git init "$ZSH" && cd "$ZSH" \
  && git config core.eol lf \
  && git config core.autocrlf false \
  && git config fsck.zeroPaddedFilemode ignore \
  && git config fetch.fsck.zeroPaddedFilemode ignore \
  && git config receive.fsck.zeroPaddedFilemode ignore \
  && git config oh-my-zsh.remote origin \
  && git config oh-my-zsh.branch "$BRANCH" \
  && git remote add origin "$REMOTE" \
  && git fetch --depth=1 origin \
  && git checkout -b "$BRANCH" "origin/$BRANCH" || {
    rm -rf "$ZSH"
    fmt_error "git clone of oh-my-zsh repo failed"
    exit 1
  }

  echo
}

setup_zshrc() {
  # Keep most recent old .zshrc at .zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh, and older ones
  # with datestamp of installation that moved them aside, so we never actually
  # destroy a user's original zshrc
  echo "${FMT_BLUE}Looking for an existing zsh config...${FMT_RESET}"

  # Must use this exact name so uninstall.sh can find it
  OLD_ZSHRC=~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
  if [ -f ~/.zshrc ] || [ -h ~/.zshrc ]; then
    # Skip this if the user doesn't want to replace an existing .zshrc
    if [ "$KEEP_ZSHRC" = yes ]; then
      echo "${FMT_YELLOW}Found ~/.zshrc.${FMT_RESET} ${FMT_GREEN}Keeping...${FMT_RESET}"
      return
    fi
    if [ -e "$OLD_ZSHRC" ]; then
      OLD_OLD_ZSHRC="${OLD_ZSHRC}-$(date +%Y-%m-%d_%H-%M-%S)"
      if [ -e "$OLD_OLD_ZSHRC" ]; then
        fmt_error "$OLD_OLD_ZSHRC exists. Can't back up ${OLD_ZSHRC}"
        fmt_error "re-run the installer again in a couple of seconds"
        exit 1
      fi
      mv "$OLD_ZSHRC" "${OLD_OLD_ZSHRC}"

      echo "${FMT_YELLOW}Found old ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh." \
        "${FMT_GREEN}Backing up to ${OLD_OLD_ZSHRC}${FMT_RESET}"
    fi
    echo "${FMT_YELLOW}Found ~/.zshrc.${FMT_RESET} ${FMT_GREEN}Backing up to ${OLD_ZSHRC}${FMT_RESET}"
    mv ~/.zshrc "$OLD_ZSHRC"
  fi

  echo "${FMT_GREEN}Using the Oh My Zsh template file and adding it to ~/.zshrc.${FMT_RESET}"

  # Replace $HOME path with '$HOME' in $ZSH variable in .zshrc file
  omz=$(echo "$ZSH" | sed "s|^$HOME/|\$HOME/|")
  sed "s|^export ZSH=.*$|export ZSH=\"${omz}\"|" "$ZSH/templates/zshrc.zsh-template" > ~/.zshrc-omztemp
  mv -f ~/.zshrc-omztemp ~/.zshrc

  echo
}

setup_shell() {
  # Skip setup if the user wants or stdin is closed (not running interactively).
  if [ "$CHSH" = no ]; then
    return
  fi

  # If this user's login shell is already "zsh", do not attempt to switch.
  if [ "$(basename -- "$SHELL")" = "zsh" ]; then
    return
  fi

  # If this platform doesn't provide a "chsh" command, bail out.
  if ! command_exists chsh; then
    cat <<EOF
I can't change your shell automatically because this system does not have chsh.
${FMT_BLUE}Please manually change your default shell to zsh${FMT_RESET}
EOF
    return
  fi

  echo "${FMT_BLUE}Time to change your default shell to zsh:${FMT_RESET}"

  # Prompt for user choice on changing the default login shell
  printf '%sDo you want to change your default shell to zsh? [Y/n]%s ' \
    "$FMT_YELLOW" "$FMT_RESET"
  read -r opt
  case $opt in
    y*|Y*|"") ;;
    n*|N*) echo "Shell change skipped."; return ;;
    *) echo "Invalid choice. Shell change skipped."; return ;;
  esac

  # Check if we're running on Termux
  case "$PREFIX" in
    *com.termux*) termux=true; zsh=zsh ;;
    *) termux=false ;;
  esac

  if [ "$termux" != true ]; then
    # Test for the right location of the "shells" file
    if [ -f /etc/shells ]; then
      shells_file=/etc/shells
    elif [ -f /usr/share/defaults/etc/shells ]; then # Solus OS
      shells_file=/usr/share/defaults/etc/shells
    else
      fmt_error "could not find /etc/shells file. Change your default shell manually."
      return
    fi

    # Get the path to the right zsh binary
    # 1. Use the most preceding one based on $PATH, then check that it's in the shells file
    # 2. If that fails, get a zsh path from the shells file, then check it actually exists
    if ! zsh=$(command -v zsh) || ! grep -qx "$zsh" "$shells_file"; then
      if ! zsh=$(grep '^/.*/zsh$' "$shells_file" | tail -n 1) || [ ! -f "$zsh" ]; then
        fmt_error "no zsh binary found or not present in '$shells_file'"
        fmt_error "change your default shell manually."
        return
      fi
    fi
  fi

  # We're going to change the default shell, so back up the current one
  if [ -n "$SHELL" ]; then
    echo "$SHELL" > ~/.shell.pre-oh-my-zsh
  else
    grep "^$USER:" /etc/passwd | awk -F: '{print $7}' > ~/.shell.pre-oh-my-zsh
  fi

  echo "Changing your shell to $zsh..."

  # Check if user has sudo privileges to run `chsh` with or without `sudo`
  #
  # This allows the call to succeed without password on systems where the
  # user does not have a password but does have sudo privileges, like in
  # Google Cloud Shell.
  #
  # On systems that don't have a user with passwordless sudo, the user will
  # be prompted for the password either way, so this shouldn't cause any issues.
  #
  if user_can_sudo; then
    sudo -k chsh -s "$zsh" "$USER"  # -k forces the password prompt
  else
    chsh -s "$zsh" "$USER"          # run chsh normally
  fi

  # Check if the shell change was successful
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    fmt_error "chsh command unsuccessful. Change your default shell manually."
  else
    export SHELL="$zsh"
    echo "${FMT_GREEN}Shell successfully changed to '$zsh'.${FMT_RESET}"
  fi

  echo
}

# shellcheck disable=SC2183  # printf string has more %s than arguments ($FMT_RAINBOW expands to multiple arguments)
print_success() {
  printf '%s         %s__      %s           %s        %s       %s     %s__   %s\n'      $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_RESET
  printf '%s  ____  %s/ /_    %s ____ ___  %s__  __  %s ____  %s_____%s/ /_  %s\n'      $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_RESET
  printf '%s / __ \\%s/ __ \\  %s / __ `__ \\%s/ / / / %s /_  / %s/ ___/%s __ \\ %s\n'  $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_RESET
  printf '%s/ /_/ /%s / / / %s / / / / / /%s /_/ / %s   / /_%s(__  )%s / / / %s\n'      $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_RESET
  printf '%s\\____/%s_/ /_/ %s /_/ /_/ /_/%s\\__, / %s   /___/%s____/%s_/ /_/  %s\n'    $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_RESET
  printf '%s    %s        %s           %s /____/ %s       %s     %s          %s....is now installed!%s\n' $FMT_RAINBOW $FMT_GREEN $FMT_RESET
  printf '\n'
  printf '\n'
  printf "%s %s %s\n" "Before you scream ${FMT_BOLD}${FMT_YELLOW}Oh My Zsh!${FMT_RESET} look over the" \
    "$(fmt_code "$(fmt_link ".zshrc" "file://$HOME/.zshrc" --text)")" \
    "file to select plugins, themes, and options."
  printf '\n'
  printf '%s\n' "• Follow us on Twitter: $(fmt_link @ohmyzsh https://twitter.com/ohmyzsh)"
  printf '%s\n' "• Join our Discord community: $(fmt_link "Discord server" https://discord.gg/ohmyzsh)"
  printf '%s\n' "• Get stickers, t-shirts, coffee mugs and more: $(fmt_link "Planet Argon Shop" https://shop.planetargon.com/collections/oh-my-zsh)"
  printf '%s\n' $FMT_RESET
}

main() {
  # Run as unattended if stdin is not a tty
  if [ ! -t 0 ]; then
    RUNZSH=no
    CHSH=no
  fi

  # Parse arguments
  while [ $# -gt 0 ]; do
    case $1 in
      --unattended) RUNZSH=no; CHSH=no ;;
      --skip-chsh) CHSH=no ;;
      --keep-zshrc) KEEP_ZSHRC=yes ;;
    esac
    shift
  done

  setup_color

  if ! command_exists zsh; then
    echo "${FMT_YELLOW}Zsh is not installed.${FMT_RESET} Please install zsh first."
    exit 1
  fi

  if [ -d "$ZSH" ]; then
    echo "${FMT_YELLOW}The \$ZSH folder already exists ($ZSH).${FMT_RESET}"
    if [ "$custom_zsh" = yes ]; then
      cat <<EOF

You ran the installer with the \$ZSH setting or the \$ZSH variable is
exported. You have 3 options:

1. Unset the ZSH variable when calling the installer:
   $(fmt_code "ZSH= sh install.sh")
2. Install Oh My Zsh to a directory that doesn't exist yet:
   $(fmt_code "ZSH=path/to/new/ohmyzsh/folder sh install.sh")
3. (Caution) If the folder doesn't contain important information,
   you can just remove it with $(fmt_code "rm -r $ZSH")

EOF
    else
      echo "You'll need to remove it if you want to reinstall."
    fi
    exit 1
  fi

  setup_ohmyzsh
  setup_zshrc
  setup_shell

  print_success

  if [ $RUNZSH = no ]; then
    echo "${FMT_YELLOW}Run zsh to try it out.${FMT_RESET}"
    exit
  fi

  exec zsh -l
}

main "$@"```
author: trolliama

body:
Is it possible that `$HOME` was unset? [The POSIX standard](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799.2018edition/utilities/V3_chap02.html#tag_18_06_01) says `~` is replaced with `$HOME`, but if it's unset the result is undefined.

> If the login name is null (that is, the tilde-prefix contains only the tilde), the tilde-prefix is replaced by the value of the variable HOME. **If HOME is unset, the results are unspecified.**

I can trigger this when unsetting HOME:

This is in zsh:
```console
$ echo ${var:-~/.oh-my-zsh}
/home/<user>/.oh-my-zsh
$ (unset HOME; echo ${var:-~/.oh-my-zsh})
/.oh-my-zsh
```

This is in ash and busybox:
```console
$ echo ${var:-~/.oh-my-zsh}
/home/<user>/.oh-my-zsh
$ unset HOME
$ echo ${var:-~/.oh-my-zsh}
~/.oh-my-zsh
```

This last one is the behavior you see. We could add a condition in the script to check where the `$HOME` directory is instead of relying on tilde expansion, but I'd like to reproduce the issue first.

author: erychagov

body: Hmm looks like you ran into [SIP](https://poweruser.blog/using-dtrace-with-sip-enabled-3826a352e64b). You can copy the git binary somewhere else (preferably your home directory) and remove the code signing signature, then reference this new binary in the OMZ code. See https://poweruser.blog/using-dtrace-with-sip-enabled-3826a352e64b#a106
author: mcornella

body:
> Hmm looks like you ran into [SIP](https://poweruser.blog/using-dtrace-with-sip-enabled-3826a352e64b). You can copy the git binary somewhere else (preferably your home directory) and remove the code signing signature, then reference this new binary in the OMZ code. See [poweruser.blog/using-dtrace-with-sip-enabled-3826a352e64b#a106](https://poweruser.blog/using-dtrace-with-sip-enabled-3826a352e64b#a106)

The process would be like so:

```console
$ cp "${commands[git]:P}" ~/git
$ sudo codesign --remove-signature ~/git
$ codesign -dv ~/git # verify
~/git: code object is not signed at all
```

After that, change `git` in the OMZ code for `$HOME/git`:
```sh
zcompdump_revision="#omz revision: $(builtin cd -q "$ZSH"; sudo dtruss $HOME/git rev-parse HEAD 2>/dev/null)"
```

author: mcornella

body:
I have the same issue. I think it might be related to the fact that the git binary in Xcode (`/usr/bin/git`) is actually some kind of a wrapper similar to `xcode-select`. I guess it might be used to display the "please install Xcode command line tools" if the user doesn't have those installed already. But it seems to do some other initialization too after every reboot, which slows down the first execution the command.

It might be worthwhile to detect whether `git` in path is the Xcode wrapper, and if it is, print a diagnostic for the user? It was quite hard to track this issue down because it only happens *once* after a reboot. The main issue for me is that it causes the terminal initialization timeouts in VSCode and Intellij IDEA to elapse, they print errors about not being able to initialize terminal and I have to restart them.

body:
That's Zsh default completion for `dnf`:
```sh
_dnf() {
  if [[ "$(readlink /usr/bin/dnf)" == "dnf-2" ]]; then
    local python_exec="/usr/bin/python2"
  else
    local python_exec="/usr/bin/python3"
  fi
  local helper=$(${python_exec} -c "import dnf.cli; print('{}/completion_helper.py'.format(dnf.cli.__path__[0]))")
  local cache_file="/var/cache/dnf/packages.db"

  _arguments -s \
    '(- *)'{-h,--help}'[show the help message]' \
    '--version[show dnf version]' \
    '(-v --verbose)'{-v,--verbose}'[set verbose, show debug messages]' \
    '(-q --quiet)'{-q,--quiet}'[show just the relevant content]' \
    '--allowerasing[allow erasing of installed  packages]' \
    '(-y --assumeyes)'{-y,--assumeyes}'[answer yes for all questions]' \
    '(-C --cacheonly)'{-C,--cacheonly}'[run entirely from cache]' \
    '(-c --config)'{-c,--config=}'[config file location]:config file:_files' \
    '(-R --randomwait)'{-R,--randomwait=}'[maximum command wait time (in minutes)]:max wait time' \
    '--releasever=[configure DNF for another release]:release' \
    '--refresh[set metadata as expired before running the command]' \
    '--nogpgcheck[skip checking GPG signatures on package]' \
    '--installroot=[set install root]:install root:_files -/' \
    '*--enablerepo=[enable one or more repositories]:repos to enable:_dnf_disabled_repos' \
    '*--disablerepo=[disable one or more repositories]:disable repos:_dnf_enabled_repos' \
    '*::dnf command:_dnf_command'
}
```
It uses `/usr/bin/python3` or `/usr/bin/python2` depending on the dnf version, to read the dnf packages database.

The solution is installing python3.
author: mcornella

body:
`rush` has a [bash completion snippet](https://rushjs.io/pages/developer/tab_completion/), you can adapt that to make it work in zsh.

You can put the code below in a file and then source it. You can put it in a file ending with `.zsh` inside `$ZSH_CUSTOM` and OMZ will load it automatically (e.g. `$ZSH_CUSTOM/rush-completion.zsh`).
```zsh
# load bashcompinit
autoload -Uz +X bashcompinit && bashcompinit

# bash parameter completion for the Rush CLI
_rush_bash_complete()
{
  local word=${COMP_WORDS[COMP_CWORD]}

  local completions
  completions="$(rush tab-complete --position "${COMP_POINT}" --word "${COMP_LINE}" 2>/dev/null)"
  if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    completions=""
  fi

  COMPREPLY=( $(compgen -W "$completions" -- "$word") )
}

complete -f -F _rush_bash_complete rush
```
author: mcornella

body:
This was reported on [Discord some weeks ago](https://discord.com/channels/642496866407284746/809850295005741056/940948318815653948). The culprit were the `helm` and `kubectl` plugins. The workaround the user found was to install `helm` and `kubectl` with apt instead of using snap.

Here are the relevant parts of the chat:

> **danielmichaelis89 — 08/02/2022**
> Hi, I already tried my luck in the ohmyzsh channel but without any success, so I'll try again here:
> Whenever I open a new terminal or simply run zsh in an IDE like PyCharm or VS Code I'm getting the following error:
> Error: write /dev/stdout: permission denied
> error: write /dev/stdout: permission denied
> 
> I don't get this error in my standard terminal even though my standard shell is zsh in all cases. I found out that the error occurs when sourcing $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh from within .zshrc. The error started appearing a while ago, it wasn't there when I first installed oh-my-zsh and I can't connect it to any changes to my system I made recently. It's not a big problem but it gets a bit annoying to start every session with an error. Does anyone know how to solve it?

> **danielmichaelis89 — 08/02/2022**
> I'm using Ubuntu 20.04, the file is owned by my user and yes, I can source it manually but the same error occurs

> **danielmichaelis89 — 08/02/2022**
> lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 15 Feb  8 09:08 /dev/stdout -> /proc/self/fd/1

> **danielmichaelis89 — 08/02/2022**
> I narrowed it down to the helm and kubectl plugins. helm leads to the first error message, kubectl to the second

> **danielmichaelis89 — 08/02/2022**
> It seems to be the following lines in helm.plugin.zsh and kubectl.plugin.zsh, respectively:
> 
> helm completion zsh >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_helm" &|
> kubectl completion zsh >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_kubectl" &|
> 
> Not sure what's causing the problem and the different behavior in Pycharm's or VSCode's terminals and in the standard terminal.

> **danielmichaelis89 — 09/02/2022**
> **I figured out a workaround. I had helm and kubectl installed via snap. Removing these installations and installing them via apt instead resolved the issue.** I can now activate the plugins and the completion scripts are written to the corresponding files without errors. Though I don't quite understand the underlying problem with the snaps and why it only occurred in certain terminals, that solved it for me. If anyone has a possible explanation it would be nice to learn, otherwise I think I'm happy with the way it works now
author: mcornella

author: strowk

body:
Aha, problems with at least helm plugin have started somewhere over here 
https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/875e7c4077c08b6cf5547929d403f70a52836388
with the goal of (as I understand this) to keep a cache of completions. However that commit breaks snap installations, as snap does not allow to write into hidden folders, which is what `$ZSH_CACHE_DIR` seems to be (~/.oh-my-zsh/cache). 

IMO, performance improvement, which breaks plugin for snap installation is not a good improvement and should be either reverted or at least changed in a way so that snap installations still work as expected.. Wouldn't you agree that this is a bug, which should be solved rather than ignored?

body:
Would be good to have a trace of the plugin running, so it's possible to see why `brew --prefix` returns the wrong thing. You can do that like so:

body:
Hi @ong-yinggao98, I believe you should fix your setup so that `/opt/homebrew/bin` is first on `$PATH` (do that at the top of your zshrc file preferably, so that the change is active when the plugin runs). That should solve the issue with the plugin.

It's weird though to have multiple brew binaries, isn't it? I'm not that familiar with it, but it seems that you should only have the one specific to your Apple Silicon Mac.

If that's a setup common with other users, we could also change the plugin in such a way that you can configure where to look for Postgres (i.e. make the `PG_BREW_DIR` variable configurable).
author: mcornella

body:
> It's weird though to have multiple brew binaries, isn't it? I'm not that familiar with it, but it seems that you should only have the one specific to your Apple Silicon Mac.

For the longest time ever there were formulae that were not compatible with Apple Silicon (even Cocoapods!) and I had a x64 binary to allow me to at least use them with Rosetta 2. This reminded me that I haven't used any such packages in ages and it is time to clean up :P 

That said, it still gave me the same problem (this time that there was no such `brew` command). As it turns out, I had left `eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"` in my script in `ZSH_CUSTOM` instead of on the top of `.zshrc` which probably resulted in `PG_BREW_DIR` being initialised before the brew environment was set. Moving that line back to `.zshrc` solved the problem. Thanks for the help!

body: This happens when you have write permissions for `others` in that folder. Probably you get those directories from a Windows system that gives `777` permissions, which means that every user in your system can read and write in that folder. You can get rid of that warning executing `chmod 755 -R <folder_you_want>`. There's a plugin in `omz` to manage that kind of permissions (`perms` plugin). By the way, it's an advanced plugin, so don't use it if you are not sure about what are you doing! 😉

body:
Hi @cooniur, sorry for the confusion.

You should be able to manually update the remote with `git remote set-url origin "https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git` assuming `origin` is the remote pointing to this repository (source https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/10778#issuecomment-1068832240)

Unfortunately since the dotfiles you use are using `git:` in the submodules, you have to use a different command. Provided you have git 2.25 or older, you can run this command to change the URL for ohmyzsh. **Note this is only specific to [the dotfiles used by @cooniur](https://github.com/atomantic/dotfiles/blob/main/.gitmodules)**:
```sh
cd <dotfiles-path>
git submodule set-url -- oh-my-zsh "https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git"
```

You should update the rest of the submodules in a similar manner.

Duplicate issue is at #10778.

author: asharpe

body:
A workaround at the moment is to use `NVM_HOMEBREW` to set the right directory, before Oh My Zsh loads:
```sh
NVM_HOMEBREW="$(brew --prefix nvm)"
```
I support making the change though, and to be extra cautious about calls to `brew` we can check for the value of `HOMEBREW_PREFIX` before:
```patch
diff --git a/plugins/nvm/nvm.plugin.zsh b/plugins/nvm/nvm.plugin.zsh
index 3ef8cc1d..b8f28c27 100644
--- a/plugins/nvm/nvm.plugin.zsh
+++ b/plugins/nvm/nvm.plugin.zsh
@@ -16,7 +16,7 @@ if [[ -f "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ]]; then
 else
   # Otherwise try to load nvm installed via Homebrew
   # User can set this if they have an unusual Homebrew setup
-  NVM_HOMEBREW="${NVM_HOMEBREW:-/usr/local/opt/nvm}"
+  NVM_HOMEBREW="${NVM_HOMEBREW:-${HOMEBREW_PREFIX:-$(brew --prefix)}/opt/nvm}"
   # Load nvm from Homebrew location if it exists
   if [[ -f "$NVM_HOMEBREW/nvm.sh" ]]; then
     source "$NVM_HOMEBREW/nvm.sh" ${NVM_LAZY+"--no-use"}
```

body:
You can't add a query parameter to a `file:` URIs and have it run something. The open command opens `file:` URIs by looking at the filetype and opening the defined program for that specific filetype. If you add a query parameter, that just looks for a file with the query parameter as part of its filename. So this is not possible to do as part of the `web_search` plugin.

This is though already implemented as part of `rustup`:
```sh
rustup docs --book <topic>
```

body:
For anyone else stumbling upon this:

[zsh-async](https://github.com/mafredri/zsh-async) provides the [async.plugin.zsh](https://github.com/mafredri/zsh-async/blob/main/async.plugin.zsh) plugin file which does nothing else but source the real `async.zsh`. You can use it directly with OMZ plugin system by cloning the repo into your `"${ZSH_CUSTOM}/plugins"` (usually `~/.oh-my-zsh/custom/plugins`) but make sure to clone as `async` (and not `zsh-async`) like this:
```bash
git clone git@github.com:mafredri/zsh-async.git "${ZSH_CUSTOM}/plugins/async"
```
_The reason is all entries under your `plugins` directory are added to your `$fpath` if it contains a matching function import. However, the `*.plugin.zsh` uses the the name `async` but the repo is named `zsh-async`._ 

Once the `async` function is in your `$fpath` you can add it to the list of plugins to be loaded. So somewhere in your `$ZSHDOTFILE/.zshrc`
```
plugins=(... async ...)
```

Also:
```
async () {
	async_init
}
```
is starting the init, and you won't have to. Just for scripts make sure they also have a sources `zsh-async` (since usually the plugins are only loaded for interactive shells)

body:
I have been researching this further, and this is expected behavior when pressing TAB twice and having no paginated completion.

You can sort of work around this by enabling paginated completion. You can use the settings below and put them on your zshrc file:
```zsh
zstyle ':completion:*' list-prompt   ''
zstyle ':completion:*' select-prompt ''
```

This will show all completions in a menu when there are more entries than fit in the page. You can scroll on it by pressing TAB.

Either way, the best approach is to be aware that there will be a lot of entries, and to never press TAB twice to complete a command (you can press TAB again to select the first entry, but don't do it as a default and you don't need to do it either to get completion). On a single TAB press, you also get a reminder of the number of entries:

```console
$ apt install 
zsh: do you wish to see all 57056 possibilities (28528 lines)? 
```
author: mcornella

body: I was able to narrow this to the `matcher-list` zstyle.
author: bjo0

body:
Workaround:
```

```
author: bjo0

body: This came up because [swift-argument-parser](https://github.com/apple/swift-argument-parser) organizes enumerable flag arguments to share help output by default. It gives the ability to customize the help output for each, but users generally prefer for them to share help values because it allows for more concise and helpful help output.

I know this is very old, but feel free to reopen if you have more details on the reproduction. Thanks!
author: mcornella

body:
.zshrc and whatnot are the config files.

ohmyzsh falls into the XDG_DATA_HOME category.

You can install ohmyzsh to any directory you want: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh#custom-directory
author: Cyberbeni

body:
[Spec says](https://specifications.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-latest.html):

> $XDG_DATA_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific data files should be stored. If $XDG_DATA_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.local/share should be used.

> $XDG_CONFIG_HOME defines the base directory relative to which user-specific configuration files should be stored. If $XDG_CONFIG_HOME is either not set or empty, a default equal to $HOME/.config should be used.

So if `~/.oh-my-sh` is data it should go to `~/.local/share/oh-my-sh`.

author: drcongo

body:
Here's the code for `up-line-or-beginning-search`:
```sh
up-line-or-beginning-search () {
	emulate -L zsh
	typeset -g __searching __savecursor
	if [[ $LBUFFER == *$'\n'* ]]
	then
		zle .up-line-or-history
		__searching='' 
	elif [[ -n $PREBUFFER ]] && zstyle -t ':zle:up-line-or-beginning-search' edit-buffer
	then
		zle .push-line-or-edit
	else
		[[ $LASTWIDGET = $__searching ]] && CURSOR=$__savecursor 
		__savecursor=$CURSOR 
		__searching=$WIDGET 
		zle .history-beginning-search-backward
		zstyle -T ':zle:up-line-or-beginning-search' leave-cursor && zle .end-of-line
	fi
}
```

So here's what happens on the first run:
```
+up-line-or-beginning-search:6> typeset -g __searching __savecursor
+up-line-or-beginning-search:8> [[ '' == *$'\n'* ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:11> [[ -n '' ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:16> [[ zle-line-init = up-line-or-beginning-search ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:17> __savecursor=0
+up-line-or-beginning-search:18> __searching=up-line-or-beginning-search
+up-line-or-beginning-search:19> zle .history-beginning-search-backward
+up-line-or-beginning-search:20> zstyle -T :zle:up-line-or-beginning-search leave-cursor
+up-line-or-beginning-search:21> zle .end-of-line
```
Since no newlines are present in the buffer (the command line), the normal backwards search happens. This calls the `history-beginning-search-backward`. [This is what this widget does](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Zsh-Line-Editor.html#index-history_002dbeginning_002dsearch_002dbackward):

> history-beginning-search-backward
> > Search backward in the history for a line beginning with the current line up to the cursor.

The key here is that it only searches matching commands in the history **up to the cursor**.

After that, the `up-line-or-beginning-search` does this:
```sh
zstyle -T ':zle:up-line-or-beginning-search' leave-cursor && zle .end-of-line
```
This tests whether the `leave-cursor` setting is undefined or `true` for `:zle:up-line-or-beginning-search`. It it's one or the other, then the widget runs `zle .end-of-line` **which moves the cursor to the end of the command line**.

----

Now, this is what happens on the second run:
```
+up-line-or-beginning-search:4> emulate -L zsh
+up-line-or-beginning-search:6> typeset -g __searching __savecursor
+up-line-or-beginning-search:8> [[ 'functions -T up-line-or-beginning-search' == *$'\n'* ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:11> [[ -n '' ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:16> [[ autosuggest-suggest = up-line-or-beginning-search ]]
+up-line-or-beginning-search:17> __savecursor=40
+up-line-or-beginning-search:18> __searching=up-line-or-beginning-search
+up-line-or-beginning-search:19> zle .history-beginning-search-backward
+up-line-or-beginning-search:20> zstyle -T :zle:up-line-or-beginning-search leave-cursor
+up-line-or-beginning-search:21> zle .end-of-line
```

On a working run, the code
```sh
[[ $LASTWIDGET = $__searching ]] && CURSOR=$__savecursor 
```
would see that `$LASTWIDGET` is `up-line-or-beginning-search`, therefore the check would pass and `$CURSOR` would be reset to `$__savecursor`, resetting temporarily the cursor to the first position you had when first searching the history.

Since this is not happening, because `$LASTWIDGET` is `autosuggest-suggest` (a part of `zsh-autosuggestions`), the cursor keeps being set at the end of the command line.

This means that in next Up-Arrow presses, the widget will search backwards for commands that match the whole line (or have extra content passed the cursor). Example:
- If the previous command found was `ls -l`, commands starting with `ls -l` will be matched.
- If another match has extra content passed that, e.g. `ls -l /super/secret/folder`, the next runs will look for commands that begin with `ls -l /super/secret/folder`.

Now, this only happens because after the history search, `zle .end-of-line` is called. There's a hacky fix to this, which is to disable this cursor movement to the end of the line, using `zstyle`:

```sh
zstyle ':zle:up-line-or-beginning-search' leave-cursor false
```

A better solution would be to figure out why `zsh-autosuggestions` is messing with the run here, so that `CURSOR` is always reset to the first starting position before calling `history-beginning-search-backward`. Looks like this was already reported in https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions/issues/227 and fixed in https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions/pull/319, maybe this is a regression that you can report.

I've tried using zsh-autosuggestions but I can't trigger the error, is it possible that you're using a version older than https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions/commit/6dfe9c8cd82c0035632857af034030bbb4637c6a?

author: IntelligentFunction

body:
Probably a `$PATH` issue. `node`, `ruby` or `python3` must be in `$PATH` before the `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` line, otherwise the plugin exits and doesn't set up the functions.

This is how it should look like in your `.zshrc` file:

```
# Set up $PATH as needed
PATH="$PATH:/any/other/paths"

# Other configurations...

plugins=(... jsontools ...)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# Other configurations...
```

I believe currently something like [`jq`](https://jqlang.github.io/jq/) or [`fx`](https://fx.wtf/) are more helpful for JSON data.

author: vortexvi

body:
Successfully set up in https://github.com/ohmyzsh/wiki.

Steps followed:
1. Prepare ohmyzsh/wiki repository:
	  1.1. Clone .wiki repository
	  1.2. Create ohmyzsh/wiki repository and add remote locally
	  1.3. Push cloned repository to ohmyzsh/wiki (renaming `master` branch to `main`)
2. [Create Deploy key (normal SSH ed25519 key pair)](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent#generating-a-new-ssh-key)
3. Add the public key to ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh, under [Deploy keys section in Settings](https://docs.github.com/en/developers/overview/managing-deploy-keys#deploy-keys).
4. Create GitHub Action to automatically push to .wiki repository\*:
	  4.1. Check out repository: the whole history (`fetch-depth: 0` with `action/checkout@v3`)
	  4.2. Set up deploy key (private key):
		    * Create `~/.ssh` directory.
		    * Create private key at `~/.ssh/id_ed25519` from a repository secret
		    * `chmod 600` the private key
	  4.3. `git push --force` to .wiki repository. 
	       The `git` command automatically uses the `id_ed25519` (see `man ssh` for a list of automatically tested keys).

\* NOTE: .wiki repositories need to use the `master` branch. If it doesn't exist, the wiki looks empty.

Pending:
- [x] Add notice to all wiki files to direct changes to `ohmyzsh/wiki`.
- [ ] Automatically reject PRs changing protected files.
- [ ] Document infrastructure in some internal documentation system.

author: ukantjadia

body:
It's not about the terminal. What's happening is that the tmux config has a
keybinding set in tmux.conf. If that tmux.conf has the same (or the
default) keybinding on the remote host as it does on the host you initiate
the ssh connection from, the local tmux will "eat" the input when it sees
that keybinding. To handle this, you need a distinct keybinding config on
the remote side.

I used to carry config to do this in my dotfiles:
https://github.com/bdwalton/config/blob/650ef8cd026864cf4cee719e031a49b285ca884c/dotfiles/_tmux.conf#L88
still facilitates loading custom configs based on an environment variable.

https://github.com/bdwalton/config/blob/0fb1a9b475e06d51481db2bd040ff83151c94990/dotfiles/_tmux-csw
is an example of one I used where I overrode the keybinding.

HTH

Thanks
-Ben

author: hofbi

body:
It seems to output nothing.

```zsh
$ echo ${functions_source[proxy]}

$ result=${functions_source[proxy]}
$ echo ${#result}
0
```

And as you suggested in #9558 : 
```zsh
$ whence -c proxy
proxy () {
        if [[ -n "$DEFAULT_PROXY" && -z "$SHELLPROXY_URL" ]]
        then
                echo "proxy: DEFAULT_PROXY is deprecated, use SHELLPROXY_URL instead" >&2
                SHELLPROXY_URL="$DEFAULT_PROXY"
                unset DEFAULT_PROXY
        fi
        if [[ -n "$CONFIG_PROXY" && -z "$SHELLPROXY_CONFIG" ]]
        then
                echo "proxy: CONFIG_PROXY is deprecated, use SHELLPROXY_CONFIG instead" >&2
                SHELLPROXY_CONFIG="$CONFIG_PROXY"
                unset CONFIG_PROXY
        fi
        local proxy="${functions_source[$0]:A:h}/proxy.py"
        local output
        output="$(SHELLPROXY_URL="$SHELLPROXY_URL" SHELLPROXY_CONFIG="$SHELLPROXY_CONFIG" "$proxy" "$1")"  || return $?
        source <(echo "$output")
}

$ functions -t proxy
$ proxy --help

body:
It looks like the OMZ folder is missing the `.git` directory. Maybe it got deleted or something. Try reinstalling it by removing the directory and cloning it back in the same place:

> **NOTE: before running `rm -rf "$ZSH"` make sure the value of `$ZSH` is correct. It should point to the directory where you installed Oh My Zsh, which by default is at `$HOME/.oh-my-zsh`**

```zsh
rm -rf "$ZSH"
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git "$ZSH"
```
You should be able to update again after that.

body:
On a Mac, if the xcode command-line license agreement has been updated and needs to be accepted, any tool that has dependencies on it can fail. In this case, the 'git' command that does the oh-my-zsh update checking is failing. 

To solve, issue following and type 'agree' to accept the license terms:

`sudo xcodebuild -license`

This solved it for me.

author: tomharrisonjr

body:
If anyone else is running into issues after the macOS 12.6 update, this worked for me:

I kept getting an error loop prompting me to install the Xcode command line tools. It would say it succeeded but then running `git --version` would throw a bunch of unintelligible nonsense and prompt me to install them again.

I ultimately used [AppCleaner](https://freemacsoft.net/appcleaner/) to locate all of Xcode's settings folders and delete everything but the Xcode binary itself. I then opened the Xcode app and accepted the install prompts which fixed the CLI tools. 

Then I reran the usual oh-my-zsh installer script and everything was dandy!

In my case, it needed to authenticate with PIV card, which it finally prompted for.

author: YutoYasunaga

body:
**This fixes:**
`[oh-my-zsh] Can't update: not a git repository.`

**Background**:
I've been using numerous instances of Xcode, and before the Xcode 14 update, I set the developer path in ZSH to be:
`/Applications/Xcode-13.3.1.app/Contents/Developer` and I forgot about it. So after the update when the name of Xcode got changed to simply `Xcode.app`, ZSH did not know where to look for the xcode tools.

Running the command suggested above in this thread, has hinted me how to resolve the issue, here's an excerpt:

`git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git "${ZSH:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh}"`
`xcrun: error: active developer path ("/Applications/Xcode-13.3.1.app/Contents/Developer") does not exist`

**Solution:**
And the solution was simple:
`sudo xcode-select --switch /Applications/Xcode.app/` - before running it, check how Xcode is called in the Applications folder on your machine :)
