Just to recap, this only happens when there's an error displayed, so the arrow is shown in the right prompt. That character is in the UTF-8 range, meaning that you need to have a UTF-8 locale.

body:
You have some merge conflicts, it looks like you edited those files. I'd recommend to do a `git reset --hard origin/master` and then `omz update`.
Do this in the omz folder (you can go there doing `cd $ZSH`).

Note that this commands are going to destroy every change you did in the `.oh-my-zsh` folder, so caution with that.

author: victor-paredes

body:
If it helps anyone who comes across this, it's also possible to reconcile the merge conflict and then update without the reset. 
Running `git status` within the `.oh-my-zsh` directory will tell you what files might have a merge conflict. You can use your code editor of choice (I like VS Code) to fix it. When there is no longer a conflict, running `omz update` works like a charm. This will help you avoid potentially overwriting your customizations.

author: Jolg42

body:
Hi!
I'd say it's better to stick to the regular workflow. If you want to execute something from `node_modules/.bin`, you can always do `npm/pnpm/yarn/whatever <binary>`, and it's going to use the `node_modules/.bin` entry, as it's prepended in all package managers to all other entries in the `PATH`.
I imagine the case of someone using `prettier`. In that project, they used the `v2`; in the global path, they installed `prettier` `v3`. I'd expect to execute the `v2` if I'm inside the project, and in this case, it wouldn't be the case since the PATH would be appended.
Executing the `<pkgManager> <binary>` does not seem a significant burden, and this plugin might have some problems like the one mentioned before, leading to non-deterministic execution in different machines.
Thanks for the idea and the comments!
author: carlosala

body:
Thanks for the feedback! I do have some counter arguments. I don’t expect to change your mind, but I’ll give it a try anyway.

- `npx` / `pnpx` / `yarn` / etc give additional runtime overhead, making the command slower to run. Running the command directly is faster.
- If no package manager is used, ZSH can autocomplete commands.
- If no package manager is used, This integrates with `thefuck`.
- I think linters / formatters / test runners are good examples of what this is useful for. I don’t think users typically install those globally.
- Oh My Zsh plugins are opt-in. The user needs to explicitly enable this.

Let me know if this changes anything. If not, I’ll publish it as an external plugin. :)

body: Hi Rupert, that happens because you technically modified the git repository, and I believe symlinks outside a git repository are not supported. Instead, you can use the `ZSH_CUSTOM` setting to point it to wherever you moved the folder: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Settings#zsh_custom.
author: mcornella

body:
> [...] Instead, you can use the `ZSH_CUSTOM` setting to point it to wherever you moved the folder: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Settings#zsh_custom.

It's worth noting that the symlink check is independent of the `ZSH_CUSTOM` setting. 

If the original symlink is still present, the error will persist regardless of the `ZSH_CUSTOM` value, so the symlink (quite rightly) also needs to be removed to get `omz update` working again.

body:
You can use `(N)` to enable `NULL_GLOB` for a glob string, which turns off no match errors. Like this:
```sh
ls BMV*(N) P*/BMV*(N)
```
Note that all this does is to not add an argument to the command executed if the glob fails, so the `ls` command will still run without argument if no matches are found:
```console
$ ls BMV*(N)
# BMV* does not find matches, ls runs without arguments
# ls output
```
If you don't want that to happen, you can use another command that only shows the matches, like `print -l`.

body:
Actually this has nothing to do with git-completion, and it's a bug in zsh itself.

Try this:

```zsh
setopt ksh_zero_subscript
typeset -A args
(( $+args[foo] )) && echo set
```

`ksh_zero_subscript` shouldn't be changing the behavior of the code, but it does.

I've [reported the bug](https://www.zsh.org/mla/workers/2022/msg00756.html), in the meantime you can just disable `ksh_zero_subscript` inside the script as a workaround.

author: rwmitchell

body:
Yeah I have been testing this out and researching why it behaves this way, and IMO it is doing what is only logical with the key combinations pressed.

When you have selected one of the options of the menu, it is no longer logical to write a wildcard for the same argument because the full filename is already typed. IMO zsh is doing the correct thing here, taking into account that directories don't get the space.

If you disagree with this you can ask around on the zsh mailing list: https://zsh.org/mla/.
author: mcornella

body:
> Yeah I have been testing this out and researching why it behaves this way, and IMO it is doing what is only logical with the key combinations pressed.
> 
> When you have selected one of the options of the menu, it is no longer logical to write a wildcard for the same argument because the full filename is already typed. IMO zsh is doing the correct thing here, taking into account that directories don't get the space.
> 
> If you disagree with this you can ask around on the zsh mailing list: https://zsh.org/mla/.

Then I think it would be better to have the appending space included as a part of the tab completion itself. So while tabbing through the list of files and directories, the space at the end would be appended (and not when the user is typing the asterisk). This way things would be visually coherent. What’s happening now is that there a disconnect between what’s the programmer is expecting and what omz is doing.

body:
workaround from **michelebologna** theme

```zsh
# Status:
# - was there an error
# - am I root
# - are there background jobs?
prompt_status() {
  local symbols
  local jobs_bg="%{%F{cyan}%}⚙"

  symbols=()
  [[ $RETVAL -ne 0 ]] && symbols+="%{%F{red}%}✘"
  [[ $UID -eq 0 ]] && symbols+="%{%F{yellow}%}⚡"
  symbols+="%1(j. $jobs_bg.)"

  [[ -n "$symbols" ]] && prompt_segment black default "$symbols"
}
```

Ah no, @rwmitchell: my search was _not_ case insensitive. Here're the results with your grep command:

```
grep -i "extended.*glob" ~/omz-debug.log
+compinit:71> setopt extendedglob
+compinit:131> _comp_options=( bareglobqual extendedglob glob multibyte multifuncdef nullglob rcexpandparam unset NO_allexport NO_aliases NO_cshnullglob NO_cshjunkiequotes NO_errexit NO_errreturn NO_globassign NO_globsubst NO_histsubstpattern NO_ignorebraces NO_ignoreclosebraces NO_kshglob NO_ksharrays NO_kshtypeset NO_markdirs NO_octalzeroes NO_posixbuiltins NO_posixidentifiers NO_shwordsplit NO_shglob NO_warnnestedvar NO_warncreateglobal )
+compaudit:14> setopt extendedglob
+compdef:5> setopt extendedglob
+compdef:5> setopt extendedglob
  setopt extended_glob
+compdef:5> setopt extendedglob
+compdef:5> setopt extendedglob
unsetopt EXTENDED_GLOB
+/Users/traviscarden/.zshrc:137> unsetopt EXTENDED_GLOB
+omz_termsupport_preexec:4> setopt extended_glob
```

If I'm reading that right, `omz_termsupport_preexec` looks like the culprit. But I have no idea what it is. I can't find such a string or a filename anywhere. I'm sure it means more to you.
author: TravisCarden

body:
`omz_termsupport_preexec` is the function that runs the auto-title functionality before a command is run (usually it sets the running command as the title).

It is run here:
https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/a7d910c3a61d8599f748a8ddae59ecdd9424022a/lib/termsupport.zsh#L58-L59

However, as you see it is preceded by `emulate -L zsh`, which sets [`setopt localoptions`](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Options.html#index-LOCALOPTIONS).

With this option, the `setopt` commands inside the function don't affect the environment outside the function.

```sh
unsetopt extendedglob

# this is an anonymous function
() {
  setopt | grep -q extendedglob || echo before: no
  emulate -L zsh
  setopt extendedglob
  setopt | grep -q extendedglob && echo function: yes
}

setopt | grep -q extendedglob || echo after: no
```

This will say

```
before: no
function: yes
after: no
```

The same is true with the construct `setopt localoptions <some-other-option>` BTW, it's not necessary to call `emulate -L zsh` to get this behavior.

TL;DR this means that this is not the culprit, and there must be something else after this command that we are not seeing.

Perhaps the issue is that `extendedglob` has not been enabled at the point where you ran exit when capturing the trace. Make sure that you only do that after `setopt | grep extendedglob` gives you a result.

If you are unable to see the culprit, and have verified point 2, you can email the trace file to logs@ohmyz.sh: this email address will relay the email to me and @carlosala for us to review. _We are working on setting up a drop bucket with traceability for user logs that is secure and automated, but until then the email address is the best choice if you want security from prying eyes._

Let me know whichever you find or whether it's not relevant anymore. Thanks!

author: gheritarish

body:
This is a regression in zsh 5.9: https://www.zsh.org/mla/workers/2022/msg00652.html. A workaround for now is to set `CASE_SENSITIVE=true` and avoid being in a situation where the only possible suggestions start with adjacent letters.

For example, this will trigger the error:
```console
$ ls
hello 		iris
$ cd [TAB]
$ cd I
```
while this doesn't
```console
$ ls
hello		iris		list
$ cd [TAB]
hello/		iris/		list/
```

This same thing happens on files, it doesn't need to be directories:
```console
$ touch hi.txt iris.txt
$ cat [TAB]
$ cat I
```

I will close it as not our fault, but update the issue when there are news about the patch.
author: mcornella

body:
There is a different option to fixing this without deactivating case insensitive search altogether:
change `m:{a-zA-Z}={A-Za-z}` to `m:{[:lower:][:upper:]}={[:upper:][:lower:]}`. I created a PR for this (#11526).

At least in my case, the incorrect completions are coming from:

```local/share/zsh/5.8.1.1-dev/functions/_compadd```

author: rwmitchell

body:
OMZ does not use `compadd` to change the z completion. It doesn't do anything extra apart from calling `compinit`. When that is done in a non-OMZ shell with zsh 5.9, the bug appears again. 

It looks like there's an incompatibility with how z uses compctl instead of compsys and zsh 5.9. I'm still investigating.

body:
That's not possible with an alias, it would have to be a function. One alternative that I use is a global alias (a global shorthand) that can be quickly added to a command without having to type the whole `| tail`:
```sh
alias -g :T='| command tail'
```
If we add the `| tail`, we would also need to add the `| head`, so I'm not sure this is so good to have. Maybe you could use [`fzf`](https://github.com/junegunn/fzf) instead of the `history` plugin and do `history | fzf`?

body: I just submitted a fix for this. It appears GitHub has just recently added the ability to use $LaTeX$ in markdown (as demonstrated with the name and here: $1+2=3$ -> $1+2=3$). To resolve, you can just add span tags around the dollar signs in the README file: `$1+2=3$`. Although #11018 fixes the git README, I suspect there are other READMEs scattered about that are going to have the same issue.

author: schuebel

body:
Encountered the same behavior in my ZSH environment running inside WSL2 as well. The source of the issue turned out to be the used oc CLI version. I was running v3.11.0 which outputs a completely different completion instruction set when calling `oc completion zsh` compared to e.g. v4.2.0, missing the referenced `_oc` function needed for `compdef _oc oc` to work. Updating my oc CLI fixed it.
If you have to stay on a version that does not provide the `_oc` function in its completion instruction set, you can remove https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/4c82a2eedf0c43d47601ffa8b0303ed1326fab8f/plugins/oc/oc.plugin.zsh#L7 and the auto completion will work again.

body:
Do you remember how you initially installed OMZ?  There really should be .git subdirectory.

How long have you been using it?  Less than a month?  (about every 2 weeks tries to update). That would account for my why you are suddenly getting the error.

The safest way to get it back is to:

```
cd ~
mv .oh-my-zsh .oh-my-zsh-HOLD
git clone https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git
mv .oh-my-zsh-HOLD/custom .oh-my-zsh
```

That will allow you to refer back to the backup copy if you discover something unexpected has changed.
If you don't have a custom subdirectory and haven't made any changes to OMZ, you could simplify that with:

```
cd ~
rm -rf .oh-my-zsh
git clone https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh.git
```

body:
> This [line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/249c708ed3a4a7a63d16a6e911a46b6fb9623cbd/plugins/ssh-agent/ssh-agent.plugin.zsh#L2) should probably use `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`.

I'm unsure whether this is really a good idea.

Any SSH-related files are generally kept in `~/.ssh/` because they can contain sensitive data, and the `ssh` tools check the permissions on that directory and refuse to run if they're too lax. That protection seems worthwhile to leverage.

The environment scripts could be stored in a (mode `0700`) _subdirectory_ of `~/.ssh/`, to keep them from polluting the toplevel dir. But I'm not sure the **common** temp/cache areas are really a wise place to store them.

author: rwmitchell

body:
I was disappointed to find that multiple ZSH_CUSTOM directories aren't a thing. I got around the restriction by making a `other_plugin_folders` array. Then, the directories are symlinked into `$ZSH/custom/plugins` if their basenames do not already exist there. Place this before sourcing `$ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh`

#### .zshrc
```sh
# Until oh-my-zsh allows multiple ZSH_CUSTOM folders, here's a workaround
other_plugins_folders=("/usr/share/zsh/plugins" "$HOME/Projects/zsh-plugins" "/Foo/Bar/plugins")

for dir in "${other_plugins_folders[@]}"; do
  if [[ -d "$dir" ]]; then
    for sdir in "$dir"/*; do
      if [[ -d "$sdir" ]] && [[ ! -e "$ZSH/custom/plugins/$(basename "$sdir")" ]]; then
        ln -s "$sdir" "$ZSH/custom/plugins"
      fi
    done
  fi
done
```

* Do note that this snippet is not a true multiple `$ZSH_CUSTOM` replacement, its scope is just the `$ZSH/custom/plugins` folder as that is all I needed.
author: elanora96

body:
I published a project that addresses this issue: https://github.com/mattmc3/omz-plus

TLDR - in your .zshrc add the following:

```zsh
# Use either ZSH_CUSTOM for a single custom directory...
# ZSH_CUSTOM=/path/to/new-custom-folder
# ...OR... use OMZ PLUS! $zsh_custom variable for multiple
zsh_custom=(
    ${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zsh_custom
    ${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zsh_custom_work
)

##### START OMZ PLUS! ##################################################################
export OMZ_PLUS=${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.omz-plus
[ -d "$OMZ_PLUS" ] || git clone https://github.com/mattmc3/omz-plus $OMZ_PLUS
source $OMZ_PLUS/omz-plus.sh
##### END OMZ PLUS! ####################################################################

# Source oh-my-zsh.sh immediately after omz-plus.sh
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

author: WGroleau

body:
The issue here is the quoting of the `~` character, it must go outside quotes for the shell to properly expand it to the home directory. When you put it within quotes, the path created has a literal tilde, not the home directory. Then, when doing something such as `ls ~/3TB-files` you will not find it, as the shell expands the tilde to the home directory.

This is not OMZ-related, but rather normal behavior of shells. See https://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/146671/does-always-equal-home/146697#146697.
author: mcornella

body:
Apart from the duplicate alias for `git pull --rebase`, also notice that the choice for all aliases of command starting with `git pull --rebase` does not follow the standard convention for choosing the initial letters from each word. I propose we change the alias of all such commands to the more appropriate derivatives of `gpr`.

| BEFORE        | AFTER         |
| ------------- | ------------- |
| gpr='git pull --rebase' | gpr='git pull --rebase' |
| gup='git pull --rebase' | **deleted** |
| gupv='git pull --rebase -v' | gprv='git pull --rebase -v' |
| gupa='git pull --rebase --autostash' | gpra='git pull --rebase --autostash' |
| gupav='git pull --rebase --autostash -v' | gprav='git pull --rebase --autostash -v' |
| gupom='git pull --rebase origin $(git_main_branch)' | gprom='git pull --rebase origin $(git_main_branch)' |
| gupomi='git pull --rebase=interactive origin $(git_main_branch)' | gpromi='git pull --rebase=interactive origin $(git_main_branch)' |

I have opened a resolving PR #11224, for the same. Please let me know your thoughts (review and approval are appreciated too).

author: tjarbo

body:
It isn't going to happen.  It has been previously suggested, but the maintainers are not going to make it.  It would admit to not being careful during previous decisions on what to accept and might disrupt someone's workflow.

This really points to why only a few very basic aliases (if any) should be included in OMZ.  Aliases are mnemonics that only make sense to the fevered mind creating them.  Put them in Readme files to give people ideas and give examples of potentially better ways to do things, but don't actually enable them.  Everyone has different workflows and different things the do frequently enough to warrant using an alias.  The more aliases, the more likely a mistype could run something you don't want and some of the git aliases are the worse - any alias using --force is an example.

Who should ever think 'ggf' is short for 'git push --force'. Completely insane.

and here is my `oh-my-zsh.sh` file:
```
# Protect against non-zsh execution of Oh My Zsh (use POSIX syntax here)
[ -n "$ZSH_VERSION" ] || {
  # ANSI formatting function (\033[<code>m)
  # 0: reset, 1: bold, 4: underline, 22: no bold, 24: no underline, 31: red, 33: yellow
  omz_f() {
    [ $# -gt 0 ] || return
    IFS=";" printf "\033[%sm" $*
  }
  # If stdout is not a terminal ignore all formatting
  [ -t 1 ] || omz_f() { :; }

  omz_ptree() {
    # Get process tree of the current process
    pid=$$; pids="$pid"
    while [ ${pid-0} -ne 1 ] && ppid=$(ps -e -o pid,ppid | awk "\$1 == $pid { print \$2 }"); do
      pids="$pids $pid"; pid=$ppid
    done

    # Show process tree
    case "$(uname)" in
    Linux) ps -o ppid,pid,command -f -p $pids 2>/dev/null ;;
    Darwin|*) ps -o ppid,pid,command -p $pids 2>/dev/null ;;
    esac

    # If ps command failed, try Busybox ps
    [ $? -eq 0 ] || ps -o ppid,pid,comm | awk "NR == 1 || index(\"$pids\", \$2) != 0"
  }

  {
    shell=$(ps -o pid,comm | awk "\$1 == $$ { print \$2 }")
    printf "$(omz_f 1 31)Error:$(omz_f 22) Oh My Zsh can't be loaded from: $(omz_f 1)${shell}$(omz_f 22). "
    printf "You need to run $(omz_f 1)zsh$(omz_f 22) instead.$(omz_f 0)\n"
    printf "$(omz_f 33)Here's the process tree:$(omz_f 22)\n\n"
    omz_ptree
    printf "$(omz_f 0)\n"
  } >&2

  return 1
}

# If ZSH is not defined, use the current script's directory.
[[ -z "$ZSH" ]] && export ZSH="${${(%):-%x}:a:h}"

# Set ZSH_CACHE_DIR to the path where cache files should be created
# or else we will use the default cache/
if [[ -z "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR" ]]; then
  ZSH_CACHE_DIR="$ZSH/cache"
fi

# Make sure $ZSH_CACHE_DIR is writable, otherwise use a directory in $HOME
if [[ ! -w "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR" ]]; then
  ZSH_CACHE_DIR="${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/oh-my-zsh"
fi

# Create cache and completions dir and add to $fpath
mkdir -p "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions"
(( ${fpath[(Ie)"$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions"]} )) || fpath=("$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions" $fpath)

# Check for updates on initial load...
if [[ "$DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE" != true ]]; then
  source "$ZSH/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh"
fi

# Initializes Oh My Zsh

# add a function path
fpath=("$ZSH/functions" "$ZSH/completions" $fpath)

# Load all stock functions (from $fpath files) called below.
autoload -U compaudit compinit

# Set ZSH_CUSTOM to the path where your custom config files
# and plugins exists, or else we will use the default custom/
if [[ -z "$ZSH_CUSTOM" ]]; then
    ZSH_CUSTOM="$ZSH/custom"
fi

is_plugin() {
  local base_dir=$1
  local name=$2
  builtin test -f $base_dir/plugins/$name/$name.plugin.zsh \
    || builtin test -f $base_dir/plugins/$name/_$name
}

# Add all defined plugins to fpath. This must be done
# before running compinit.
for plugin ($plugins); do
  if is_plugin "$ZSH_CUSTOM" "$plugin"; then
    fpath=("$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/$plugin" $fpath)
  elif is_plugin "$ZSH" "$plugin"; then
    fpath=("$ZSH/plugins/$plugin" $fpath)
  else
    echo "[oh-my-zsh] plugin '$plugin' not found"
  fi
done

# Figure out the SHORT hostname
if [[ "$OSTYPE" = darwin* ]]; then
  # macOS's $HOST changes with dhcp, etc. Use ComputerName if possible.
  SHORT_HOST=$(scutil --get ComputerName 2>/dev/null) || SHORT_HOST="${HOST/.*/}"
else
  SHORT_HOST="${HOST/.*/}"
fi

# Save the location of the current completion dump file.
if [[ -z "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" ]]; then
  ZSH_COMPDUMP="${ZDOTDIR:-$HOME}/.zcompdump-${SHORT_HOST}-${ZSH_VERSION}"
fi

# Construct zcompdump OMZ metadata
zcompdump_revision="#omz revision: $(builtin cd -q "$ZSH"; git rev-parse HEAD 2>/dev/null)"
zcompdump_fpath="#omz fpath: $fpath"

# Delete the zcompdump file if OMZ zcompdump metadata changed
if ! command grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_revision" "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" 2>/dev/null \
   || ! command grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_fpath" "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" 2>/dev/null; then
  command rm -f "$ZSH_COMPDUMP"
  zcompdump_refresh=1
fi

if [[ "$ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX" != true ]]; then
  source "$ZSH/lib/compfix.zsh"
  # Load only from secure directories
  compinit -i -d "$ZSH_COMPDUMP"
  # If completion insecurities exist, warn the user
  handle_completion_insecurities &|
else
  # If the user wants it, load from all found directories
  compinit -u -d "$ZSH_COMPDUMP"
fi

# Append zcompdump metadata if missing
if (( $zcompdump_refresh )) \
  || ! command grep -q -Fx "$zcompdump_revision" "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" 2>/dev/null; then
  # Use `tee` in case the $ZSH_COMPDUMP filename is invalid, to silence the error
  # See https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/dd1a7269#commitcomment-39003489
  tee -a "$ZSH_COMPDUMP" &>/dev/null <<EOF

$zcompdump_revision
$zcompdump_fpath
EOF
fi
unset zcompdump_revision zcompdump_fpath zcompdump_refresh

# Load all of the config files in ~/oh-my-zsh that end in .zsh
# TIP: Add files you don't want in git to .gitignore
for config_file ("$ZSH"/lib/*.zsh); do
  custom_config_file="$ZSH_CUSTOM/lib/${config_file:t}"
  [[ -f "$custom_config_file" ]] && config_file="$custom_config_file"
  source "$config_file"
done
unset custom_config_file

# Load all of the plugins that were defined in ~/.zshrc
for plugin ($plugins); do
  if [[ -f "$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/$plugin/$plugin.plugin.zsh" ]]; then
    source "$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/$plugin/$plugin.plugin.zsh"
  elif [[ -f "$ZSH/plugins/$plugin/$plugin.plugin.zsh" ]]; then
    source "$ZSH/plugins/$plugin/$plugin.plugin.zsh"
  fi
done
unset plugin

# Load all of your custom configurations from custom/
for config_file ("$ZSH_CUSTOM"/*.zsh(N)); do
  source "$config_file"
done
unset config_file

# Load the theme
is_theme() {
  local base_dir=$1
  local name=$2
  builtin test -f $base_dir/$name.zsh-theme
}

if [[ -n "$ZSH_THEME" ]]; then
  if is_theme "$ZSH_CUSTOM" "$ZSH_THEME"; then
    source "$ZSH_CUSTOM/$ZSH_THEME.zsh-theme"
  elif is_theme "$ZSH_CUSTOM/themes" "$ZSH_THEME"; then
    source "$ZSH_CUSTOM/themes/$ZSH_THEME.zsh-theme"
  elif is_theme "$ZSH/themes" "$ZSH_THEME"; then
    source "$ZSH/themes/$ZSH_THEME.zsh-theme"
  else
    echo "[oh-my-zsh] theme '$ZSH_THEME' not found"
  fi
fi
```

author: MagnasiePro

body:
I ran across this issue as well.

**TLDR;** add `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true` to the top of your .zshrc to fix this on MacOS with zsh installed via homebrew.

When running /bin/zsh, it works fine because the fpath is using the system locations. But, /opt/homebrew/bin/zsh fails with `compinit:480: compdump: function definition file not found`. Looking at the `fpath` for homebrew's zsh, it appeared that its fpath locations weren't present. But, if you try adding them, they get reset:

```zsh
export fpath=(
   /opt/homebrew/Cellar/zsh/5.9/share/zsh/functions
   /opt/homebrew/share/zsh/site-functions
   $fpath
)
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
# oops! fpath is now reset!? What?!
```

Digging deeper, the issue is with `compaudit` in Oh My Zsh's lib/compfix.zsh file. You can disable this functionality by adding `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true` to the top of your .zshrc. The issue is likely due to directory permissions, which can be dealt with by running `brew doctor`, but if you are on a multi-user system like a family computer, [that can be problematic](https://github.com/orgs/Homebrew/discussions/66).

body: Beware - the solutions that point you to save off your `fpath` and then reset it after sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh` are just masking the problem, and creating another. You'll get your functions back by resetting your `fpath`, but you still lost all your non-OMZ completions because `compinit` got called without a proper `fpath`. Seriously, double check that your `fpath` doesn't have anything you don't trust, and then just add `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true`.

> **TLDR;** add `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true` to the top of your .zshrc to fix this on MacOS with zsh installed via homebrew.
> 
> When running /bin/zsh, it works fine because the fpath is using the system locations. But, /opt/homebrew/bin/zsh fails with `compinit:480: compdump: function definition file not found`. Looking at the `fpath` for homebrew's zsh, it appeared that its fpath locations weren't present. But, if you try adding them, they get reset:
> 
> ```shell
> export fpath=(
>    /opt/homebrew/Cellar/zsh/5.9/share/zsh/functions
>    /opt/homebrew/share/zsh/site-functions
>    $fpath
> )
> source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
> # oops! fpath is now reset!? What?!
> ```
> 
> Digging deeper, the issue is with `compaudit` in Oh My Zsh's lib/compfix.zsh file. You can disable this functionality by adding `ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true` to the top of your .zshrc. The issue is likely due to directory permissions, which can be dealt with by running `brew doctor`, but if you are on a multi-user system like a family computer, [that can be problematic](https://github.com/orgs/Homebrew/discussions/66).


adding ZSH_DISABLE_COMPFIX=true at the top of my .zshrc file fixed it for me and im using kali linux. 
My issue started after messing with sudo chmod -R g+rwx /usr/bin and a bunch of other directories, dont ask me why i did that lol, im sure i had a reason.

body:
You can specify a different remote name for Oh My Zsh with the command below:
```zsh
cd $ZSH
git config --local oh-my-zsh.remote up
```
This variable is automatically set when the installer installs OMZ, but it names the remote `origin` directly. Did you perhaps clone the repository manually?

In any case, this is a bug in the installer, which does not use `git clone` but instead does it manually, so it should be aware of `clone.defaultRemoteName`.

author: StevenTang1998

body:
Commands that don't have a completion function get the generic one, which completes paths to the command.

This only works if you pass in the path in a single argument, if you use `--path=` then zsh doesn't know what to complete here and you won't get any suggestions.

Try either of these:
```
python test.py --path aaa[TAB]
python test.py aaa[TAB]
```

body:
If I understand correctly, the `git` version you have (Git for Windows) fails when doing a `git clone`, and so you'd like the `grep` check to also look for the `windows` string in the `git --version` output, as this would indicate the error cause properly.

This is a simple fix. However, note that you'll still have to install a `git` version in cygwin that takes precedence over the Git for Windows one.

Thanks for reporting!

author: haipengpan

body:
Hi, we stumbled upon this during the setup of new machines, as it has worked for us in the past just fine and is only an issue on new installations. Currently the fix applied prevents us from installing oh-my-zsh on Cygwin with only the Windows Git client installed.
Since Git version 2.32.0 using only the windows git client without an additional installation of the cygwin git client works just fine, as the last annoying issue (converting cygwin paths to windows paths) was addressed in the windows git client itself.
We are working with this configuration since the end of 2021 without any git related issues, neither in cygwin nor in windows itself.
This fix now caugth my attention, as we are swapping out our workstations with new ones and the first of my co-workers could not install oh-my-zsh as we were used to.

This 'fix' prevents us from using the windows git client. But I can't see the reason why it was implemented in the first place.
Besides the 'git clone failed' there is no further information what exactly did not work in the first place.
Maybe the windows git client and the cygwin git client were installed in parallel?

@mcornella or @aaftio can you explain why this change was required? I would like to prevent managing two competing git installations on our systems.

I would be happy to help find a solution which supports both usecases - git windows client only and git windows client & cygwin client installed in parallel.

body: `brew shellenv` is only called if `$HOMEBREW_PREFIX` is not defined. That variable is used in several places and we need to have it defined. If you define that properly then is not going to be called. Thanks for your contribution!
author: carlosala

body:
But why not `$(brew --prefix)` or `${HOMEBREW_PREFIX:-$(brew --prefix)}`. I can only see one place that uses `HOMEBREW_PREFIX` in oh-my-zsh, the nvm plugin, and its already does the latter to fall back. 

The problem is that change has now messed up my `PATH` with duplicate and out of order entries. The brew installation instructions and post install step for MacOS Intel do **not** tell you to set  `HOMEBREW_PREFIX` or call `brew shellenv`. So a normal user could not easily know that they need to manually set just `HOMEBREW_PREFIX` prior to loading the plugin, to by-pass their `PATH` getting duplicate entries.

body:
Hey @leyuskckiran1510 and @robbyrussell.

Thanks for looking into this! For what it's worth, I just tested it and can confirm that this is still an ongoing issue.

**To reiterate, here's how I would expect this plugin to work:**
* In either history mode (global or local), all commands would be saved to the global history and all directory-specific commands would be saved to the history for the current directory.
* When in local history mode, you would only see the history of commands run in the current directory (from new to old).
* When in global mode, you would see the history of all commands (from new to old), regardless of where they were run.

The current behavior is pretty screwy and is definitely not consistent with the above, though it's hard to pinpoint exactly where things are going wrong. Happy to do more troubleshooting if helpful though.

author: rwmitchell

body:
@JediMaster25 you can override the `d` function in your zshrc file to call the zoxide command to list recent directories:

```sh
alias d='zoxide query -ls'
```
or
```sh
function d { zoxide query -ls }
```

---

That said, the purpose of `d` is to know which number to pick when using the number aliases though. For example, with this

```console
$ d
0	~/code
1	~/code/dotfiles
2	~/code/dotfiles/ohmyzsh
3	~
```

I know that running `2` I will jump to the `~/code/dotfiles/ohmyzsh` folder. Because of that, this is out of scope of the `d` function and we won't be implementing the change.
author: mcornella

body: That’s not how it works. Write a new d function that uses zoxide instead of dirs.
author: rwmitchell

body:
```sh
function d () {
  if [[ -n $1 ]]; then
    dirs "$@"
  else
    zoxide query -ls
  fi
}
```

author: rilysh

body:
@rilysh is spot on. To install zsh there are two paths that I'd suggest as Unraid doesn't come with a package manager.

Option 1. 
Follow the steps provided [here](https://forums.unraid.net/topic/35866-unraid-6-nerdpack-cli-tools-iftop-iotop-screen-kbd-etc/?do=findComment&comment=1159381) which state:
```
To replicate the functionality (unsupported):
- Go to https://slackware.pkgs.org/15.0/slackware-x86_64/ which 
lists packages for Slackware 15 Unraid is based on
Search for the packages you want
- Download the txz files for them, and put them on the flash drive 
in /extra (/boot/extra on a running system), that will cause them to 
auto-install on boot (create the folder if there isn't one)
- To be able to use them without a reboot use unraid CLI to navigate 
where you put the packages and run installpkg <filename>
```
Option 2. 
Slightly more advanced option would be to manually install @ich777's un-get plugin available [here](https://github.com/ich777/un-get). This is more advanced but if you install packages regularly, will make things a lot easier. 

Note that while installing a package such as zsh is unlikely to cause any problems, it's not supported by Lime, and there are a lot of plugins available via slackware that will irreparably damage your Unraid installation: Unraid isn't a standard Linux distro, and relies on a lot of proprietary customisation. Please ensure you back up your flash drive first, and be careful what you install.

Best to get support for this issue over on the [Unraid forums](https://forums.unraid.net/) though.

I don't think it's exactly a downside – the readme will be more self-documenting when it includes the verbose flags. author: marafa-sugarcrm

body:
If it does happen, your system is screwed in ways that need to be investigated and not just glossed over.

Your script is great to run on initial setup and you should continue to use it as such, but it shouldn't need to be run beyond that.  OMZ automatically checks and updates itself every 2 weeks, which for the of changes that actually happen is way plenty often.

By waiting two weeks to update, you also increase the chances that a non-optimal update will get corrected before the automatic refresh.

That's my take.  You could also just go to your .oh-my-zsh directly and run ```git pull``` from there (after it has been installed) to avoid using ```omz update``` as you don't really care about tracking when to update (which is mostly what that does, nor do you want to auto restart zsh to pick up the changes, if there any.  You'll need to do a bit more digging in tools/upgrade.sh if you care about seeing the changes.
author: rwmitchell

body:
`omz update` is not supposed to be used in scripts, for that you can call the upgrade script directly:

```sh
ZSH="$ZSH" zsh -f "$ZSH/tools/upgrade.sh"
```

`omz update` has an undocumented flag `--unattended` but it is not supposed to be used as `omz update` does other stuff for the user that is not expected for non-interactive runs.

With the command above you have the extra optimization of not having to call `zsh` and source the `.zshrc` file.

> I think it should be installed as dependency by default, if it is not installed

OMZ does not install dependencies as that would require sudo access and it wouldn't be expected by the user either. As `column` is just a text formatting tool we can remove the dependency altogether or just display a long list if it's not installed.

> column has been a standard Unix cmd for a very long time.

There may be systems were it is not installed it they're not normally used interactively. For example I have confirmed that the Docker image for Ubuntu does not have it, and it could be the same for Ubuntu Server edition.

In any case, this is an easy fix, no need to debate it.

body:
OMZ sets the `setopt autopushd` which means all directories visited are pushed to the stack. This has no impact if you're not using the stack at all, but if you want to turn this off you can add
```
unsetopt autopushd
```
to your `.zshrc` file, after OMZ is sourced.

Not considering this a bug.

body:
@carlosala Yes, here is my `.zshrc` and new `omz-debug.log`.

omz-debug.log __REDACTED__

```sh
# Oh My Zsh
export ZSH=$HOME/.oh-my-zsh # Path to oh-my-zsh installation.
export ZSH_COMPDUMP=$ZSH/cache/.zcompdump-$HOST # Store zsh's completion cache inside oh-my-zsh cache folder

# Oh My Zsh config variables
ZSH_THEME=half-life
ZSH_CUSTOM=~/.config/oh-my-zsh-custom # my oh-my-zsh customization folder. contains aliases, functions, plugins, etc.

# Oh My Zsh plugins
plugins=( z )

# Init Oh My Zsh after its config variables are set
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# User Configuration
export EDITOR=vim # Default editor
export BAT_THEME=gruvbox-dark # Set bat theme

# init nvm
export NVM_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
[ -s "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM_DIR/nvm.sh"  # This loads nvm
[ -s "$NVM_DIR/bash_completion" ] && \. "$NVM_DIR/bash_completion"  # This loads nvm bash_completion

# init iTerm2 shell integration
test -e "${HOME}/.iterm2_shell_integration.zsh" && source "${HOME}/.iterm2_shell_integration.zsh"
```
author: dvakatsiienko

body:
You have a custom `aliases.zsh` file that deletes all aliases:
```
alias ${ZSHZ_CMD:-${_Z_CMD:-z}}='zshz 2>&1'
+/Users/furthur/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/z/z.plugin.zsh:845> alias 'z=zshz 2>&1'
...
# Delete all built-in aliases: system and oh-my-zsh
unalias -m '*'
+/Users/furthur/.config/oh-my-zsh-custom/aliases.zsh:2> unalias -m '*'
```

Without aliases, there is no alias named `z`, which is how the plugin works.

You can set `alias 'z=zshz 2>&1'` in the `oh-my-zsh-custom/aliases.zsh` file if you still want to keep the `unalias -m '*'` line.

body:
I've reproduced this without rc files.

1. Type `zsh -f`. This will start zsh without startup files.
2. Type `reset` to ensure clean environment.
3. Type `cat` and press <kbd>ENTER</kbd>.
4. Type `asdf`.
5. Press <kbd>TAB</kbd>.
6. Press and hold <kbd>Backspace</kbd>
7. Observe that "asd" cannot be deleted.

This isn't an issue with Oh My Zsh or with terminal titles.

This can be reproduce in any program that reads from a terminal in cooked (not raw) mode. For example, `cat`, `dash`, or the plain `read` builtin.

body:
The full line to enable lazy mode is, see the extra `yes`. 
```
zstyle :omz:plugins:ssh-agent lazy yes
```


Note that you also need to configure SSH to `AddKeysToAgent`, as specified in https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/ssh-agent/README.md#lazy, or SSH keys won't be loaded when running `ssh`.

body:
The equal should be without spaces
```sh
ZSH_THEME=agnoster 
```

In zsh and shell languages, variable assignments are done without spaces.

For future reference, as it is written now, the shell tries to run a command named `agnoster` with an environment variable named `ZSH_THEME` set to `<empty string>`.

body:
I ran into this issue, actually, because this thing broke the `debian` plugin.  That plugin is a handy user affordance to the operating system.  I still have no idea what the `aliases` plugin is, nor why it is considered "global", but I've been using the `debian` plugin since whenever I discovered omz to begin with.

`acs` likewise became undefined for me, as of the breakage introduced as a result of the new plugin's collision.

The end user fix is fairly trivial other than needing to be applied on every debian-derived machine where omz is in use everywhere.  It'd make more sense to fix this upstream rather than in thousands or hundreds of thousands of rc files.
author: majick

body:
> I would say `acs` function should be priority as it's the most important thing in the aliases plugin.

> `acs` is the key functionality of `aliases` plugin, and for `debian` and `ubuntu` is just one more.

With respect, this seems like an odd primary consideration. It makes sense if the primary goal is balancing the feelings of two people, but we're not talking about the temporary feelings of two people. We're talking about something that affects whole groups of users, and will continue to do so indefinitely in some ways. The focus should be on maximizing overall utility for them, which requires weighing a lot of factors, almost all of which lean toward the `ubuntu` and `debian` plugins in this case.

I suggest that the first question that should've been asked in this situation is: how do `aliases`'s available alternatives compare to those of `debian` and `ubuntu`? `alcs` would be just as evocative of `aliases`'s behavior (listing defined aliases) as `acs` is. `alist` would be even more! (Even `aliases` seems to be compatible with at least some uses of the zsh builtin.) Meanwhile, `acs` follows a clear pattern within the `debian` and `ubuntu` plugins that users use to easily remember it, while there is no pattern to break within the `aliases` plugin precisely _because_ it's all that that plugin consists of.

> It needs to be fixed in both `ubuntu` and `debian` plugins!

While I think it's less important than the above question of alternatives, the fact that two much older, more established plugins have to be altered to accommodate one younger one also weighs against the action taken. With `debian` and `ubuntu` having used the `acs` name four times as long, it's likely to be more ingrained in their users' minds.

> The aliases plugin broke the ubuntu plugin. Regardless of your knowledge of what ubuntu is, the aliases plugin should not have been allowed to be created with a conflicting alias.

I strongly agree given that it would've been of basically no detriment to `aliases` to use a different function name from the start.

It's not too late to correct it. Countless debian and ubuntu users will appreciate not having to remember which alias breaks the pattern with an 'e' stuck on the end.
author: tjnycum

body:
> I suggest that the first question that should've been asked in this situation is: how do `aliases`'s available alternatives compare to those of `debian` and `ubuntu`?

I would go so far as to argue that the first question is "what is `aliases` and why did it just leave completely undefined existing decade-old functionality for installing packages on one of the largest OSes on the planet?"

Breaking changes happen, but breaking the underlying infrastructure of most of the deployed planet for... I don't even know what this thing is?  Poking at the plugin It looks like it lists aliases in prettyprint, for which a better mnemonic would have been `als` in the first place.  The plugin is called `aliases` ("als") and it lists things ("als").

It's not even clear what that plugin's mnemonic is supposed to mean.

Meanwhile basically every person with a shell prompt is going to want to search apt.
author: majick

body: Submitted #11799 against this.  Please do not bog me down in debate about it.  Debian is large.  Your python script is not.  If you need a debate, please have it among yourselves.
author: majick

body:
"Meanwhile basically every person with a LINUX shell prompt is going to want to search apt."

fixed that for you.

This is really just a symptom of a larger issue and that is the user base for OMZ, supporting a wide variety of OSes and tools, has grown too big and ultimately clashes with individual workflows.  Once something has made it into OMZ, the maintainers are reluctant to make any change that could disrupt someone's previous usage, even if that change is good.

I'm only getting this discussion because of making a snarky comment earlier, but the truth is, I haven't found anything recently new in OMZ terribly useful.  As a result, I've forked my own clone  where I can fix things for my usage instead of trying to come up with work-arounds.

My recommendation is to do the same, sharing your local repo with friends/co-workers with the same usage goals.

Or you can continue trying to get your changes forced onto everyone else.  I don't care anymore.
author: rwmitchell

body:
As far as I can see, it seems that this change was important to several people, I've just reverted the changes of that commit, and renamed `acs` function from `aliases` plugin to `als`.
Thanks to everyone that reported this!

Even though, I'd like to ask to be a bit more polite with this kind of discussions, respecting both the original contributor and us that merged it.
`omz` is a huge project, with a lot of plugins, running in a lot of systems... We try our best to keep up with everything in our spare time, always trying to do the less breaking changes possible but keeping the evolution of the project and fixing the broken things. Being as respectful as possible will make this community even more open and nicer.
I'm sure everyone will understand it :)

body:
I managed to fix the issue by regenerating the locale according to the instructions here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45995530/manpath-cant-set-the-locale-make-sure-lc-and-lang-are-correct/46946414#46946414

My `.zshrc` already has the variables for `LANG` set, but somehow that's not working.

```bash
export LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8
export LANG=en_US.UTF-8
```

After doing the commands below again, cursor is now aligned properly.

```bash
sudo locale-gen "en_US.UTF-8"
sudo dpkg-reconfigure locales
```

The `last-working-dir` plugin uses `pwd` to track the current working directory, is it possible that you have it aliased to something?

Run `which -a pwd` to know. Also, it's only useful when starting a new shell, as it will change to the last directory you were in the last session. 

If you run `lwd` in the middle of a shell session it will just send you to the current directory.

author: andrej-urvantsev

body:
It looks like stdout is not initialized as a tty, sample from the beginning of the init process:
```shell
# - is not run from a tty
# - git is unavailable on the system.
if [[ "$update_mode" = disabled ]] \
   || [[ ! -w "$ZSH" || ! -O "$ZSH" ]] \
   || [[ ! -t 1 ]] \
   || ! command git --version 2>&1 >/dev/null; then
  unset update_mode
  return
fi
+/home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:26> [[ prompt = disabled ]]
+/home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:27> [[ ! -w /home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh || ! -O /home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh ]]
+/home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:28> [[ ! -t 1 ]]
+/home/andrej/.oh-my-zsh/tools/check_for_upgrade.sh:30> unset update_mode
```

The `[ -t 1 ]` check fails. This is why you get the error
```
Cannot get terminal settings: Inappropriate ioctl for device (25)
```

- Use default mc version in 22.04 (in my case it was 4.8.27 as well)
- Temporarily back up .zshrc file some place
- Replace the .zshrc file with nothing (opening mc shell should work)
- Add `sleep 2` in the .zshrc file (opening mc shell should not work)

It's great to know that recent mc versions work well though, this confirms that OMZ is not at fault. Unless someone confirms otherwise, we'll be closing the issue in a few days.

body:
Here's a summary of what we found:

It appears this issue is related https://github.com/MidnightCommander/mc/commit/e89713a3d6a2edd4813958608ce461540c4f715e

To confirm, you can reproduce it with the following steps:
- Back up you `.zshrc` file somewhere, and make it a blank file with no contents. Issue should be solved
- Then, add `sleep 2` in the `.zshrc`. This should slow down the shell startup enough to make mc skip it.

This is not reproducible on 4.8.30, which should contain the fixes.

TL;DR: update `mc` to v4.8.30 or newer. I'm going to close this unless someone has a reproducible setup that makes 4.8.30 fail.

body:
One problem I see is that `colorize_less` needs to forward `ZSH_COLORIZE_CHROMA_FORMATTER` (added by #8824) when invoking `colorize_cat`. That said, the issue description has `ZSH_COLORIZE_CHROMA_FORMATTER` commented out so perhaps there's something else going on too.
At least locally on my machine (macOS, Terminal.app), I do use `ZSH_COLORIZE_CHROMA_FORMATTER=terminal256` and my `cless` output is not correct unless I fix it to forward `ZSH_COLORIZE_CHROMA_FORMATTER`.
author: monoxygen

body:
I'm not able to reproduce this at all on iTerm2, macOS and chroma. I know this is very old, but if you are still having this issue, having the trace of the `colorize_less` function would show exactly where the issue is. You can enable a trace with `functions -t colorize_less`, and then running `cless <file>`. After exiting less, you'll see the trace.

With this said, I don't think the `chroma` and `pygmentize` tools are worth supporting anymore in this plugin. Installing [bat](https://github.com/sharkdp/bat) which is bundled in all major package managers, we can substitute both functions like so:

```sh
alias ccat='bat -P'
alias cless='bat'
```

I'm going to close this issue, mainly because it's old and I presume you're either not interested or not having the issue anymore. If that's not the case, please go ahead and reopen it with a trace of the function as shared above.

Thanks!

You can try to send an email to the zsh users mailing list (zsh.org/mla) to see if they can fix it.

I had the same problem, but i found a workaround.

I  uncommented this in the `.zshrc`:
```zsh
# Uncomment the following line if pasting URLs and other text is messed up.
DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS="true"
```

I hope, this is somehow helpful for finding the underlying bug. 
I can also send my log, if you need it for debugging.

I always thought this was a generalized bug, lots of folks in my company do experience it. That said, after disabling and re-enabling `DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS` I'm no longer able to replicate. I also did update omz this morning, so can't rule out it being patched.

Here's the definition of that flag based on omz-debug's output:
```batch
# *-magic is known buggy in some versions; disable if so
if [[ $DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS != true ]]; then
  for d in $fpath; do
    if [[ -e "$d/url-quote-magic" ]]; then
      if is-at-least 5.1; then
        autoload -Uz bracketed-paste-magic
        zle -N bracketed-paste bracketed-paste-magic
      fi
      autoload -Uz url-quote-magic
      zle -N self-insert url-quote-magic
    break
    fi
  done
fi
```

And here's my `omz-debug.log` generated with `zsh -xvlic exit &> ~/omz-debug.log`. This was run before tampering with `DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS` but after updating omz. Can't confirm if the issue was replicable or not at that time.

body:
Hi Hector, the `docker` plugin does not define any `docker-compose` aliases, and the `docker-compose` plugin also supports using `docker compose`: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/docker-compose/docker-compose.plugin.zsh.

If you have a `docker-compose` alias you should find whether you have it enabled in your .zshrc file or similar place.

author: carlosala

body:
> @mcornella it looks like it's this function that spawns the process https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/82ba981835aeeff0f64c9175be7d87d4451d9b3e/src/vs/platform/shell/node/shellEnv.ts#L102. I let this here and when I have time try to see what's going on.

Looks like they run interactive login shells in order to capture the user configuration: 
https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/82ba981835aeeff0f64c9175be7d87d4451d9b3e/src/vs/platform/shell/node/shellEnv.ts#L143

This is because usually the user configuration lives in .zshrc, instead of in .zshenv as is recommended (we even suggest setting the `$PATH` in .zshrc in our template, so we can see why it's so).

This means we can't rely on the interactive shell option, we could instead check for one of the env variables they set in the file, the best one being `VSCODE_RESOLVING_ENVIRONMENT` IMO.

author: carlosala

body:
@alexchandel we have two solutions that might solve your issue, one super general, and the second specific to vscode. Let's first try the generic one so we introduce as less code as possible in core directly related to some external app. We need your test for this (I was not able to reproduce using Linux!). So, the steps in this case would be:

- Edit your zshrc adding `zstyle ':omz:update' frequency 0` *before* sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`. This will make `omz` to ask for update every time you open a session (it'd be easier for testing, so we don't have to wait).
- Update once oh-my-zsh running `omz update`.
- Go to `cd $ZSH` and run `git reset --hard HEAD^`. This will get 1 commit back in the history of oh-my-zsh and, then, `omz` will detect an upgrade an propose it every time we open a terminal (answer `NO` all times to prevent updating before testing with vscode).
- Run `omz pr test 11400` to download the fix branch and use it, then run `exit` and answer `NO` to the question `omz` asks you, to keep yourself in that branch.
- Open vscode and see if it's fixed.

To come back to previous state, you should remove that line from `zshrc` and run `cd $ZSH; git switch master`, close and reopen the terminal, and `omz update; omz pr clean`.

Thanks for testing it, tell me if you have any doubt!
author: carlosala

body: @alexchandel friendly ping :)
author: carlosala

body: Closing, if this don't solves the issue feel free to test it and ping us 😊
author: carlosala

body:
@carlosala there are still issues with vscode. When starting a python debugging session, vscode opens a terminal with an interactive shell and immediately sends `source some_virtualenv/bin/activate` into it. If the update prompt `[oh-my-zsh] Would you like to update? [Y/n]` happens to trigger for that shell, it eats the "s" from "source".

I put this in my `.zshrc` before sourcing omz:
```
[[ $TERM_PROGRAM = vscode ]] && zstyle ':omz:update' mode disabled
```
author: camillol

body: It's indeed a good option. Even though it should only happen in certain specific cases. Normally, when you open a vscode terminal, you'd be interested in updating ohmyzsh. Using `mode auto` would be useful as well, since it cancels the upgrade if there's input before updating.
author: carlosala

body:
If you already have the ability to detect if there is pending input, then I think it would be a good idea to check for that before displaying the update prompt, and skipping it if there is input. In fact, it seems even more important to do that in the prompt case than in the auto-update case.

In general, I think it's reasonable to expect that if you start an interactive shell and send commands into it, the shell will receive them. Omz's update prompt breaks this assumption by "hijacking" the shell's prompt. The problem is not vscode-specific, either; a user opening a new terminal tab and immediately pasting commands into it could also run into this problem.

I think the best thing to do would be to change omz's default behavior to print a reminder ("updates available, run some command to update") without prompting for a response.

body:
For me, option-l/r/u/d have different key codes than the ones hard coded in the plugin. Here is a workaround, change the hard coded key codes: 
- Use `xxd -psg` to find option-l/r/u/d codes
- Change lines for iterm2, example: 
```zsh
  case "$TERM_PROGRAM" in
  ....
  iTerm.app) bindkey -M $keymap "<key-code>" dirhistory_zle_dirhistory_up ;;     # iTerm2 <-- change this line 
  esac
```
EDIT: Here is a [tutorial](https://aaronaddleman.com/post/hexcodes-and-iterm/) on iterm2 key codes, might be helpful.

body:
Maybe this will give you some ideas.

In my `.zshrc`:
```shell
# Homebrew (brew) zsh completions
if type brew &>/dev/null
then
  FPATH="$(brew --prefix)/share/zsh/site-functions:${FPATH}"
  fpath=(~/.zfunc $fpath)
  autoload -Uz compinit

  if [ "$(date +'%j')" != "$(stat -f '%Sm' -t '%j' ~/.zcompdump 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
      compinit
  else
      compinit -C
  fi
fi

# Alias tab completion with expansions
zstyle ':completion:*' menu yes select
zstyle ':completion:*' verbose yes
zstyle ':completion:*' group-name ''
zstyle ':completion:*:descriptions' format '%F{yellow}-- %d --%f'

_aliases() {
  local -a list expl
  local -a exp_names exp_display
  local k word="${PREFIX}${SUFFIX}"

  for k in ${(k)aliases}; do
    if [[ $k == ${PREFIX}* ]]; then
      list+=("$k:${aliases[$k]}")
    elif [[ -n $word ]]; then
      local direct="${aliases[$k]}"
      local first="${direct%% *}"
      # Resolve one level of alias chaining (e.g. gff=gfa -f, gfa=git fetch ...)
      local full_exp="$direct"
      if [[ -n "${aliases[$first]}" && "$first" != "$k" ]]; then
        full_exp="${direct/$first/${aliases[$first]}}"
      fi
      if [[ $full_exp == *${word}* ]]; then
        exp_names+=("$k")
        exp_display+=("$k -- $full_exp")
      fi
    fi
  done

  (( $#list )) && _describe -t aliases 'alias' list -- -l

  if (( $#exp_names )); then
    _wanted aliases expl 'alias' compadd -U -l -d exp_display -- $exp_names
    compstate[insert]=menu
  fi
}
```
Then in `~/.zfunc/_command_names`:
```shell
#autoload

# Modified version of _command_names that shows alias expansions via _aliases.
# Only change from the original: 'aliases:alias:compadd -Qk aliases'
# is replaced with 'aliases:alias:_aliases' so _describe shows expansions.

local args defs expl ffilt

zstyle -t ":completion:${curcontext}:commands" rehash && rehash

zstyle -t ":completion:${curcontext}:functions" prefix-needed && \
 [[ $PREFIX != [_.]* ]] && \
 ffilt='[(I)[^_.]*]'

defs=()

if [[ -n "$path[(r).]" || $PREFIX = */* ]]; then
  defs+=( 'executables:executable file:_files -g \*\(-\*\)' )
else
  # this is ignored but exists to facilitate the use of the fake style
  _description executables expl 'executable file'
fi

if [[ "$1" = -e ]]; then
  shift
elif (( ${#precommands:|builtin_precommands} )); then
  # precommand excludes internal options below
else
  [[ "$1" = - ]] && shift

  defs=( "$defs[@]"
    'aliases:alias:_aliases'
    'builtins:builtin command:compadd -Qk builtins'
    "functions:shell function:compadd -k 'functions$ffilt'"
    'commands:external command:_path_commands'
    'suffix-aliases:suffix alias:_suffix_alias_files'
    'reserved-words:reserved word:compadd -Qk reswords'
    'jobs:: _jobs -t'
    'parameters:: _parameters -g "^*(readonly|association)*" -qS= -r "\n\t\- =[+"'
    'parameters:: _parameters -g "*association*~*readonly*" -qS\[ -r "\n\t\- =[+"'
  )
fi

args=( "$@" )

local -a cmdpath

zstyle -a ":completion:${curcontext}" command-path cmdpath

if (( ! $#cmdpath && $#_comp_priv_prefix )); then
  cmdpath=( $path ${path/%\/bin//sbin} )
  cmdpath=( ${(u)^cmdpath}(/-N) )
fi

if (( $#cmdpath )); then
  local -a +h path
  local -A +h commands
  path=( $cmdpath:A )
fi

_alternative -O args "$defs[@]"
```

author: carlosala

body:
Many thanks Carlo!

> We were checking if there was a binary named git before checking for upgrade

Ah, I didn't spot that in my (admittedly cursory) hunt.

> but maybe macOS is letting the binary there but breaking it somehow

Yes, macOS has some indirection in its developer tools, so there's always "something" there, but by default that something just prompts you to install the developer tools, and if your installation is broken (e.g. by a macOS upgrade) then instead it complains about that to you. The actual `git` binary sits somewhere deep in the Xcode installation.
author: edbennett

body: Nice, then it should be solved!
author: carlosala

body:
I was having this problem as well. This can be resolved by moving the body of the workaround git function to a script in a `$PATH` directory. 

So:
 ```bash
touch ~/bin/git && sudo chmod +x ~/bin/git
```

Then you want to go ahead and add the function body but you'll likely have to modify it. so if you have:
```bash
if $(pwd -P | grep -q "^\/mnt\/**\/*"); then
  git.exe "$@"
else
  command git "$@"
fi
```

Change `command git "$@"` to `"/usr/bin/git" "$@"` and of course include the shebang at the top. So:
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash

if $(pwd -P | grep -q "^\/mnt\/**\/*"); then
  git.exe "$@"
else
  "/usr/bin/git" "$@"
fi
```

Then of course you want to make sure that the file location is in the path. I think the `.zshrc` file generated by oh-my-zsh on install has the necessary command included but if it's not there just add:
```bash
PATH="$HOME/bin:$PATH"
```

 Personally I prefer to put this line in `.zprofile` or `.profile` (just make sure you source the latter from the former).

body: It looks like a great workaround! WSL has its own bad things, that's sure. Closing this, as it's not our issue 😉
author: carlosala

body:
Fixing the default git prompt without modify the git binary involves putting the logic by @spider-mane inside of a `function git () { ... }` block, then changing the section of **lib/git.zsh** from `command git` to simply `git`, so that the function runs when the prompt is generated.

As @carlosala states, this is purely a workaround to a problem not stemming from ohmyzsh (namely, slow VM performance), but this got my WSL2 prompts from 10+ seconds down to a few hundred ms, and I can still keep a binary executable in **/bin/git** rather than overwriting with a script.

Thanks all!

body:
If you've got something that _you_ find useful, open a PR.

I'm a FreeBSD user but really can't find much in my configuration that is especially FreeBSD specific. Stuff like `freebsd-update fetch install` could perhaps be aliased.

From a quick glance at the archlinux plugin, it looks mostly like pacman aliases, often prefixing them with `sudo`. Given that FreeBSD doesn't include `sudo` in base and many FreeBSD people either use `doas` instead or prefer plain `su` I'm not sure how useful that would be. Note also that `pkg` supports it's own aliases - see the ALIAS block in `/usr/local/etc/pkg.conf`. The macos plugin seems to be functions reflecting areas where macos differs from other Unix systems.

In my opinion, even for ArchLinux, it should test `${+commands[sudo]}` and `(( EUID ))` before including `sudo` in the alias.
author: okapia

body: Working on a PR for this now. It's arguably a feature more than a bug; the `ZSH` environment variable controls the directory to which the oh-my-zsh repo gets cloned, but zsh itself supports a `ZDOTDIR` environment variable to control the directory in which it expects to find its `.zshrc` file. The README is correct, but I think the install script should respect zsh's `ZDOTDIR` environment variable.

This was done this way so that you don't need to make two separate calls. If you need to use it in a script, you can use `| sed -n 1p` for the IPv4 address or `| sed -n 2p` for the IPv6 address.

But most of the time you'll just use it to view the IP, so it does not hurt to have both. I have submitted #11458 for having a better error experience:

```sh
% geteip
77.98.1.354
error: no IPv6 route to host
```

body:
It might be feasible using commit trailers [1] [2]. A possible function command would be

```sh
function gccab {
  local coauthor="$1"; shift $(( $# > 0 ))
  git commit --trailer "Co-authored-by: ${coauthor}" "$@"
}

gccab "John Doe <name@example.com>"
```

[1]: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit#Documentation/git-commit.txt---trailerlttokengtltvaluegt
[2]: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers

That said, this takes a while to type and it might be better as Carlo said to add an automatism to the editor you use to write commits.

Either way, feel free to use the solution above. In [2] you'll find some configuration options if you want to automate this further, such as automatically running a command to get the author from somewhere (a commit maybe).

But I don't see this being a thing that will be used by many people.

author: carlosala

body:
The gnu-utils plugin does not add that alias. All it does is wrap `gls` as `ls` so that you use GNU `ls` instead of BSD `ls`. You are seeing a difference in behavior when the omz `gnu-utils` plugin is not enabled because then you are using BSD `ls`.  For BSD `ls`, `-G` enables color output. For GNU `ls`, `-G` suppresses printing group names in a long listing.

The alias is defined in this [if clause](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/lib/theme-and-appearance.zsh#L27-L33) in the "theme-and-appearance" lib that is always loaded by default by omz. It is loaded before plugins are, so it doesn't know that you will be wrapping `gls` as `ls` and the behavior can be different.

I think the best compromise may be to make the `gnu-utils` plugin aware of what `theme-and-appearance.zsh` has done by switching the `ls` alias to the `gls --color=tty` one or just dropping the `ls` alias.

body:
The only place where `$plugins` can be defined is in `.zshrc`, or any place before `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh` is reached (.zshenv or .zprofile also work). `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins.zsh` is not a supported location where `plugins=()` can be defined.

If you need to do it this way for some reason (e.g. different hosts get different plugins) I suggest you to copy the plugin sourcing logic (from `oh-my-zsh.sh`) directly in the `plugins.zsh` file so that the plugins in `$ZSH_CUSTOM` are sourced by `plugins.zsh`.

If this is not what you are doing please describe fully this statement

> My plugins reside the `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/` directory, and **are loaded in a `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins.zsh`**

```bash
#!/bin/bash

num_files=$(tar -tf file.tar.gz | wc -l)

if [ $num_files -gt 1 ]; then
  mkdir -p /path/to/folder/
  tar -xzvf file.tar.gz -C /path/to/folder/
else
  tar -xzvf file.tar.gz
fi
```

body:
Hi @k3n, thank you very much for the thorough feature request, it shows that you took the time to provide an actionable feedback and did the research.

To first answer your last point, this hasn't been discussed yet. To be fair, the only way to add links to both commits and issues on the terminal output is if the terminal emulator supports hyperlinks (i.e. [terminal hyperlinks](https://gist.github.com/egmontkob/eb114294efbcd5adb1944c9f3cb5feda) feature).

We are already testing for that feature on a variety of terminals in the installer and updater, so it isn't difficult to adapt the terminal changelog to add suport for that. If the terminal doesn't support this, it isn't feasible to add links as they'd be fully visible in the terminal output. 

The other option you sh, linking to a browser changelog, is only possible once we start using GitHub releases but that will come when we use a proper versioning process which is a long time coming.

So to summarize, I'll start working on adding hyperlinks to the output which is what can be shipped sooner. Do check if your terminal will support this feature though.

author: rwmitchell

body:
> Hm, I don't think thats an issue. Count the lines since the last command, write into the buffer at that location, done.
> 
> Applications like htop are constantly changing their buffer to provide "real-time" data, so a single rewrite in the buffer to display an icon shouldn't be hard.

As trivial as this is, I'm surprised you haven't submitted a patch yet.  If you're stuck on something, post it and maybe someone can help you out.
author: rwmitchell

body: > Hm, I don't think thats an issue. Count the lines since the last command, write into the buffer at that location, done. > > Applications like htop are constantly changing their buffer to provide "real-time" data, so a single rewrite in the buffer to display an icon shouldn't be hard. @Gandalf1783 Given your lack of response, I'm assuming you just wanted to gaslight someone (me in this case) without any actual knowledge of the problem? You just wanted to sound like you knew more than someone else without knowing anything at all? No program has enough knowledge (currently) to pull off what you think is trivial. The terminal doesn't know the purpose of the text it displays, it can't do it. zsh/omz/p10k doesn't know what happens in-between the prompts. They can control what is displayed on the current prompt lines. They can query where on the terminal they are, but once another program has run with unknown amount of text output and the screen scrolls, all for that previous knowledge is useless. Terminal queries for row number only apply to the displayed screen, not relative to the top of the scroll back buffer. To be fair, I too was annoyed by this some time ago and solved it for myself by displaying green/red lines across the entire terminal width before the next prompt is displayed. The color line is the status for everything above the line. I've left the prompt symbol in, but rarely look it. Here's an example: Now, if you'll notice, iTerm2 does indeed update its prompt marker (which is outside the terminal text area).

```
zstyle ':completion:*' list-colors ${(s.:.)LS_COLORS}
```

body:
Due to [1], `zle-keymap-select` does not actually trigger when selecting the `visual` mode.

In order to work around this limitation, we can define a user widget that triggers when `visual-mode` is triggered (i.e. when <kbd>v</kbd> is pressed) and there we can update the cursor. A naive implementation of this can be:

```sh
function _visual-mode {
  typeset -g VI_KEYMAP=visual
  _vi-mode-set-cursor-shape-for-keymap "$VI_KEYMAP"
  zle .visual-mode
}
zle -N visual-mode _visual-mode
```

[1]: https://zsh-users.zsh.narkive.com/T1J6F9Im/visual-viopp-modes-are-not-detected-by-zle-keymap-select

@ArkoSno let me know if that fixes the issue for you (you can copy-paste this in your terminal) and I will push the fix.

author: mcornella

body:
The completion function for `scp` calls ssh under the hood to get a list of files under the path specified. For example this is what it would run with `scp user@host:/`:
```sh
ssh -o 'BatchMode=yes' -a -x user@host command ls -d1FL -- '/\*'
```
This means that there needs to be a SSH connectivity and credentials, and it could freeze because of the ssh connection. You should check that you have good SSH connectivity when attempting to complete scp, or otherwise not use it at all.

body:
The issue is that you have defined a custom function that zsh doesn't know how to complete. As the expected arguments are the same as for `cd`, define the completion function as below
```sh
compdef _cd _my_cd
```
Here `_cd` is the completion function for cd and `_my_cd` is the function / command to be completed.

author: kaykayehnn

body: I catched the bug! If there's no update available (no new commits), the latest epoch checked is never updated. So, from that point until there's some update, it'll check every time at startup. Fixing!
author: carlosala

body:
Looks like this is an edge case when the local repository is at the latest upstream version but still the .zsh-update file reports a date too in the past.

The obvious fix is to rewrite the .zsh-update file if there are no updates, but on default settings this will mean the next update will be checked again in 13 days.

I think that was the behavior prior to the introduction of the `is_update_available` though, so it's a fine change. Let me work on that and push a fix.

Thank you for reporting!

author: Racle

body:
I was able to reproduce using `zsh` docker image with with `5.8.1`, but it's not reproducible anymore when using `5.9`.
I wasn't able to fix that issue using completion, but it seems it's some regression with case insensitive completion. It's something from zsh itself, and it's not only related to `cd`, so we can't do anything. You can workaround that error adding `CASE_SENSITIVE=true` in your `zshrc` before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`. This will make completion to be case sensitive but also avoid this error.
It seems like zsh team solved it in `5.9`, try to update if you can.
Thanks for the report!

body:
> I was able to reproduce using `zsh` docker image with with `5.8.1`, but it's not reproducible anymore when using `5.9`. I wasn't able to fix that issue using completion, but it seems it's some regression with case insensitive completion. It's something from zsh itself, and it's not only related to `cd`, so we can't do anything. You can workaround that error adding `CASE_SENSITIVE=true` in your `zshrc` before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`. This will make completion to be case sensitive but also avoid this error. It seems like zsh team solved it in `5.9`, try to update if you can. Thanks for the report!

Uncommenting the `CASE_SENSITIVE=true` setting in `~/.zshrc` solved the issue for me (macos + latest oh-my-zsh), thanks

The simplest reproducible scenario i could come up with is 
1. Create 2 folders `tmp_te` and `tmp_sq`. In my case it was important that the folders have the same prefix and that one ends with _te and the suffix of the second one starts with _s. This looks like it is voodoo but i hope there is some rational explanation to the fact that it's specifically `_te` not working with `_s`.
2. In that folder try `ls te<tab>` and that would complete `ls tmp_T` instead of `ls tmp_`

The fact that in all the cases that i've encountered this behaviour there was a capital letter involved made me think it does have to do something with case sensitive setting. I've tried. It worked. Thanks a lot for the hint. 👏🙏❤️🚀

body:
Hi @carlosala! Thank you for suggesting `zstyle` with `nvm`. It really helps me think better

It turned out that it has a bug. `alias-finder` already has [`-l`](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/master/plugins/alias-finder/README.md#options) flag which can turn on "showing longer alias", **but it seems broken.**

> Use --longer or -l to allow the aliases to be longer than the input (match aliases if they contain the input).

```sh
$ alias AAAAAAAAAA="echo a"
$ alias-finder "echo a" # [1]
AAAAAAAAAA='echo a'
$ alias-finder -l "echo a" # [2]
AAAAAAAAAA='echo a'
```

According to README, [1] must not be shown.
author: roeniss

body: I'd like to take this, the plugin is in need of a big rewrite. I'll work on it this weekend.
author: mcornella

body:
Ok, the -l argument is not actually for that but to show aliases even if the alias value is longer than the command (i.e. if the command is in the start of the alias value but is not the full thing).

Here's an example of how it would apply:

![IMG_20230421_194438.jpg](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1441704/233701470-5b59b96e-ddf1-4302-a957-3f87f30846ee.jpg)

So there is no bug here, but I also agree that what you're asking is sensible, so I'll still work on adding the parameter (I think moving to zstyle is a great idea Carlo).

body: It is neither an issue with zsh or omz, nor is it a bug.  It IS a feature in iterm2 as a part of shell integration.  It marks each prompt and allows you to use keyboard control to jump back to each prompt.  You can also create triggers that will add the blue triangle during any output.

body:
Basically, I never knew that the changelog appearing every time I run `omz update` was a bug. I got very used to seeing the changelog whenever I update without having to add an extra option or run the changelog command separately. I would like a way to have the changelog appear _as though I'd run it with `-i`_ without having to run it with `-i`.

My ideal solution would be a setting (like the `zstyle ':omz:update' interactive true` in the issue description) which, when set, means the `update` command runs as though `-i` is used even when it isn't used.
i.e. when the setting is true, `omz update` and `omz update -i` are identical.

Does that make sense?
author: GuySartorelli

body:
@GuySartorelli that doesn't make much sense. That interactive is just to check if your zsh session is running in a terminal (so you can see the changelog), and it's automatically set if you are running `omz update` directly in your terminal. If you want to see the changelog, you can run `omz changelog`.
Does this solves your issue?

Regardless, I'll close this and do a one-off solution for my own uses.

author: matusaurio

body: Actually, your `FZF_BASE` is not right. Normally, if your `fzf` binary is at `/usr/bin/fzf` then your `FZF_BASE` will be `/usr/share/fzf`. I'd recommend to remove `FZF_BASE` declaration and let plugin discover it. You can check the plugin docs to see what's going on https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/tree/master/plugins/fzf

body:
I know the issue is closed but I had the same problem and applied every solution provided here with no success. Finally I've found the right config. 
The key is to export `FZF_BASE`  *before* plugins section. Like this:

```
export FZF_BASE="/opt/homebrew/opt/fzf"
plugins=(... fzf)
```

hope it helpts
author: charafau

body: Indeed! All `omz` settings typically go before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`. In this case, this is the case! It's not important if it goes before the plugins array; it's important to appear before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`. I saw that it's explained in `fzf` plugin [README](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/tree/master/plugins/fzf#settings)!
author: carlosala

body:
Revamping this issue!

Homebrew can have a different installation folder. So, in the file `~/.zshrc`, before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`, you need to initialize Homebrew, and the folder of `fzf` will be discovered, not needing to set `FZF_BASE`.

So, file `~/.zshrc`:

```zsh
...
plugins=(
  ...
  fzf
  ...
)

# Homebrew
eval "$(/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin/brew shellenv)" # this is Homebrew folder in my case in Linux
fpath=($HOMEBREW_PREFIX/share/zsh/site-functions $fpath)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
...
```

Hope it helps!
