body:
This does not fix the bug. This is because you test if a file with the directory name already exist, but you must test if the archive does not contain a file with the same name as the archive. (Which is the case of `gzip file`.

You might have been confused by the working of gzip, but it replaces the file by the archive. The file will not exist then in the directory. 

Preferably you just remove this feature as it introduces bugs and it is better if it was kept simple.

```sh
touch file
gzip file
ls
> file.gz
extract file.gz
> extract: extracting to file
> /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file exists -- overwrite (y/n)? y
> pigz: abort: write error on /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file (Is a directory)
> gzip: /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file already exists; do you wish to overwrite (y or n)? y
> gzip: /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file: Is a directory
```
author: hwalinga

body:
Hello @mcornella again, 

[c4d1ea4](https://github.com/nbaronov/oh-my-zsh/commit/c4d1ea4cb5460ed4fdce211f25348858406dfb79) also does not fix this bug. The problem is that _inside_ the archive there is a file that is equal to the name of the archive. `[[ -e "$extract_dir" ]]` does not check that, because it is a file _inside_ the archive, **not** inside the current directory.

And please just like actually test if your code changes work against the bug report, not just assume. 

```
touch file
gzip file
ls
> file.gz
extract file.gz
> extract: extracting to file
> /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file exists -- overwrite (y/n)? y
> pigz: abort: write error on /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file (Is a directory)
> gzip: /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file already exists; do you wish to overwrite (y or n)? y
> gzip: /home/hielke/tmp/extract/file: Is a directory
```

You can see that your added `echo` statement now, and it does not add the random string, because `file` does not exist in the directory, but _inside_ the archive.

> @hwknsj what do you think if the default behaviour is to use gnu-ls? I think if someone installed gnu-ls is because they really want to use it. I was thinking on a zstyle setting. Could you try #11647 and tell me your thoughts!

@carlosala I took [your branch (PR 11661)](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/11661) for a spin and it works great using the `zstyle ':omz:lib:theme-and-appearance' gnu-ls no` setting!

I noticed that[ this commit not overwriting aliases](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/b22593c) was included in the updates today–I'm wondering if that achieves the same thing? (but maybe only in the case that `ls` has been `aliased` 🤔 ... ?)

re: default setting `gnu-ls`–i don't know if i can answer that for anyone but myself but i will speculate: maybe others have a compelling reason to use that versus the macOS native version, but I would think that if they did, they would probably create the alias themselves (e.g. `alias ls='gls'`), right? i encountered this because i'm using`macOS` and have `coreutils` installed via `homebrew` which includes `gls` etc. but i definitely _don't_ want to use `gls`, really ever as it breaks certain features such as listing xattributes

for the average user (and myself) on macOS it seems it can only cause mysterious issues like this one, right? perhaps this is why `homebrew`'s [coreutils _does not_ automatically link all the g*-utils](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/fe9eec52b85c2c9e3a103ff3e5bf37398421e528/Formula/coreutils.rb#L78-L100) by default (which would break some) but provides instructions to link the non-g versions upon install.

thank you for giving this issue so much attention! i realize there are a million other open issues and your time is valuable!

author: sebouh00

body:
Yes, I can reproduce this as well (never used that context menu option before, I think). I think it is a bug in Konsole to not handle the situation better though, as it appears that it just uses the directory URL reported using the OSC 7 message as-is here. While ZSH can guess whether it is executing on local system or not based on environment variables, Konsole is in a much better position to judge this based on the provided hostname.

As for what Konsole should probably do here: If the hostname of the reported URL matches the local’s system hostname it should instead open `file:///{PATH}` (dropping the hostname part), if the hostname is something else it should probably just hide the menu option since there is no reliable way for it to know how the connection to the shell reporting the OSC 7 message came to be – alternatively I guess to assume that is always happened using a direct SSH connection and open `sftp://{host_name}/{PATH}` instead.

This could be worked around in OMZ though (at least for the SSH vs local case by examining the environment variables). Should it be addressed this way by special-casing it and sending a differently structured directory URL just for Konsole? Or should this just be deferred to Konsole upstream? Finally Konsole could also be dropped from the whitelist again, but then “open new tab in same directory“ would also break.

@hrzhu: May I ask you to report this issue to Konsole? It’d be nice to remove that Konsole-specific workaround at some point again…

author: hrzhu

body:
Found this discussion on the exact semantics of OSC 7, particularly related to the remote case: https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/terminal-wg/specifications/-/issues/20

Unfortunately it never concluded and different Terminal Emulators appear to be implementing slightly different thing. (What a surprise given there is no spec…!)
From what I can tell though, minimal consensus appears to be:

  1. Shells should always emit a `file:`-URL including the current hostname as seen by the shell process, even if we could know the actual protocol used (this is what OMZ currently does for all whitelisted terminal emulators)
  2. The Terminal Emulator may only treat the path component of the received URL as local path if the hostname matches the one of the current machine or is empty and the `file:`-protocol is used
     * Shells should always include the hostname though, an empty hostname is considered legacy behaviour
     * Konsole violates this rule by treating all received paths as local paths
  3. The Terminal Emulator may ignore all other received URLs or treat them in some other unspecified way
     * Konsole offering to *Open File Manager* for remote URLs is hence technically OK, but obviously the current implementation is broken (hence this issue)

So to work around that OMZ still needs to know whether it is executing on a local or a remote machine, then we can work around this for Konsole by:

  1. Not including the hostname when executing on a local machine
  2. Doing any of the following:
     1. Do not emit any OSC 7 at all when executing on a remote machine
     2. Emit an OSC 7 when executing on a remote machine, but change the scheme part from `file:` to `sftp:`
     3. Do the second one only if we detect SSH, otherwise do the first (I like this one the most)

In any case, I doubt that it will be bullet-proof but if you read the linked issue, neither is OSC 7 itself in any case.

author: mcornella

body:
It is perfectly valid for a particular terminal to lack particular terminfo properties. However, in omz, setting this up is guarded with:

```
if (( ${+terminfo[smkx]} )) && (( ${+terminfo[rmkx]} )); then
  function zle-line-init() {
    echoti smkx
  }
```
I would suspect that `$TERM` is getting changed at some point after this is defined. Perhaps from `xterm-256color` to `alacritty` with the latter not available in the terminfo database. `TERM` is configurable from `.config/alacritty/alacritty.yml`.

You could use `print -r - ${terminfo[smkx]:-}` instead of `echoti`.

Thanks for the debugging @principis. #11703 should fix that, can you test it with `omz pr test 11703` (from within the SSH session).

Note: in order to fully test this, you should have 2 tabs:
1. Local session
2. SSH session

Then:
1. Run the command in tab (2)
2. Switch to (1) and press enter a few times
3. Switch to (2) and press enter a few times
4. Open a new tab: the tab opens in the same directory as tab (1) (local session).

Thank you for your help!

body:
If you want to detect visual mode reliably, use the `zle-line-pre-redraw` hook and test for `[[ $KEYMAP = vicmd ]] && (( REGION_ACTIVE ))`. That maps to how zsh defined visual mode internally. Wrapping widgets should be a last resort because it tends to lose the effect of widget flags. That #11586 "fix" misses the `visual-line-mode` widget. And a user might use an emacs region widget or a custom widget. And it only detects entering visual-mode, not leaving it.

The cursor is a bit of a tricky issue with visual mode. If the cursor is done in reverse video and reverse is used for the selection they cancel each other out. Otherwise, a block cursor works best for vi-command mode including visual mode. But this user has configured a vertical bar for visual mode.

If you really want to wrap the `visual-mode` widget, consider making it conditional depending on whether the cursor is even configured to be different.

It's worse in emacs mode where the cursor position is part of the region at the beginning but not at the end. But I think there are fewer users of the emacs region and they don't configure their cursor so it tends not to come up.

author: carlosala

body:
We need to run zshell as an interactive mode since we must load .zshrc before executing the command. In [rbenv](https://github.com/rbenv/rbenv), one of the well-known version managers for Ruby, the initial script is written in .zshrc.
Thus, we need to load .zshrc before running Ruby program to run the initial script of rbenv. That's a common practice in Ruby.
author: ono-max

body: Is it the configuration (not the script) in zshrc? You can for sure find the script outside of zshrc. I'd say that you might find some issues if you try to go this way, people might have some executions at the start of their login shells (neofetch, for example).
author: carlosala

body:
Sorry, that's right. It is the configuration in zshrc. The users of rbenv write the following expression in .zshrc.
```sh
eval "$(~/.rbenv/bin/rbenv init - zsh)"
```
author: ono-max

body: Then you should execute that command manually and then run your desired command.
author: carlosala

body:
Unfortunately, it's hard to run it manually because there are other version managers that run initialization expressions in .zshrc as well.
[chruby](https://github.com/postmodern/chruby) is another example.

body:
Taking into account how is `autoload` implemented, lazy-loading `nvm` makes no sense when used with `autoload` option, because it actually needs to be loaded as `autoload` uses a function from `nvm.sh`.
Even though, it probably make sense to me to make the code more robust, then if both options are set it just skips the `lazy` one.

Another option would be to implement the whole autoload process within `omz` so we can lazy-load as well.
author: carlosala

body: Makes sense, that they can not be used together, now that you mention it. It sure would be great if it would not trow an exception though. Adding a hint to the documentation would probably already help a lot :)

body:
Hey again!
I'm sorry if I didn't explain correctly, but what I wanted to say in the last message is that sometimes we have some plugins installed in our dotfiles for some/most of our machines, but not all.
For example I don't use nvm in my main personal machine, but share the same dotfiles with my work machine and my laptop.
Same thing happens for example with the Docker plugin. I don't have Docker in my laptop, but I do in my main and work machines. The plugin just gets silently ignored in my laptop.

I figured it out I think. The PATH var for my `kubectl` isn't set until the end in `.zshrc` and that's why it doesn't work.
```
### MANAGED BY RANCHER DESKTOP START (DO NOT EDIT)
export PATH="/Users/atael/.rd/bin:$PATH"
### MANAGED BY RANCHER DESKTOP END (DO NOT EDIT)
```
If I move the above lines to the top of `.zshrc` it works

author: DGotshalk

body:
@jjmarcos

Could you confirm that you see a similar issue without Oh My Zsh? via Bash or perhaps by temporarily disabling Oh My Zsh?

To do that, you could:

```shell

  % mv ~/.zshrc ~/.zshrc-disabled
  % zsh
```

after your testing, just reverse that

```sh
  % mv ~/.zshrc-disabled ~/.zshrc
  % zsh
```
author: robbyrussell

body:
Thanks for the reply robbyrussell.

I can confirm that when I disable Oh My Zsh following the steps you indicated, the issue is gone. When I enable Oh My Zsh, the issue described happens again.

Also tried with bash, if I type 'bash' at the prompt I can use "mc" without any problems at all.
author: jjmarcos

body:
Can you check whether adding the following at the end of your `.zshrc` file (with OMZ enabled) has any impact?
```sh
omz_termsupport_cwd(){}
add-zsh-hook -d precmd omz_termsupport_cwd
```
author: mcornella

body:
A workaround:

```bash
alias mc='SHELL=/usr/bin/bash /usr/bin/mc'
```

So you'll use bash when within Midnight Commander and zsh the rest of the time.
author: gasull

body:
Thanks for the workaround, it works!
In my case the alias line looks like this:
```shell

```

Unfortunately, these two didn't help:
```shell
omz_termsupport_cwd(){}
add-zsh-hook -d precmd omz_termsupport_cwd
```

another solution for me (mac os x): alias at `.zshrc`:

author: lastsky

body:
I've had this same issue since some time last year. I use p10k rather than omz. And I've encountered the issue on arch and debian (I don't have a mac to test with). As @BigBobDalton mentioned, when using `sudo` I don't get the message or slow start up.

I finally decided to search a solution after setting up a fresh Truenas Scale installation and experiencing the same problem. @lastsky's solution worked for me. With a minor change for Truenas since they don't let you install anything from the repositories. I created a `~/.bin` directory in my home folder and copied the mc binary into it. Below is my alias.

```
alias mc="SHELL=/usr/bin/bash ~/.bin/mc"
```

That works for Truenas and `alias mc="SHELL=/usr/bin/bash mc"` works for my arch and debian installations.

body:
I think the newer version of the docker plugin is broken. See my SO question: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/76506697/docker-tab-command-completion-not-showing-all-commands/76507287#76507287

Older docker plugin works fine but my oh-my-zsh install with newer version of docker plugin does not work as expected.
author: sdondley

body:
If I replace User B's ~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/docker directory (which uses the newer version of the docker plugin), with User A's `~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/docker` directory (which uses the old version of the docker plugin), everything works for User B.

`omz version` for the working version of the plugin reports `master (4c82a2ee)`. This version is from june of 2022,
author: sdondley

body: @sdondley which is your output to `docker version`?
author: carlosala

body:
```
 $ docker --version                                                                                                        
Docker version 24.0.2, build cb74dfc
```
author: sdondley

body:
You should have proper completion then! It should be generated properly. Does `docker completion zsh` works well?

And try also opening a new terminal, it might show not proper results the first terminal you open after updating
author: carlosala

body:
`docker completion zsh` dumps this out:

```
#compdef docker
compdef _docker docker

# zsh completion for docker                               -*- shell-script -*-

__docker_debug()
{
    local file="$BASH_COMP_DEBUG_FILE"
    if [[ -n ${file} ]]; then
        echo "$*" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

_docker()
{
<snip>
```

Opening new terminal/reloading zshrc or logging out and back in does not help.

As mentioned in the SO question linked to above, there are some completions that display in the newer version of the plugin, but not all of them, and none with subcommand descriptions like the older version of the docker plugin displayed. The newer plugin is loading and does something, but not nearly as well as the older version. 

Also, if the older version of the plugin worked 100% and the newer version does not work as well for the same user and same exact configuration, I think it's safe to say the newer version of the docker plugin has a bug.
author: sdondley

body: I have no idea if this has anything to with the problem but in the old, working version of the plugin, the `_docker` file is found in the `~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/docker` directory. But with the newer, broken docker plugin, there is no `_docker` in the docker plugin directory. Instead it shows up in `~/.oh-my-zsh/cache` directory.
author: sdondley

body: The `_docker` file for the old plugin has over 3100 lines of code while the new plugin has about 200. That's a pretty big discrepancy.
author: sdondley

body:
ok, so yeah, that's the difference. The completions generated from the command line using `docker completion zsh` are not nearly as complete as the completions found in the GitHub repo that the old plugin uses, which can be found at https://github.com/felixr/docker-zsh-completion/blob/master/_docker

I'm not sure if this is because I'm on macOS using a stock version of zsh and getting stale completions or what. It's beyond my understanding of how completions are implemented.
author: sdondley

body:
Here's a suggestion to fix the new plugin:

Instead of: 
    `docker completion zsh >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker"
    content=$(curl -sf -L`
    
Change to:
`content=$(curl -sf -L https://raw.githubusercontent.com/docker/cli/master/contrib/completion/zsh/_docker) && echo "$content" >| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker"`

Not sure if downloading the file each time is the best approach but it was the easiest to do.

author: carlosala

body:
Same issue here using an external plugin manager (antidote), as @akimdi.

`ZSH_CACHE_DIR` variable is not usually set by plugin managers. I've added this as a workaround to my zshrc before loading omz:
```zsh
# Set omz variables prior to loading omz plugins
ZSH_CACHE_DIR="${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/oh-my-zsh"
mkdir -p $ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions
```

Nevertheless, `docker` is not the only plugin affected: a quick grep of `>| "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/` in the `plugins` folder of this repo shows 22 of them.
author: borreeero

body: We use that a lot to generate completions (we set that variable in `oh-my-zsh.sh` at startup). Feel free to open an issue in plugin managers, we cannot remove that at this point.

```sh
typeset -p PROMPT; set -xv

Then you can turn this off with `set +xv`.

author: wszgrcy

body:
Seems like the culprit is `omz_termsupport_cwd` which outputs an OSC7 sequence to report the current working directory
```
+omz_termsupport_cwd:4> URL_PATH=/c/Users/chen/Desktop
+omz_termsupport_cwd:7> [[ -z '' && -z '' ]]
+omz_termsupport_cwd:10> printf '\e]7;file://%s%s\e\' DESKTOP-KLERGB9 /c/Users/chen/Desktop\
```
You can see the last line ends with the `\`. The OSC7 string sends a backslash character to signify the end of the URL, and it looks like it's not swallowed by msys.

Let me check this further to see if it's expected and need to disable the feature there or we can fix it.

author: carlosala

body:
Hi Justin, I just got around to searching the logs in-depth. I haven't been able to reproduce the issue, but I believe this may be caused by [the following 2 lines](https://github.com/justinharringa/dotfiles/blob/97158e4a0dedbaa0bc6c3cfb36f00a9708058866/zsh/config.zsh#L15-L16):
```sh
setopt LOCAL_OPTIONS # allow functions to have local options
setopt LOCAL_TRAPS # allow functions to have local traps
```

These never need to be set at a top level, what they do is define options at a function level to only be defined at a function-scope level. It may be possible that all the options that are set afterwards are undefined when exiting a function scope. 

Checking the `.zshrc` file, this file is loaded [outside a function scope](https://github.com/justinharringa/dotfiles/blob/97158e4a0dedbaa0bc6c3cfb36f00a9708058866/zsh/zshrc.symlink#L25-L29) (i.e. at the top level) so it may not be causing the issue as I have been able to reproduce. However it might be a good idea to remove those lines and see if they fix the issue.

----

Just writing this out, I have understood what's happening. Some time ago we introduced `_omz_source`, a function to load lib and plugin files, so that [alias disabling could be performed in an ergonomic manner](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh#skip-aliases).

With the above 2 lines, this is effectively removing all setopts once the `_omz_source` function is exited. So in effect, the above 2 lines are the culprit.

Thank you for your patience, and I hope this will solve your issue 😄
author: mcornella

body:
Aha! Fantastic - thank you so much. I cleared those out and everything works perfectly again. I appreciate the detailed response and all of the work you folks are doing. 

Thanks!

body:
@delfuego As i mentioned above, you may try to modify or remove the line number 49 of 
`~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/docker/docker.plugin.zsh` which loads the docker built in auto completion when docker version is higher than 23.0.0

My current code is like below
> ~/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/docker/docker.plugin.zsh

```
...
{
  # docker version returns `Docker version 24.0.2, build cb74dfcd85`
  # with `s:,:` remove the comma after the version, and select third word of it
  local _docker_version=${${(s:,:z)"$(command docker --version)"}[3]}
  # `docker completion` is only available from 23.0.0 on
    command cp "${0:h}/completions/_docker" "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker"
} &|
...
```
Then this will load your previous auto completion by oh-my-zsh

author: carlosala

body:
> Does it `docker completion zsh` output something? If it does, then run `rm $ZSH_COMPDUMP` (it's safe, it can be regenerated) and let's see if it works

`docker completion zsh` returns this below, and after remove $ZSH_COMPDUMP, also returns the same.

```bash
#compdef docker
compdef _docker docker

# zsh completion for docker                               -*- shell-script -*-

__docker_debug()
{
    local file="$BASH_COMP_DEBUG_FILE"
    if [[ -n ${file} ]]; then
        echo "$*" >> "${file}"
    fi
}

_docker()
{
    local shellCompDirectiveError=1
    local shellCompDirectiveNoSpace=2
    local shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp=4
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt=8
    local shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs=16
    local shellCompDirectiveKeepOrder=32

    local lastParam lastChar flagPrefix requestComp out directive comp lastComp noSpace keepOrder
    local -a completions

    __docker_debug "\n========= starting completion logic =========="
    __docker_debug "CURRENT: ${CURRENT}, words[*]: ${words[*]}"

    # The user could have moved the cursor backwards on the command-line.
    # We need to trigger completion from the $CURRENT location, so we need
    # to truncate the command-line ($words) up to the $CURRENT location.
    # (We cannot use $CURSOR as its value does not work when a command is an alias.)
    words=("${=words[1,CURRENT]}")
    __docker_debug "Truncated words[*]: ${words[*]},"

    lastParam=${words[-1]}
    lastChar=${lastParam[-1]}
    __docker_debug "lastParam: ${lastParam}, lastChar: ${lastChar}"

    # For zsh, when completing a flag with an = (e.g., docker -n=<TAB>)
    # completions must be prefixed with the flag
    setopt local_options BASH_REMATCH
    if [[ "${lastParam}" =~ '-.*=' ]]; then
        # We are dealing with a flag with an =
        flagPrefix="-P ${BASH_REMATCH}"
    fi

    # Prepare the command to obtain completions
    requestComp="${words[1]} __completeNoDesc ${words[2,-1]}"
    if [ "${lastChar}" = "" ]; then
        # If the last parameter is complete (there is a space following it)
        # We add an extra empty parameter so we can indicate this to the go completion code.
        __docker_debug "Adding extra empty parameter"
        requestComp="${requestComp} \"\""
    fi

    __docker_debug "About to call: eval ${requestComp}"

    # Use eval to handle any environment variables and such
    out=$(eval ${requestComp} 2>/dev/null)
    __docker_debug "completion output: ${out}"

    # Extract the directive integer following a : from the last line
    local lastLine
    while IFS='\n' read -r line; do
        lastLine=${line}
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")
    __docker_debug "last line: ${lastLine}"

    if [ "${lastLine[1]}" = : ]; then
        directive=${lastLine[2,-1]}
        # Remove the directive including the : and the newline
        local suffix
        (( suffix=${#lastLine}+2))
        out=${out[1,-$suffix]}
    else
        # There is no directive specified.  Leave $out as is.
        __docker_debug "No directive found.  Setting do default"
        directive=0
    fi

    __docker_debug "directive: ${directive}"
    __docker_debug "completions: ${out}"
    __docker_debug "flagPrefix: ${flagPrefix}"

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveError)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __docker_debug "Completion received error. Ignoring completions."
        return
    fi

    local activeHelpMarker="_activeHelp_ "
    local endIndex=${#activeHelpMarker}
    local startIndex=$((${#activeHelpMarker}+1))
    local hasActiveHelp=0
    while IFS='\n' read -r comp; do
        # Check if this is an activeHelp statement (i.e., prefixed with $activeHelpMarker)
        if [ "${comp[1,$endIndex]}" = "$activeHelpMarker" ];then
            __docker_debug "ActiveHelp found: $comp"
            comp="${comp[$startIndex,-1]}"
            if [ -n "$comp" ]; then
                compadd -x "${comp}"
                __docker_debug "ActiveHelp will need delimiter"
                hasActiveHelp=1
            fi

            continue
        fi

        if [ -n "$comp" ]; then
            # If requested, completions are returned with a description.
            # The description is preceded by a TAB character.
            # For zsh's _describe, we need to use a : instead of a TAB.
            # We first need to escape any : as part of the completion itself.
            comp=${comp//:/\\:}

            local tab="$(printf '\t')"
            comp=${comp//$tab/:}

            __docker_debug "Adding completion: ${comp}"
            completions+=${comp}
            lastComp=$comp
        fi
    done < <(printf "%s\n" "${out[@]}")

    # Add a delimiter after the activeHelp statements, but only if:
    # - there are completions following the activeHelp statements, or
    # - file completion will be performed (so there will be choices after the activeHelp)
    if [ $hasActiveHelp -eq 1 ]; then
        if [ ${#completions} -ne 0 ] || [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp)) -eq 0 ]; then
            __docker_debug "Adding activeHelp delimiter"
            compadd -x "--"
            hasActiveHelp=0
        fi
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoSpace)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __docker_debug "Activating nospace."
        noSpace="-S ''"
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveKeepOrder)) -ne 0 ]; then
        __docker_debug "Activating keep order."
        keepOrder="-V"
    fi

    if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterFileExt)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File extension filtering
        local filteringCmd
        filteringCmd='_files'
        for filter in ${completions[@]}; do
            if [ ${filter[1]} != '*' ]; then
                # zsh requires a glob pattern to do file filtering
                filter="\*.$filter"
            fi
            filteringCmd+=" -g $filter"
        done
        filteringCmd+=" ${flagPrefix}"

        __docker_debug "File filtering command: $filteringCmd"
        _arguments '*:filename:'"$filteringCmd"
    elif [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveFilterDirs)) -ne 0 ]; then
        # File completion for directories only
        local subdir
        subdir="${completions[1]}"
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            __docker_debug "Listing directories in $subdir"
            pushd "${subdir}" >/dev/null 2>&1
        else
            __docker_debug "Listing directories in ."
        fi

        local result
        _arguments '*:dirname:_files -/'" ${flagPrefix}"
        result=$?
        if [ -n "$subdir" ]; then
            popd >/dev/null 2>&1
        fi
        return $result
    else
        __docker_debug "Calling _describe"
        if eval _describe $keepOrder "completions" completions $flagPrefix $noSpace; then
            __docker_debug "_describe found some completions"

            # Return the success of having called _describe
            return 0
        else
            __docker_debug "_describe did not find completions."
            __docker_debug "Checking if we should do file completion."
            if [ $((directive & shellCompDirectiveNoFileComp)) -ne 0 ]; then
                __docker_debug "deactivating file completion"

                # We must return an error code here to let zsh know that there were no
                # completions found by _describe; this is what will trigger other
                # matching algorithms to attempt to find completions.
                # For example zsh can match letters in the middle of words.
                return 1
            else
                # Perform file completion
                __docker_debug "Activating file completion"

                # We must return the result of this command, so it must be the
                # last command, or else we must store its result to return it.
                _arguments '*:filename:_files'" ${flagPrefix}"
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# don't run the completion function when being source-ed or eval-ed
if [ "$funcstack[1]" = "_docker" ]; then
    _docker
fi
```

body:
Docker completion changed, and now uses a new system that will improve with time (official docker/cli repo is pushing it, not us) but now misses some important things like completing container names, image names, etc. As it will overtake the legacy completion, we already implemented it in `omz`.
If you prefer the old style, you can add the following snippet before sourcing `oh-my-zsh.sh`.
```zsh
zstyle ':omz:plugins:docker' legacy-completion yes
```
Thanks for the report!

The' fn' key is handled outside of any terminal emulator. It's related to the keyboard; when pressed, it sends a single keycode to the terminal.
In both `alacritty` (which works) and `xfce4-terminal` (which doesn't work), could you press `Ctrl+v` and then `fn+0` and post the output here? We can debug it; let's see which keycode your keyboard sends to the terminal.

Could you now repeat the same process, but executing previously `zsh -f` on each terminal?
It's really weird, because Alacritty is literally receiving the char `/`. It's not interpreting keycodes, it just receives one keycode.
author: carlosala

body:
![image](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/assets/76575902/6823cf37-20fa-45ee-8b0f-f319123eebc8)

**Alacritty**, **gnome-terminal**, **xfce4-terminal** and **urxvt** (from `rxvt-unicode` package).  
PS: **gnome-terminal** and **urxvt** also receive `/` as `^[Oo` in omz.

I tried if before making this issue. The problem is not with zsh as it seems.
author: pandasoli

body: That's extremely weird, being something that changes from term to term. I'd recommend you add `bindkey -s '^[0o' '/'` in your `zshrc`. It should solve your issues, and the terms that behave correctly should not be affected.
author: carlosala

body:
Solved! Thanks @carlosala.

Just put the following line in the `.zshrc`:
```bash
bindkey -s '^[Oo' '/'
```
> PS: It's a uppercase "o" letter, no a zero.

author: mcornella

body:
Hi @FFdhorkin, thank you for the report.

This looks like a zsh issue. In addition to the commands you tried, I also ran the command below, which [rehashes commands when performing a completion](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Completion-System.html#index-rehash_002c-completion-style):
```sh
zstyle ':completion:*' rehash true
```
In any case, I believe the only one needed in this case is [`setopt hash_list_all`](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Options.html#index-HASHLISTALL), which is in effect when offering corrections.

I can reproduce the same issue with `tsc`, which gets corrected to `tc`. Other commands in `node_modules/.bin` that don't have a similar command, such as `esbuild`, don't get a correction, but they also don't appear in `hash | grep`.

I have checked the docs but found no explanation for this behavior, so I then searched what happens when this correction is offered.

This happens in the following function [1], which as you can see in the link, rehashes the directories in `$PATH` if the `HASHLISTALL` option is set.

This leads us to the function in [2], which as you can see, has a condition to not hash a direcctory if the path is relative.

Indeed, if I instead add the directory with an absolute path, then the command appears in `hash | grep` and the correction is no longer offered.
```sh
path+=("$PWD/node_modules/.bin")
```

So this seems to be expected behavior. I would suggest you to either ask for this behavior to be changed, or to at least document this in [3] or [4].

[1]: https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/blob/70320635b4b50b1e84f70e17bf40f107d140bdcf/Src/utils.c#L3085-L3086

[2]: https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/blob/70320635b4b50b1e84f70e17bf40f107d140bdcf/Src/hashtable.c#L644-L645

[3]: https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Options.html#index-HASHLISTALL

[4]: https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/Release/Options.html#index-CORRECT

author: todd-dsm

body:
You could use docker if you have it installed:
```sh
docker run -it ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh zsh
```
Note that it comes bare-bones, without any extra tooling, and it's using Ubuntu so it's unlikely that you'll be able to reproduce it.

You can also copy the `templates/zshrc.zsh-template` file into the `.zshrc` after backing it up, however this assumes that you don't have any other changes made, like to `.zshenv`, `.zprofile`, or other global files installed by your OS.

In your case there may be other integrations that make this issue happen combined with the `common-aliases` plugin. The fastest way to see the full rc files is creating a log file of the startup:
```sh
zsh -xvlic exit &>~/omz-debug.log
```
and sharing this over at logs@ohmyz.sh

FWIW, I have tested your repro with Terminal.app on my macOS 13.6 VM (Ventura) and I was unable to reproduce it as well.

body:
Finally had a chance to go through this. I ran the command to output the logs and I got lucky; right at the top of the first log was a mis-configuration (on my part) in `~/.zprofile`

```sh
+/Users/USERNAME/.zprofile:3> [ -f /Users/USERNAME/.zshrc ']'
+/Users/USERNAME/.zprofile:4> . /Users/USERNAME/.zshrc
```

Some mbp configuration scripts gone wild 😬  Anyway, all fixed...

And now I know how to generate logs  😀  thank you @mcornella
author: todd-dsm

body:
Please see [related FAQ entry about `no matches found`](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/FAQ#zsh-no-matches-found): you can use quotes when using characters such as `[]` in a parameter (called glob characters). 

Any of these would work:
```sh
# using double quotes
pip install "rembg[gpu,cli]"

# using single quotes
pip install 'rembg[gpu,cli]'

# using back slashes
pip install rembg\[gpu,cli\]

# using `noglob`
noglob pip install rembg[gpu,cli]

# alias pip with `noglob` so all `pip` command runs disable globs
alias pip='noglob pip'
pip install rembg[gpu,cli]
```

body:
Hi Gerry, the only place I've found this related error is from command `atool`: https://fossies.org/linux/atool/atool#l_276

Is it possible that you have that aliased to the `alias` name? Try the commands below to check:
```
which -a als
which -a alias
```

They should report the following:
```
als () {
	(( $+commands[python3] )) || {
		echo "[error] No python executable detected"
		return
	}
	alias | python3 "/home/user/.ohmyzsh/plugins/aliases/cheatsheet.py" "$@"
}
alias: shell built-in command
```

author: carlosala

body:
I was able to reproduce this on a repo (clone of https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh) with a few local branches (18) amounting to 5 seconds of delay. Trace can be observed in https://asciinema.org/a/615487.

Further dissecting the times of each separate command, I found that the one contributing the most to the delay is `git cherry`:
```console
$ time git cherry $default_branch $(git commit-tree $(git rev-parse $branch\^{tree}) -p $merge_base -m _)
+ 3436c3ded2272c726b4579bb6590c9a29cf147c3
git cherry $default_branch   0,39s user 0,02s system 99% cpu 0,408 total
$ time git commit-tree $(git rev-parse $branch\^{tree}) -p $merge_base -m _
40cae639c0f56dd4df88ed5a4798d49572c7fdc2
git commit-tree $(git rev-parse $branch\^{tree}) -p $merge_base -m _  0,00s user 0,00s system 84% cpu 0,002 total
$ time git rev-parse $branch\^{tree}
5eb6b5d6e12923dec383e062bc8e3891d13d290c
git rev-parse $branch\^{tree}  0,00s user 0,00s system 85% cpu 0,001 total
$ time git cherry master 60032f151744a56e8a4a14d83cfde6dd877214ba                                        
+ 60032f151744a56e8a4a14d83cfde6dd877214ba
git cherry master 60032f151744a56e8a4a14d83cfde6dd877214ba  0,37s user 0,01s system 98% cpu 0,388 total
```

Looking at [the docs](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-cherry) it seems that this is normal, as `git` needs to walk the full list of commits between branch and merge base, for each branch that is checked.

I haven't found any way to speed up this process, so I would rather keep the old behavior and make it fast for frequent runs, and separate this new feature into its own function for infrequent ones where we can deal with the slowness.
author: mcornella

body:
Hello @8ullyMaguire, we are not looking to implement other people's code in zsh.

You are welcome to take on this feature request to do the porting yourself, especially since this is something that you seem to care about. I looked a bit over the code, and it doesn't seem to be using much or any bash-only features.

Other alternatives are using [`fzf`](https://github.com/junegunn/fzf) and history, as is [already possible in zsh](https://github.com/joshskidmore/zsh-fzf-history-search).

Thanks for the contribution!

body: > This is the result of executing code You are right, I updated my MAC system from Intel to ARM(change my MAC), but it was three months ago, and the problem only appeared recently. Possibly you're hit by https://github.com/docker/for-mac/issues/6343 and you updated Docker recently. The right path should be in `/Applications/Docker.app/Contents/Resources/etc/docker` (try looking on that path and finding the right file), or otherwise not exist under that path (in which case, you should remove the symlink). Confirmed not happening because of OMZ.

author: ThisIsMissEm

body:
I had a similar problem on `wsl` and I've solved it.
My reported error was something like this:  `coinit:527: no such file or directory: /usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/_docker`
The cause of my problem is that the file `/usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/_docker` is softlinked to `/mnt/wsl/docker-desktop/cli-tools/usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/_docker`, and my docker-desktop was uninstalled, when I looked at the directory /usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/ I found the _docker file was red, so I just needed to delete this file and everything worked fine!
```zsh
sudo rm /usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/_docker
```

The symbolic link `/mnt/wsl/docker-desktop/cli-tools/usr/share/zsh/vendor-completions/_docker` became broken after a Docker Desktop update (Docker Desktop 4.27.1, build 136059). I did not manage to find a new target in `/mnt/wsl/docker-desktop/cli-tools` so I removed the symlink.
author: maximaging

body:
遇到了类似问题，已解决；

# 报错
运行
`brew doctor && brew cleanup && source ~/.zshrc`
报错
`compinit:527: no such file or directory: /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew`
`compinit:527: no such file or directory: /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew_services`

# 定位
查看该文件指向的软链
`ls -l /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew`
找到原因，'/site-functions/_brew'是指向了一个不存在的路径(不确定这里的软链是咋来的)
`/usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew -> ../../../Homebrew/completions/zsh/_brew`

# 解决
### 先找到brew安装目录'/opt/homebrew/'，再向下进入'/completions/zsh/'，ls发现有'_brew'文件，建立正确软链
`ln -fsv /opt/homebrew/completions/zsh/_brew /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew`

### 至此第一个报错已解决，要想解决第二个，得先看下是否安装了'brew_services'
运行该命令，未安装会自动安装；
`brew services`
获取到安装路径
`Cloning into '/opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services'...`

### 再来一次找'_brew_services'，先cd到'/opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services/'，再向下进入'/completions/zsh/'，ls发现有'_brew_services'文件，建立正确软链
`ln -fsv /opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services/completions/zsh/_brew_services /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew_services`

### 再次运行检查
`brew doctor && brew cleanup && source ~/.zshrc`
无报错
`Your system is ready to brew.`

> 
> # 解决
> ### 先找到brew安装目录'/opt/homebrew/'，再向下进入'/completions/zsh/'，ls发现有'_brew'文件，建立正确软链
> `ln -fsv /opt/homebrew/completions/zsh/_brew /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew`
> 
> ### 至此第一个报错已解决，要想解决第二个，得先看下是否安装了'brew_services'
> 运行该命令，未安装会自动安装； `brew services` 获取到安装路径 `Cloning into '/opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services'...`
> 
> ### 再来一次找'_brew_services'，先cd到'/opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services/'，再向下进入'/completions/zsh/'，ls发现有'_brew_services'文件，建立正确软链
> `ln -fsv /opt/homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services/completions/zsh/_brew_services /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew_services`
> 
> ### 再次运行检查
> `brew doctor && brew cleanup && source ~/.zshrc` 无报错 `Your system is ready to brew.`

This worked. Thanks @yaoliangcui

body:
For me, the symlink was wrong:

```
$ ls -l /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/
total 0
lrwxr-xr-x  1 matze  admin  39 Dec 29  2016 _brew -> ../../../Homebrew/completions/zsh/_brew
lrwxr-xr-x  1 matze  admin  88 Jun 11  2020 _brew_services -> ../../../Homebrew/Library/Taps/homebrew/homebrew-services/completions/zsh/_brew_services
```

Both files were not available in these paths.

I unlinked both:

```
unlink /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew
unlink /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew_services
```

… and linked the one manually that was available:

```
ln -s /opt/homebrew/completions/zsh/_brew /usr/local/share/zsh/site-functions/_brew
```

author: mcornella

body:
Nevermind, I was able to test this on (was on phone).

The issue happens because you put `%t` inside `%{%}` (which tell the shell to effectively consider the content taking 0 characters of length in the prompt.

Since `%t` expands to a string like `HH:mm`, that takes 5 characters. When you press tab to complete, the prompt is not redrawn, but Zsh thinks it's 5 characters more to the left.

This is solved by putting it outside `%{%}`:
```sh
PROMPT="%(?:%{${fg[yellow]}%}%t %{${fg_bold[green]}%}➜ :%{${fg[yellow]}%}%t %{${fg_bold[red]}%}➜ )"
PROMPT+=' %{${fg[cyan]}%}%c%{$reset_color%} $(git_prompt_info)'

ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX="%{${fg_bold[blue]}%}git:(%{${fg[red]}%}"
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX="%{$reset_color%} "
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_DIRTY="%{${fg[blue]}%}) %{${fg[yellow]}%}✗"
ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_CLEAN="%{${fg[blue]}%})"
```

body:
I was able to reproduce the same on Termux.

This happens because the `omz_urlencode` function does not work correctly when there are no ASCII locales such as `C`, and Termux [only has a UTF-8 locale](https://github.com/termux/termux-packages/issues/546).

I pushed #12076 to work around this limitation. You can test it out with `omz pr test 12076`, it should work correctly but please confirm otherwise.

body:
This is not OMZ-related, but rather a difference in how the global zsh files are set up in the distribution you're using.

Effectively, either `/etc/zsh/zshrc` or `/etc/zsh/zshenv` should read and source the files under `/etc/profile.d/`. This doesn't need to be the case, since as a general rule these files might contain bash-only syntax.

If you're only going to use this with zsh, I'd suggest putting this in a different location and sourcing it from your .zshrc or .zshenv file. You can also put this in `$ZSH_CUSTOM/conda.zsh` if you're only going to use it with Oh My Zsh, but I'm not sure how miniconda runs.

You can check out [this doc about zsh startup files](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Intro/intro_3.html) for more information.

body:
```
# Enable Powerlevel10k instant prompt. Should stay close to the top of ~/.zshrc.
# Initialization code that may require console input (password prompts, [y/n]
# confirmations, etc.) must go above this block; everything else may go below.
if [[ -r "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh" ]]; then
  source "${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/p10k-instant-prompt-${(%):-%n}.zsh"
fi

# If you come from bash you might have to change your $PATH.
# export PATH=$HOME/bin:/usr/local/bin:$PATH

# Path to your oh-my-zsh installation.
export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"

# Set name of the theme to load --- if set to "random", it will
# load a random theme each time oh-my-zsh is loaded, in which case,

plugins=(git)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# User configuration

# export MANPATH="/usr/local/man:$MANPATH"

### Added by Zinit's installer
if [[ ! -f $HOME/.local/share/zinit/zinit.git/zinit.zsh ]]; then
    print -P "%F{33} %F{220}Installing %F{33}ZDHARMA-CONTINUUM%F{220} Initiative Plugin Manager (%F{33}zdharma-continuum/zinit%F{220})…%f"
    command mkdir -p "$HOME/.local/share/zinit" && command chmod g-rwX "$HOME/.local/share/zinit"
    command git clone https://github.com/zdharma-continuum/zinit "$HOME/.local/share/zinit/zinit.git" && \
        print -P "%F{33} %F{34}Installation successful.%f%b" || \

fi

source "$HOME/.local/share/zinit/zinit.git/zinit.zsh"
autoload -Uz _zinit
(( ${+_comps} )) && _comps[zinit]=_zinit

# Load a few important annexes, without Turbo

I'll look into zstyle more. I will also look into seeing if there is a way to allow `sudo -e` to work in writable directories safely somehow since that'd be much nicer in my opinion since, as I recall, since `sudo -e` runs as the user you can keep your own editing setup rather than using root's. If I make progress on either I'll let you know.

Oh, and for the above, I forgot to mention this but sudo vim should use the root env so vim should root env; you'd need `sudo -E vim` for the malicious plugin situation, unless said plugins were installed for the root user I believe; that doesn't change the fact that I agree with you that `sudo -e` is greatly preferred in most situations though.
author: zwimer

body:
Looking into sudoedit more, it seems the only way to permit writable dirs is to edit the sudoers file and override a global config options, and that's not acceptable; it seems this was prevented for security purposes so I guess I'll nix that idea.

The next two options are to use zstyle as config to:
1. Change what `sudo $EDITOR` transforms into
2. If the user prefers this, only for directories where `sudo -e` would fail, use sudo vim. I think this is acceptable since the user is intentionally invoking sudo presumably, given that the nature of this plugin is 'prepend sudo'; it could be a fallback if `sudo -e` will fail. Something like `if owner(dirname(args)) == user || is_writable((dirnames(args)) then`. Though thinking on this, that feels like a lot of engineering as you might have to parse args, ignore flags, etc; if someone else wants to put in that labor great but I don't know if I have the time for that. Not unless we are ok with it being a rudimentary logic that assumes all arguments not starting with a dash are filepaths and just checks the `dirname` of each? Which maybe is ok since that'd probably work 99% of the time and if it fails, we are just at the status quo.

Both have their strengths and weaknesses; though I'm leaning towards the second as the first would just disable `sudo -e` entirely which is a loss in my opinion. The by-default replacement `sudo -e` over `sudo vim` is very nice and I'd like to keep that whenever doing so wouldn't cause the command to fail.
author: zwimer

body: Actually, thinking about that, if the user already has permission to write on that directory, `sudo -e` shouldn't be needed at all, right? It's a weird corner case otherwise. We should leave it as is IMO
author: carlosala

body:
This is very common issue for me, I hit it daily. For example, editing files of a docker container's user (maybe root; but maybe some other UID/GID). I often edit the config files of my containers and these are typically owned by other users (sometimes root, sometimes other users); yet because I mount these inside of containers they aren't in a directory owned by root but rather somewhere within my home directory.

I think files in container volumes is a perfect example of where the enclosing dir (on the host) is not owned by root despite the inner files being owned by root (or other users), as within the container likely the enclosing dir is owned by root. Mounted volumes in general might have this actually, but docker is the use case I hit most often personally.

A (less common) use case of this issue in general is that `sudo -e` also fails when a user tries to edit `<some dir>`, a supported feature of vim. I mention this as I do sometimes `vim` directories with sudo, in a root owned directory; it fails despite not being a writable directory because sudoedit doesn't support directory arguments like `vim` does. That said, for most people the docker issue is much more likely. I am just pointing out other failure cases the solution above would also fix.

If you dislike the idea of changing the default behavior, we could also make it a toggle using zstyle. I.e. 'if you enable this zstyle option it can allow editing files in writable directories'. I think it ought to be fine on by default, but having it off by default isn't really an issue as long as the zstyle config option is documented.
author: zwimer

body: My main concert is adding too much complexity to a simple plugin and keep bumping over corner cases, not sure which is the best option to proceed here...
author: carlosala

body: Another common failure mode is simply editing a link. `sudo -e` fails for all links, including those entirely owned by root, pointing to a root owned file, all in root owned directories. This has also bit me editing various files like systemd files and some config files in `etc`.

author: carlosala

body:
I'll figure out how to convert this function into a PR (and clean it up) in a bit, but right now I need to sleep.

I'm thinking running this function on the arguments is sufficient to catch at least the biggest offenders here:
```zsh
__sudo_e_ok() {  # Return 1 if we should not prefer sudo -e "$@" over sudo "$EDITOR" "$@"
    local FILE GRP GROUPS=( $(id -G) )
    for FILE in "$@"; do
        [[ -d "$FILE" || -L "$FILE" ]] && return 1  # Dirs and symlinks fail
        local DIR="$(dirname "$FILE")"
        [[ ! -d "$DIR" ]] && return 1  # Non-existent parent
        [[ "$(id -u)" -eq "$(ls -ldn "$DIR" | awk '{print $3}')" ]] && return 1  # We own the dir
        local OWNER=( $(ls -ldn "$DIR" | awk '{print $4}') )
        for GRP in "${GROUPS[@]}"; do
            [[ "$GRP" == "$OWNER" ]] && return 1  # A group we are in owns the dir
        done
    done
    return 0  # sudo -e is ok
}
```

As a simple test, the following all pass:
```zsh
# sudo -e
__sudo_e_ok /etc/hosts      && echo "Success 1" # File exists
__sudo_e_ok /foo            && echo "Success 2" # File does not exist
__sudo_e_ok /etc/hosts /foo && echo "Success 3" # All of the above
# no sudo -e
__sudo_e_ok /tmp                     || echo "Success 4" # Symlink
__sudo_e_ok "$HOME"                  || echo "Success 5" # Dir
__sudo_e_ok "$HOME/foo"              || echo "Success 6" # User owned
__sudo_e_ok /tmp "$HOME" "$HOME/foo" || echo "Success 7" # All of the above
```

author: pavanjadhaw

body:
Fix `tee: /completions/_docker: No such file or directory`  

**Root cause**
The [docker.plugin.zsh](docker.plugin.zsh) from Oh My Zsh runs:
```
command docker completion zsh | tee "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions/_docker" > /dev/null
```
Full Oh My Zsh sets `ZSH_CACHE_DIR` (and creates cache dirs) in `oh-my-zsh.sh`.
But if your setup uses [Zim](https://github.com/zimfw/zimfw) and sources the docker plugin directly without loading that file, so **`ZSH_CACHE_DIR`** is never set.

**Recommended fix**
Set a default cache location and ensure the completions subdirectory exists before Zim initializes in `.zshenv`
```
# Oh My Zsh plugins (via Zim) expect ZSH_CACHE_DIR; if unset, paths become /completions/... under /
: "${ZSH_CACHE_DIR:=${XDG_CACHE_HOME:-$HOME/.cache}/zsh}"
[[ -d $ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions ]] || mkdir -p -- "$ZSH_CACHE_DIR/completions"
```

Hi Andrew, thanks for the ideas!
1. Sounds sensible to me, but it is actually [what happens at the end of successful update](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/c0074974313215360a9529e01204792cdf6ca473/lib/cli.zsh#L793-L798)

author: carlosala

body:
The reason why I didn't document the `--unattended` flag was to prevent misuse in scripts. To run the `omz update` command, an interactive zsh session needs to be ran, and then OMZ loaded, in order to have the `omz update` command.

It's more straightforward to just run the upgrade.sh script directly (see below), which already supports non-interactive runs, especially if this is done in an automated fashion.

```sh
zsh "$ZSH/tools/upgrade.sh"
```
author: mcornella

body:
Odd UX.

Can a `#!/bin/zsh`, `#!/usr/bin/env zsh`, etc. shebang be supplied, and the `.sh` file extension removed, so that the upgrade script can be executed more conveniently, like other executable scripts? If we rename it to `$ZSH/tools/upgrade-zsh` then the basename would be suitable to add to `$PATH`.

Can we merge these competing scripts together, so that the overall `omz update` no longer depends on an interactive context?

author: mcandre

body:
> Can we merge these competing scripts together, so that the overall `omz update` no longer depends on an interactive context?

If we rework the CLI to be a standalone script, we won't have access to some features that are only local to the interactive session. These are a few commands that require access to the interactive session data:

- `omz theme list`
- `omz theme use`
- `omz reload`
- `omz plugin load`
- `omz plugin enable`

I'd rather keep the CLI as a function, than a script, as changing it will make everything else almost impossible to work.

The removal of the `--unattended` flag is tracked in #9808.
author: mcornella

body:
I understand that some features currently require an interactive prompt, based on the existing design.

I know that it will make more work. I look forward to an improved design that supports automation for of the features.

Especially welcome any incremental steps in that direction. We don't have to resolve all of them at once, unless there is reason to believe a comprehensive solution is attainable near term.

Sourcing brew plugin directly can be used as a workaround:

```diff
+ # Preloading brew plugin so compinit can correctly find brew completions
+ # see https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/12183
+ source $ZSH/plugins/brew/brew.plugin.zsh

# Which plugins would you like to load?
# Standard plugins can be found in $ZSH/plugins/
# Custom plugins may be added to $ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/
# Example format: plugins=(rails git textmate ruby lighthouse)
# Add wisely, as too many plugins slow down shell startup.
plugins=(
-   brew
    ...
)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

Other shells might not act on bracketed paste sequences, that's why I'm suspecting the terminal emulator instead.

The only other suspect is url-quote-magic, but using `DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS=true` should have bypassed it. 

Try this debugging script now:

```sh
rm "${HOME}/zsh-bracketed-paste.log" 
for widget in self-insert bracketed-paste autosuggest-orig-1-bracketed-paste; do
  eval "
  _debug_widget-$widget() {
    {
      setopt localoptions xtrace
      ${widgets[$widget]#user:*} \"\$@\"
    } 2>>| ${HOME}/zsh-bracketed-paste.log
  }
  "
  zle -N $widget _debug_widget-$widget
done
```

It would also be helpful to know the value of `$TERM`. Thanks!

author: marcospgp

body:
The inconsistency with spacing is related to how the prompt is loaded (it checks `git status` before drawing). Basically if you manage to click enter before drawing it happens. You can reproduce it easier executing `PROMPT+='(sleep 2)'`.
That thing of pressing the up arrow, I can reproduce it as well. The problem is that the character sent to the terminal when the prompt is not drawn yet is `^[[A` instead of `^[[OA`, which in my case is the one that matches with `"${terminfo[kcuu1]}"`.
[Here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/43613d7b710f9a06971fac48dea3301bbc15b579/lib/key-bindings.zsh#L34-L51) we're setting that only for `"${terminfo[kcuu1]}"`, and not `^[[A`.
author: carlosala

body: @mcornella what do you think about this? Should we bind as well `^[[A` to avoid this behaviour?
author: carlosala

body:
I think terminal's application mode (smkx) is not yet ready when the prompt hasn't been drawn. If we bind a specific key sequence without using `$terminfo` we might have issues cross-terminal, i.e. when a different terminal emulator sends a different key sequence or the same for a different key combination.

We might be able to fix this one instance, but let's be vigilant for other issues.

body:
Hi! Most of the aliases are under plugins (which are opt-in for users). I'm sorry you're having such a bad experience. You can as well fine-grain choose your aliases as explained [here](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh?tab=readme-ov-file#skip-aliases), but aliases are one of the main purpose of plugins. We're not adding any new aliases to the `lib/` directory, which is loaded for every user.
Thanks for your idea, but we're not willing to make such a breaking change for many users, taking into account that most aliases are opt-in with plugins.
author: carlosala

body:
Hello Kiran, thanks for the report. I assume this is related to the ohmyz.sh website.

We are currently working on refreshing it and we have taken note of your suggestion.

As this issue tracker is only related to Oh My Zsh itself (the framework), I'll be closing the issue.

Cheers
author: mcornella

body:
Confirming that a rollback to Tmux 3.3a does indeed fix the issue in my case. The `-t` flag doesn't expect an argument.

Edit: [This NULL check was added](https://github.com/tmux/tmux/blob/608d113486835515e7a89b1511704440c68ae817/arguments.c#L170) since 3.3a and is probably the reason this no longer works.

ZSH Version: 5.9
TMUX Version: tmux 3.3a
OH MY ZSH date: 2023-11-29 (Nixpkgs unstable has the oh-my-zsh repo pinned to this date)

> Could you make sure that you have the exact configuration that https://github.com/gpakosz/.tmux proposes?

author: carlosala

body:
So, the configuration is exactly as noted in the .tmux link provided above.

With ZSH_TMUX_AUTOSTART (which I have disabled as it was causing other, unrelated issues) all seemed to load fine. That doesn't negate the fact that now, if I have the tmux plugin enabled, launching 'tmux' causes the .tmux.conf.local to be completely ignored.  If I use the 'tmux' command (which is an alias at this point: _zsh_tmux_plugin_run) it skips the .tmux.conf.local.

If I directly call the tmux executable or use a function that wraps tmux, all works properly (such as 'ts'):

```
 which ts
ts () {
        if [[ -z $1 ]] || [[ ${1:0:1} == '-' ]]
        then
                tmux new-session "$@"
        else
                tmux new-session -s "$@"
        fi
}

```

executing 'tmux' with any argument works properly of course.  It's just the bare 'tmux' execution that seems to launch the tmux session without parsing the .tmux.conf.local file.

body:
I can reproduce this exact issue right now. 

With the plugin enabled, when I launch via `tmux` it does not load my `$HOME/.tmux.conf`. 

When I disable the plugin and launch via `tmux` OR run it directly via the binary path (`/opt/homebrew/bin/tmux`), it loads my `$HOME/.tmux.conf`.

I can't reproduce this issue, using the latest .tmux and default settings: https://asciinema.org/a/SDK12FjXqhIYY9zrnCFcFLLzQ

Make sure that all variables are set by default, including for .tmux and for OMZ tmux plugin. It looks like if you're setting some .tmux variable it needs to go before OMZ is sourced [^1].

Also provide the trace after running the command below and running `tmux` (when it doesn't work):

```sh
functions -t _zsh_tmux_plugin_run
```

[^1]: https://github.com/gpakosz/.tmux/issues/645#issuecomment-1538717018

body:
I'm not understanding the logic order that's causing this but not when autostart is enabled.

I believe it might be that the `ZSH_TMUX_CONFIG` variable is not set at the right order perhaps? Could you share your `.zshrc` file?

It could also be a good idea to run the command manually and add `-vv` to see the tmux trace logs:
```sh
tmux -vv -f /Users/smayer/.oh-my-zsh/plugins/tmux/tmux.extra.conf new-session
```

body:
I had a similar issue to this when using zinit to load the tmux plugin. Even when ZSH_TMUX_CONFIG was set to ~/.tmux.conf it still wouldn't detect my standard conf file. 

I managed to find a workaround by adding: 

`source-file /home/usr/.tmux.conf` to my tmux.extra.conf.

author: carlosala

body:
With `GIT_TRACE=1`, run `git feat "abc"`

``` shell
09:52:40.338179 git.c:748               trace: exec: git-feat abc
09:52:40.338307 run-command.c:661       trace: run_command: git-feat abc
09:52:40.343128 run-command.c:661       trace: run_command: 'a() {
local _scope _attention _message
while [ $# -ne 0 ]; do
case $1 in
  -s | --scope )
    if [ -z $2 ]; then
      echo "Missing scope'\!'"
      return 1
    fi
    _scope="$2"
    shift 2
    ;;
  -a | --attention )
    _attention="'\!'"
    shift 1
    ;;
  * )
    _message+=" $1"
    shift 1
    ;;
esac
done
git commit -m "feat${_scope:+(${_scope})}${_attention}:${_message}"
}; a' abc
a() {
local _scope _attention _message
while [ $# -ne 0 ]; do
case $1 in
  -s | --scope )
    if [ -z $2 ]; then
      echo "Missing scope!"
      return 1
    fi
    _scope="$2"
    shift 2
    ;;
  -a | --attention )
    _attention="!"
    shift 1
    ;;
  * )
    _message+=" $1"
    shift 1
    ;;
esac
done
git commit -m "feat${_scope:+(${_scope})}${_attention}:${_message}"
}; a: 18: _message+= abc: not found
09:52:40.348725 git.c:462               trace: built-in: git commit -m feat:
```

BTW according to the log, I think that's a pure shell script problem.

For example, if I create a file called `test.zsh`
``` shell
while [ $# -ne 0 ]; do
_message=""
case $1 in
  * )
    echo "argument: $1"
    _message+=" $1"
    shift 1
    ;;
esac
done
echo $_message
```
Give the script `755` permission, then run `./test.zsh "abc"`

>argument: abc
./test.zsh: 6: _message+= abc: not found
author: yf-yang

body:
Ah, let me give you a minimum reproducible code:
``` shell
_message=""
variable="abc"
_message+=" $variable"
echo $_message
```

Put the code to `test.zsh`, give permission 755, run `./test.zsh`, then
> ./test.zsh: 3: _message+= abc: not found

Instead, directly copy paste the code into zsh, it can run.

body:
Don't ask why, but I have a `.zshrc` of 1 605 lines of code 😅 so I wouldn't want to send the logs.

But I took a look myself, and found this:
```sh
# autoload -Uz compinit && compinit
autoload -Uz compinit
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1577> autoload -Uz compinit
if [[ $(date +'%j') != $(stat -f '%Sm' -t '%j' ~/.zcompdump) ]]; then
	compinit
else
	compinit -C
fi
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> [[+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> date +%j
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> [[ 102 != +/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> stat -f %Sm -t %j /Users/revolt/.zcompdump
stat: /Users/revolt/.zcompdump: stat: No such file or directory
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> [[ 102 != +/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> stat -f %Sm -t %j /Users/revolt/.zcompdump
stat: /Users/revolt/.zcompdump: stat: No such file or directory
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1578> [[ 102 !=  ]]
+/Users/revolt/.zshrc:1579> compinit
```

So, these lines:
```sh
# The following lines were added by compinstall
zstyle ':completion:*' completer _expand _complete _ignored _correct _approximate
zstyle ':completion:*' expand prefix suffix
zstyle ':completion:*' format 'Completing %d'
zstyle ':completion:*' group-name ''
zstyle ':completion:*' list-suffixes true
zstyle :compinstall filename "${HOME}/.zshrc"

fpath+=~/.zfunc

# autoload -Uz compinit && compinit
autoload -Uz compinit
if [[ $(date +'%j') != $(stat -f '%Sm' -t '%j' ~/.zcompdump) ]]; then
	compinit
else
	compinit -C
fi
_comp_options+=(globdots)
# End of lines added by compinstall
```
got added to my `.zshrc` file when I ran `compinstall` some many years ago.

Should I just delete the part that calls `stat`? 🤔

body: Thanks for the update, and it looks like you figured it out. Oh My Zsh is essentially doing the `compinit` itself, so indeed you are free to remove those lines (at least the `compinit` part).

author: carlosala

body:
We found the culprit and I committed the fix in 06753e8. For now, the async prompt for git is turned off by default. You can however test it out with
```sh
zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt yes
```
We're quite proud of this one, and although it needs some work, we think it will get rid of all the "slow git prompt" issues, as it will now be rendered asynchronously.

Here's a recording of a before/after enabling on a shallow copy of the git repo https://github.com/torvalds/linux (which is a good test for slowness).

[![asciicast](https://asciinema.org/a/OBK9cA3Yc6kvU87BHm2jooL4v.svg)](https://asciinema.org/a/OBK9cA3Yc6kvU87BHm2jooL4v)

Please test and send in your feedback! Thanks!!
author: mcornella

body: @mcornella that seems to be working fine on the first few runs. I must say that already on my small git repos I can feel the difference. Amazing work!
author: vanniktech

body:
This is now enabled by default since [ec1afe9](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/commit/ec1afe9dd683c36e6384db25fc1e95acbb0cbc7a) after fixing some logic and performance issues.

[Disabling it is done the same way](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh#disable-async-git-prompt), but you should not experience any issues and instead get the full awesomeness of async prompt.

Please comment here or reopen if you have any issues!

body:
@coradinibr I had the same issue, to fix it you have to switch the git info command in `$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/themes/pygmalion.zsh-theme`:
```
#local gitinfo=$(git_prompt_info)                                                       
local gitinfo=$(_omz_git_prompt_info)
```
author: tomncooper

body: I was using a custom theme (bullet-train) and I needed to replace `git_prompt_info` call to `_omz_git_prompt_info` call and it worked again
author: naveedkakal

body:
Don't do this:

> I was using a custom theme (bullet-train) and I needed to replace `git_prompt_info` call to `_omz_git_prompt_info` call and it worked again

Instead, add
```sh
zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no
```
to your `.zshrc` file, before Oh My Zsh is sourced. This will temporarily disable the async prompt until we have a better fix. Relying on the current function names will break in the very near future.

Please track #12328 for updates.
author: mcornella

body:
Hi there, please note that powerlevel10k is not related to Oh My Zsh, the proper place to ask is on the official repository. 

However, this is an easy answer. What you are seeing is a prompt without username, and only showing the current directory path. In this case, `~` is shown because you are in your home directory, also abbreviated to a tilde character (`~`).

If you want to customize your powerlevel10k prompt to show other details, such as your username, you can use its configuration wizard: https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k#configuration-wizard
author: mcornella

I have just fixed it by noticing by changing my locale file.

```
#LC_ALL=C
LC_ALL=en_GB.UTF-8
LANG=en_GB.UTF-8
```

Commented out LC_ALL=C was the issue.

flutter_3_0_course  --  2 windows (created Tue Feb 20 20:40:50 2024)
flutter_randomizer  --  2 windows (created Mon Mar 18 21:10:47 2024) (attached)
```

For my own curiosity, why is something like the following not used?
```
function _build_tmux_alias {
  eval "if [[ -z \$1 ]] || [[ \${1:0:1} == '-' ]]; then
    alias \$1=\"tmux $2\"
  else
    alias \$1=\"tmux $2 $3\"
  fi"
}
```
I did a quick test just now and it seems to work OK without having to reimplement any of the completions. And I imagine it would stand up to any completion updates in the future.

author: hwti

body:
With the two empty `_omz_register_handler` in my example, I see :
 - on each prompt, `/usr/bin/true` and `/usr/bin/cat` are executed
 - when the shell is frozen, it is reading on the read end of a pipe connected to the stdout of `cat`

With `set -x`, the last line is `+_omz_async_callback:15> _OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]=+_omz_async_callback:15> cat
`.
So it's the `_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]="$(cat <&$fd)"`.
With `echo aaa` in `function foo()`, I see with `set -x` that when I get the issue the `+foo:2> echo aaa` doesn't appear : it looks like the handler process is blocked.
Replacing `(exit $ret)` by `() { return $ret }` seems to fix the issue when using the empty handlers at least (and it is faster).
I don't know why the subshell would block (so delay the handler) when I have the issue.

But I don't understand how the `async_prompt.zsh` is supposed to work.
`builtin echo $handler` triggers `_omz_async_callback` : it reads `handler`, and then run the `cat <&$fd` which waits for the handler process to exit (or close stdout).
With a slow handler (for example `sleep 2`), I clearly see the shell blocking on almost all prompts, so it doesn't seem to be asynchronous at all !
Without this issue maybe the `(exit $ret)` could still cause problems, but it would be a delay for the async handler, not the shell being frozen.
Ideally the callback would only run on `HUP` (when the handler process exits), but `zle -F` doesn't allow to select what we want.

Btw, the two exec could probably be avoided :
 - the `$(cat <&$fd)` could be replaced by something like `read -r -d '' -u $fd`.
 - It seems the `command true` is no longer needed on zsh >= 5.8 : https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions/pull/612
author: hwti

body:
Hi Loïc, thanks for the thorough report and the improvement pointers!

I've been testing out your reproduction and I can see the same issue. It looks like `zle` is calling the `_omz_async_callback` function even though the fd has no data ready to read, which is not what we intended to happen.

For example, with this modification
```diff
 # Called when new data is ready to be read from the pipe
 function _omz_async_callback() {
   emulate -L zsh
+  zmodload zsh/zselect
 
   local fd=$1   # First arg will be fd ready for reading
   local err=$2  # Second arg will be passed in case of error
@@ -116,18 +113,23 @@ function _omz_async_callback() {
   if [[ -z "$err" || "$err" == "hup" ]]; then
     # Get handler name from first line
     local handler
-    read handler <&$fd
+    read -u $fd handler
 
     # Store old output which is supposed to be already printed
     local old_output="${_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]}"
 
     # Read output from fd
-    _OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]="$(cat <&$fd)"
-
-    # Repaint prompt if output has changed
-    if [[ "$old_output" != "${_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]}" ]]; then
-      zle reset-prompt
-      zle -R
+    if ! zselect -t 0 -r $fd; then
+      zle -M "$fd ($handler): no data to read
+$(ls -l /proc/$$/fd)"
+    else
+      read -u $fd -r -d '' "_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]"
+
+      # Repaint prompt if output has changed
+      if [[ "$old_output" != "${_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[$handler]}" ]]; then
+        zle reset-prompt
+        zle -R
+      fi
     fi
 
     # Close the fd
```
I can see printed that the pipe does not yet have any data to read, even though it is readable (and therefore zle calls the callback):
```
➜  ohmyzsh git:(master) ✗
12 (foo): no data to read
```
We'll have to research this further and find the proper way to go about this before a full release. Thanks again for the report!
author: mcornella

body:
> I can see printed that the pipe does not yet have any data to read, even though it is readable (and therefore zle calls the callback):

It has data to read when `_omz_async_callback` is called  : the handler name (read by `read handler <&$fd`).

In my PR, I stored the handler name in an associative array (to get it back from the fd), instead of writing it to the pipe.
The PID is still written, but it is read before setting the callback, so now `_omz_async_callback` is only called when the completion function writes its output (ideally right at the end).

I still don't know what happens with the subshell (which created the full shell freeze, even with empty async handlers, when combined with the wait in `_omz_async_callback`).
Most of the time it only delays the prompt update by about 10-20ms (compared to using a function).
But on the first prompt (in Dolphin / Kate especially), I can still sometimes see a 10s delay for the first handler, and a 18-20s delay for the second one (and if I press Ctrl+D, the terminal panel only closes after the delay).
That's why my PR is still in draft : replacing `(exit $ret)` by `() { return $ret }` is faster and avoids the issue, but I would like to understand why (especially since async handler might to use subshells).

I will create separate PRs for the optimization (`true` / `cat`) and to make `git_prompt_status` asynchronous.

body:
Hi @seisenreich, thanks for the report! What's happening is that we are looking for `docker` in the `$PATH` on load, to figure out the version.

Since the `$PATH` is only updated at the bottom of the `.zshrc` file, it is too late as Oh My Zsh has already loaded. For this we suggest to put `$PATH` modifications right at the top of the `.zshrc` file, or within the `.zshenv` file if preferred.

So even though Rancher put the lines there, I think pasting them at the top of the `.zshrc` file should be enough. Please reopen if that's not the case!

author: marcospgp

body:
This is expected behavior if you are adding paths to `$PATH` without checking if they're already there. The same happens if you reload the shell with `source ~/.zshrc` or other equivalent approaches.

There is no way to undo that, as by the time you run `exec zsh` to replace the shell, the `$PATH` value (already changed) gets passed down to the new shell. The only possible way to undo this if we force a new environment on `omz reload`, but that would have unintended consequences and possibly break stuff much worse.

The 2 approaches you have are:
1. Check if a directory is in `$PATH` before adding it: https://stackoverflow.com/a/1397020
2. Use `typeset -U path` (zsh-only) which will remove all non-unique entries of `$PATH` (`path` is a special variable in zsh which is the array form of `$PATH`, `-U` says remove make the array contents unique).

author: dadastoor

body:
So here's a short term and a mid term answer.

In the short term, there are no current guarantees whether a lib file is or isn't supposed to be loaded. For now, there is a hodge podge of logic within each lib file, and it is a possibility that a lib file requires one of the previous ones (sorted alphabetically). If you have a custom setup, where only some lib files are sourced, some functionality will be broken if there are lib file changes such as the one you encountered. 

We realise that many people use plugin managers, or have custom files to override lib files. Some of the appeal of this is that some lib files have side effects or add performance overhead on startup.

That's why, in the mid term, we are thinking of separating required logic (in essence, a folder of loadable functions, maybe via `autoload`) so that all Oh My Zsh users load them on startup as required, including this automatically out of the box on plugin managers. The contract on those functions / APIs would be that they don't have a performance impact, and they don't have side effects. This will improve the integration with plugin managers. 

But, for now, the `async_prompt` lib file is indeed required.

vcs_status() {

  else
    _omz_git_prompt_info # https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/12328#issuecomment-2041350726

  fi
}

It seems that adding `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` to my zshrc will cause the PTY integration of VS code to hang in the current versions of code. So if you are using VS code with the debugger, you may not want to use this as a work-around to the bug.

Work-around if you are using vscode:
```
BULLETTRAIN_PROMPT_ORDER=(
  dir
  git
  status
)


if [ "$TERM_PROGRAM" = "vscode" ]; then
  BULLETTRAIN_PROMPT_ORDER=(
    dir
    status
  )
else
  # Fix git status
  zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no
fi
source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

> ### Describe the bug

author: hodgechung

body:
Hi folks, thanks for all the reports and logs and such. Unfortunately we are unable to reproduce the issue (i.e. get it to core-dump). We have tested using CentOS 7 containers, as well as with a VM. However, based on https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/12331#issuecomment-2044520298 we see that the segfault happens at the `get_widget` function. 

Checking the blame for zsh, we found that a segfault was fixed on zsh 5.0.6 ([commit](https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh/commit/97115e0e7f33378390aabd3c15d0cd69bf8d191c) and [related zsh-workers thread](https://www.zsh.org/mla/workers/2014/msg00237.html)). We were able to see that 5.0.6 was the first version to also not be affected by the [stale branch info reported](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/12331#issuecomment-2047444770).

We therefore believe that 5.0.6 will not have the issue. I have also a hunch that `get_widget` will not be called if we replace `zle reset-prompt` with `zle .reset-prompt`, and as such I have raised #12358, which you can test by running `omz pr test 12358`. Please test it out and let us know whether this bypasses the crash, or otherwise it stays the same.

If we are unable to find the root cause, and as we can't successfully reproduce it, we will then disable the feature for zsh < 5.0.6, and just recommend that people upgrade (we know this is sometimes not a possibility, therefore we'll just turn off the async feature by default).
