author: dylanroman03

body:
**Solution**:  
To avoid the conflict between `tldr-command-line` and `sudo-command-line`, it is recommended to change the key bindings so that each function uses a different trigger. Specifically, bind `tldr-command-line` to the combination of **`Esc`** and **`T`**, instead of just using the `Esc` key. This ensures that both functions can be used without interfering with each other.

**Steps to Implement the Solution**:
1. Locate the following [original bind key line](https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/blob/750d3ac4b493dca13ef0ced55fa6a2cd02dc7ee8/plugins/tldr/tldr.plugin.zsh#L18):
   ```shell
   bindkey "\e" tldr-command-line
   ```
2. Change it to:
   ```shell
   bindkey "\et" tldr-command-line
   ```
   This will trigger `tldr-command-line` with **`Esc`** followed by **`T`**, while **`Esc` + `Esc`** remains available for `sudo-command-line`.

body: @todfox-net was my original code on the pr correct? https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/pull/13189 I wrote it a bit simplistically and it was improved to use a better approach, i'm wondering if that logic was backwards in the improved version
author: zack6849

body:
Yes, the logic seems reversed in the new pr.

Originally it had -
	"if (( major < 2 || (major == 2 && minor < 9) )); then"
which checks if it's _less than_ 2.9 and applies the line.

In the new script pr it has -
	"if is-at-least 2.9 "$version_string"; then"
which checks if it's _at least_(_greater than or equal to_) 2.9 and applies the line.

However, as the code inside the if/then/else block wasn't similarly flipped, the --agents code is being applied against 2.9 and not against the earlier versions.

body:
In that case, it's obvious that you need to provide a start commit  and end commit because the update mean something has been changed since a specific point, right?


```
cd ~/.oh-my-zsh
git checkout HEAD~10
omz update
```


is this what you need?
author: roeniss

body:
If I understand correctly, you want a list of changelogs for all updates done, each update changelog in its own section. 

What you want is not possible. What you can get:
- A single changelog of the last update: `omz changelog`
- (Undocumented) A single changelog of a particular commit range: `omz changelog HEAD HEAD~10`
- (Undocumented) A single changelog of all reachable commits: `omz changelog HEAD --all`

We don't have tags / proper tagged versions, we only update to latest commits. And since we don't keep track of the commits that were 'latest" for you, it's not possible to get what you want. But hopefully the above helps.

body:
I made a project that will let you do exactly this: https://github.com/mattmc3/omz-plus

TLDR:

```zsh
# sample .zshrc

# OMZ PLUS! lets you use git repos for your theme
ZSH_THEME=romkatv/powerlevel10k

# OMZ PLUS! lets you use git repos in your plugins array
plugins=(
  git
  extract
  zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions
  zsh-users/zsh-completions
)

# OMZ PLUS! lets you use multiple paths for your ZSH_CUSTOM
zsh_custom=(
  $ZDOTDIR/custom
  $ZDOTDIR/custom.local
)

# Enable OMZ PLUS! by simply sourcing it immediately before oh-my-zsh
source /path/to/omz-plus.sh
source /path/to/oh-my-zsh.sh
```

body:
Hi @jmschp, thank you for the suggestion. 

I'm trying to find docs for these commands to confirm they're like this, and if there are recommendations for how to run them, but I didn't find anything specific. Could you provide some references?

If db name needs to be provided like this, as part of the `db:[action]` argument, I can suggest creating a dedicated function for it, that appends the db name. This limits the need for many dedicated aliases, and if possible, we could also provide completion for it.

Example:
```sh
function rdr {
  rails db:rollback:$1
}

# used like
rdr primary
# and also as a custom user alias
alias rdrp='rdr primary'
```

@mcornella I couldn't find docs either, but check the code where the tasks are generated.
https://github.com/rails/rails/blob/681c86674fa16996e4563b93aab4e9c494f6a651/activerecord/lib/active_record/railties/databases.rake#L259

Not sure if you are a Rails guy, But as far as I am aware there is no other way to invoke the task, the syntax is `rails db:rollback:primary`

The annoying part is that if you are in a multiple database application you will get an error, if you use `rdr` such as:

```
You're using a multiple database application. To use `db:rollback` you must run the namespaced task with a VERSION. Available tasks are db:rollback:primary and db:rollback:cache.
```

You suggestion with a function seems nice.

body:
Reopening because this is still an issue. 

@sauchye could you post the output of `locale -a`? Also post the output of `setopt` to see the regex version configured. 

According to Gemini this is an underlying error from the C library performing the regex match, which depends on the locale:

> Locale Setting (LC_CTYPE): Zsh, like many Unix utilities, uses your shell's LC_CTYPE (or LANG or LC_ALL) environment variable to determine how to interpret byte sequences as characters. On modern systems, this is often set to a UTF-8 locale (e.g., en_US.UTF-8).
>
> ​Regular Expression Engine: When Zsh performs a regex match (using [[ string =~ regex ]] or certain glob qualifiers), it passes the string and the pattern to the underlying regex library.
>
> ​Invalid Byte Sequence: If the string being matched contains a sequence of bytes that does not form a valid character in the character encoding defined by LC_CTYPE (e.g., a non-start byte where a start byte is expected in UTF-8), the regex library function will fail and return an error code like EILSEQ (Illegal byte sequence).
> 
> ​Zsh Error Handling: Zsh's internal code that calls the regex library (likely in a file related to pattern matching, such as Src/zsh_regex.c or similar utility files) checks this error code and translates it into the shell error message: regex matching error: illegal byte sequence.

body:
Hey! `nvm` expects `$NVM_DIR` to contain `nvm.sh`. For example, [Arch Linux init script](https://gitlab.archlinux.org/archlinux/packaging/packages/nvm/-/blob/6607170a1d3b2f524a7e045e3eb4de11b6625b97/init-nvm.sh) creates a symbolic link for that.
I don't think the right solution to the problem is what you're proposing, but rather fix your environment to ensure `nvm.sh` is available in `$NVM_DIR`, with a symlink, for instance.

Besides that, I don't see any references to `$NVM_SOURCE` inside `nvm.sh`, not sure where that comes from.
author: carlosala

body: You can check [here](https://github.com/nvm-sh/nvm?tab=readme-ov-file#additional-notes) it says that you can customise the nvm source through NVM_SOURCE. When nvm is installed systemwide and NVM_DIR is set to that it requires root in order to install nodejs versions the solution to that would be to have different path where nvm would store the nodejs binaries. Overall NVM_SOURCE is the directory where nvm is actually installed and the NVM_DIR is the directory where it keeps the nodejs stuffs.

body:
Hi Adrian, I've checked the root cause. I had no issues with using `'{0,1}` as a pattern in `grep -E` under macOS, with the exact same version of grep. Also in openBSD and freeBSD this was documented as an available option in man.

I think what's happening is a version of this: https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/13016#issuecomment-2746264880

In line 43, there is a pattern built that could contain negative numbers. When I test in your same system, I see:
```console
$ grep -E "'{0,1}" <<< "hello world"
$ # no output
```
vs.
```console
$ grep -E "'{0,-1}" <<< "hello world"
grep: invalid repetition count(s)
$
```

There is a guard clause before line 43 to prevent invalid range, so there is maybe something else happening here.

Could you provide an exact trace file of the function, to see exactly where it fails? You can do so by adding this at the bottom of our zshrc file, and copying the trace when the error happens
```sh
functions -t preexec_alias-finder
```
author: mcornella

author: mcornella

body:
Hi Larry, you can try to reproduce this with barebones zsh.

To do so, run `zsh -f`, and enable completion with 
```
autoload -Uz compinit && compinit
```
Then try to reproduce the issue, see if the loop still happens.

If it doesn't, it's probable that it may have something to do with matcher-list zstyle variables, check whichever you have set up via
```
zstyle -L | grep matcher-list
```
and run it in the barebones zsh session.

Those are defined in `lib/completion.zsh` btw. If this still doesn't do it, maybe there is another completion related zstyle setting (check `zstyle -L` for anything that's suspicious).

You can also run `functions -t _main_complete` before completing, in your main shell, to get a trace output of what's specifically running in zsh code when it gets to that loop.

author: larry-cdn77

body:
OK, thanks for all the information, I could reproduce the same thing under zsh 5.9.

With this info, I was able to find that this has been reported in the zsh-workers mailing list (53994) [^1]. It seems it's directly connected to substring matching (completing from a substring in a filename, not from the start), and also requires multibyte support and a supporting locale [^2].

Unfortunately the report was very recent, and the patch is still in consideration, so it hasn't made it into any released version yet.

Moreover, it seems that there is no workaround that allows keeping substring completion. For now, you can use a different `matcher-list` configuration. For example this one below allows partial completion around separators (`f-b` completes to `foo-bar`, or `.zsh` completes to `a.zsh`):

```
zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list \
    'm:{[:lower:][:upper:]}={[:upper:][:lower:]}' \
    'r:|[._-]=* r:|=*'
```

I can add this option and document it as a known issue in the wiki.

[^1]: https://www.zsh.org/mla/workers/2025/msg00694.html
[^2]: https://www.zsh.org/mla/workers/2025/msg00696.html
author: mcornella

body:
OK, found a workaround, which is enabling true fuzzy matching. This doesn't trigger the bug, while still showing both options:
```sh
zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list 'm:{[:lower:][:upper:]}={[:upper:][:lower:]}' 'r:|=*' 'r:|?=**'
```
It may be more computationally expensive in some cases, and I haven't tested difficult stuff, so it may crash in some edge cases. But it works for this case.
author: mcornella

body:
#13436 will add the workarounds for this if you set either of these in your zshrc file, before OMZ is sourced:
```sh
zstyle :omz:completion fuzzy-match yes
zstyle :omz:completion substring-match no
```

body: Thanks, good to know what the cause is and to have a workaround!

body:
Hey @MKKurbandibirov thank you for the report. As you've said, this error happens because of unavailable processes, but the culprit could be many things, not just because the error happens in an Oh My Zsh function it'd be caused by it.

Is there any way for you to capture the command responsible? I assume you should be able to capture what parent process is generating some many children. One idea is to, at that point, close some apps, and then open Activity Monitor.

> [!NOTE]
> - If Activity Monitor doesn't show the big number of processes, maybe the parent was one of the closed apps.
> - If you can't open Activity Monitor, maybe more apps need to be closed.

If that doesn't show you the culprit, and if this is reproducible, you could set some kind of background script to take snapshots of the running processes and save it to a file, for later analysis.
author: mcornella
ankk13

body: You can set trace mode (essentially, add `set -xv` at the top of your zshrc or zshenv file). You'll have to do this from an editor program. Then when you start the terminal, you'll see all commands run. You can take a look right before the error happens and see if there's any specific pattern of commands or background jobs run

body:
Note that upstream zsh has added support for this, at least in vi-mode with registers similar to those of vim. And you'll need to wait for a new zsh release  or build from sources to get it.

For a pipe, the following does the job:
```
alias xc='openssl base64 -e -A|sed -e $''s/.*/\e]52;p;&\a/'''
```

author: oil666oil

body:
This is unfortunately unsupported in zsh as of today [^1] [^2], using `matcher-list` and `[[:upper:]]=[[:lower:]]`, which is what we use, because of multibyte not being supported.

The only option as of yet is to manually specify the upper and lower- case characters with the accents, umlauts and symbols in general. You can see [^2] for your specific case.

However, for us to support this out of the box would require a long list of characters (apparently there are more than 1000 pairs of upper/lower case characters with symbols in the Unicode spec). As of now, it's infeasible for us to add a workaround that covers all cases. The ideal method would be for zsh to add support for it.

[^1]: https://zsh.org/mla/users/2011/msg00014.html
[^2]: https://zsh.org/mla/users/2018/msg00618.html

body:
I can't reproduce that, and actually, I've been using ZSH as default shell, default terminal in VSCode with Github Copilot (and Codex, and Claude), on at least 2 different macOS versions and 3 different Linux distros and never had any issue. The steps to reproduce are insufficient to reproduce. 

You mention `path.d`. IIRC that doesn't exist in macOS. macOS has the `path_helper` utility that reads `/etc/paths.d` and at least the latest macOS version apparently correctly treats path items with whitespaces on them. (you can test that by creating entries, then inspecting the output `path_helper -s` and/or inspecting the path entries (e.g.: `print -l ${path}`)

> If I disable all the Copilot extensions, then there is no problem:

I bet the underlying problem is still there, you just don't see the symptoms. As pointed out, in a stock macOS installation you definitely have `sed`, `tr` and `tail` in the path. Hence, you most likely did something else that changed your environment in a way that fundamentally made those tools inaccessible to the script being executed.

Carlos,

Here is the zshrc file I am using.

```# If you come from bash you might have to change your $PATH.
# export PATH=$HOME/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:/usr/local/bin:$PATH

# Path to your Oh My Zsh installation.
export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh"

# Set name of the theme to load --- if set to "random", it will
# load a random theme each time Oh My Zsh is loaded, in which case,
# to know which specific one was loaded, run: echo $RANDOM_THEME
# See https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/wiki/Themes
ZSH_THEME="avit"

# Set list of themes to pick from when loading at random
# Setting this variable when ZSH_THEME=random will cause zsh to load
# a theme from this variable instead of looking in $ZSH/themes/
# If set to an empty array, this variable will have no effect.
# ZSH_THEME_RANDOM_CANDIDATES=( "robbyrussell" "agnoster" )

# Uncomment the following line to use case-sensitive completion.
# CASE_SENSITIVE="true"

# Uncomment the following line to use hyphen-insensitive completion.
# Case-sensitive completion must be off. _ and - will be interchangeable.
# HYPHEN_INSENSITIVE="true"

# Uncomment one of the following lines to change the auto-update behavior
# zstyle ':omz:update' mode disabled  # disable automatic updates
# zstyle ':omz:update' mode auto      # update automatically without asking
# zstyle ':omz:update' mode reminder  # just remind me to update when it's time

# Uncomment the following line to change how often to auto-update (in days).
# zstyle ':omz:update' frequency 13

# Uncomment the following line if pasting URLs and other text is messed up.
# DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS="true"

# Uncomment the following line to disable colors in ls.
# DISABLE_LS_COLORS="true"

# Uncomment the following line to disable auto-setting terminal title.
DISABLE_AUTO_TITLE="false"

# Uncomment the following line to enable command auto-correction.
# ENABLE_CORRECTION="true"

# Uncomment the following line to display red dots whilst waiting for completion.
# You can also set it to another string to have that shown instead of the default red dots.
# e.g. COMPLETION_WAITING_DOTS="%F{yellow}waiting...%f"
# Caution: this setting can cause issues with multiline prompts in zsh < 5.7.1 (see #5765)
# COMPLETION_WAITING_DOTS="true"

# Uncomment the following line if you want to disable marking untracked files
# under VCS as dirty. This makes repository status check for large repositories
# much, much faster.
# DISABLE_UNTRACKED_FILES_DIRTY="true"

# Uncomment the following line if you want to change the command execution time
# stamp shown in the history command output.
# You can set one of the optional three formats:
# "mm/dd/yyyy"|"dd.mm.yyyy"|"yyyy-mm-dd"
# or set a custom format using the strftime function format specifications,
# see 'man strftime' for details.
# HIST_STAMPS="mm/dd/yyyy"

# Would you like to use another custom folder than $ZSH/custom?
# ZSH_CUSTOM=/path/to/new-custom-folder

# Which plugins would you like to load?
# Standard plugins can be found in $ZSH/plugins/
# Custom plugins may be added to $ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins/
# Example format: plugins=(rails git textmate ruby lighthouse)
# Add wisely, as too many plugins slow down shell startup.
plugins=(git zsh-autosuggestions you-should-use zsh-syntax-highlighting)

source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh

# User configuration

# export MANPATH="/usr/local/man:$MANPATH"

# You may need to manually set your language environment
# export LANG=en_US.UTF-8

# Preferred editor for local and remote sessions
# if [[ -n $SSH_CONNECTION ]]; then

# Compilation flags

# Set personal aliases, overriding those provided by Oh My Zsh libs,
# plugins, and themes. Aliases can be placed here, though Oh My Zsh
# users are encouraged to define aliases within a top-level file in

author: tullo-x86

body:
I’ve decided to close this issue for the time being. The shortcut in question is one of many optional web searches in this plugin, and inclusion does not imply endorsement.

Oh My Zsh is simply initiating a browser-based search, and we have to trust our users to use these tools responsibly. Our guiding principle is to provide tooling that helps developers do their work.

We wouldn’t remove an optional tool unless it were legally taken down or rendered unusable. Given that this addition was contributed and maintained, we’ll assume users have found value in the functionality.

I appreciate the research and effort that went into raising this topic. While I understand some may be disappointed with this outcome, the project isn’t in a position to arbitrate these issues beyond the scope of developer tooling.
author: robbyrussell

body:
For anyone else who comes across this issue later and wonders what this is referring to: this alias simply takes a query, constructs a search URL, and opens the user’s default web browser to run that search on the selected site.

It does not fetch, display, store, filter, or moderate any content in the terminal. It’s one of ~40 optional web search shortcuts defined in the plugin.

author: mcornella

body:
@mcornella @technoweenie @atmos @drnic I think I see why this happens with async-prompt enabled.

In async mode, `git_prompt_info` is redefined to only print the cached async output (it basically echoes `_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[_omz_git_prompt_info]` if it’s set). The async worker that populates that cache is only registered if OMZ detects the literal pattern `$(git_prompt_info)` (or backticks) directly inside PS1/RPROMPT/etc.

So when the prompt uses a wrapper like `$(_my_git_prompt_info)` or `$(_my_theme_vcs_info)`, the string `$(git_prompt_info)` never appears in the prompt variables → the deferred registration never triggers → `_omz_git_prompt_info` is never run → `_OMZ_ASYNC_OUTPUT[...]` stays empty → `git_prompt_info` prints nothing.

This also explains why disabling async-prompt makes it work again: in that mode `git_prompt_info` calls `_omz_git_prompt_info` synchronously.

Possible ways forward:
- Update the jj plugin README example to mention this limitation and recommend either:
  - `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt force` (so handlers are registered unconditionally), or
  - `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` (fully synchronous), or
  - using `_omz_git_prompt_info` for the fallback if you accept synchronous execution.
- Or (code fix) make `git_prompt_info`/`git_prompt_status` auto-register their async handler on first invocation (lazy registration), so wrapper calls still trigger async population.

Happy to open a PR (README change or code change) if @mcornella agree with the preferred approach.

body:
Yes, hitting this on Ghostty on macOS. 

monkey-patch below, but it is suspicious if this is just a Ghostty issue.

```diff
--- themes/jonathan.zsh-theme   2025-08-01 19:00:15
+++ custom/themes/jonathan.zsh-theme    2026-05-22 20:15:30
@@ -102,25 +102,13 @@
 fi

 # Finally, the prompt.
-PROMPT='${PR_SET_CHARSET}${PR_STITLE}${(e)PR_TITLEBAR}\
-${PR_CYAN}${PR_ULCORNER}${PR_HBAR}${PR_GREY}(\
-${PR_GREEN}%${PR_PWDLEN}<...<%~%<<\
-${PR_GREY})$(virtualenv_prompt_info)$(ruby_prompt_info)$(conda_prompt_info)${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_HBAR}${(e)PR_FILLBAR}${PR_HBAR}${PR_GREY}(\
-${PR_CYAN}%(!.%SROOT%s.%n)${PR_GREY}@${PR_GREEN}%m:%l\
-${PR_GREY})${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_URCORNER}\
+# Each logical line is a single string to avoid \<newline> in single quotes
+# (zsh treats \ as literal in single quotes, breaking the prompt layout).
+PROMPT='${PR_SET_CHARSET}${PR_STITLE}${(e)PR_TITLEBAR}${PR_CYAN}${PR_ULCORNER}${PR_HBAR}${PR_GREY}(${PR_GREEN}%${PR_PWDLEN}<...<%~%<<${PR_GREY})$(virtualenv_prompt_info)$(ruby_prompt_info)$(conda_prompt_info)${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_HBAR}${(e)PR_FILLBAR}${PR_HBAR}${PR_GREY}(${PR_CYAN}%(!.%SROOT%s.%n)${PR_GREY}@${PR_GREEN}%m:%l${PR_GREY})${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_URCORNER}
+${PR_CYAN}${PR_LLCORNER}${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}(${PR_YELLOW}%D{%H:%M:%S}${PR_LIGHT_BLUE}%{$reset_color%}$(git_prompt_info)$(git_prompt_status)${PR_BLUE})${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_HBAR}>${PR_NO_COLOUR} '

-${PR_CYAN}${PR_LLCORNER}${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}(\
-${PR_YELLOW}%D{%H:%M:%S}\
-${PR_LIGHT_BLUE}%{$reset_color%}$(git_prompt_info)$(git_prompt_status)${PR_BLUE})${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}\
-${PR_HBAR}\
->${PR_NO_COLOUR} '
-
 # display exitcode on the right when > 0
 return_code="%(?..%{$fg[red]%}%? ↵ %{$reset_color%})"
-RPROMPT=' $return_code${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}\
-(${PR_YELLOW}%D{%a,%b%d}${PR_BLUE})${PR_HBAR}${PR_CYAN}${PR_LRCORNER}${PR_NO_COLOUR}'
+RPROMPT=' $return_code${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}(${PR_YELLOW}%D{%a,%b%d}${PR_BLUE})${PR_HBAR}${PR_CYAN}${PR_LRCORNER}${PR_NO_COLOUR}'

-PS2='${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}\
-${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}(\
-${PR_LIGHT_GREEN}%_${PR_BLUE})${PR_HBAR}\
-${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_NO_COLOUR} '
+PS2='${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_BLUE}${PR_HBAR}(${PR_LIGHT_GREEN}%_${PR_BLUE})${PR_HBAR}${PR_CYAN}${PR_HBAR}${PR_NO_COLOUR} '
```

body:
Thanks for the suggestion regarding the post-install reminder. Making the
backup message more prominent to help users migrate their PATH exports and
custom configurations is a great idea.


On Thu, Apr 2, 2026, 8:13 AM lishilong ***@***.***> wrote:

> *XiaoLi-XiaoTian* created an issue (ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh#13661)
> <https://github.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/issues/13661>
> Is your feature request related to a problem?
>
> Hi there! First of all, thanks for the awesome project — I use Oh My Zsh
> every day and love it. 🙌
>
> I just ran into a small hiccup after a fresh install: the installer
> replaced my ~/.zshrc with the default template (and kindly backed up the
> old one to ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh). However, I didn't notice the backup
> message in the terminal output, so I spent a while wondering why several
> CLI tools (that relied on PATH entries and shell functions defined in my
> old .zshrc) suddenly stopped working.
>
> I imagine this is a fairly common experience for new users who have
> accumulated custom configurations — conda init blocks, proxy settings, PATH
> exports, shell functions, etc.
> Describe the solution you'd like
>
> It would be really helpful if the installer printed a *more prominent
> reminder* after installation, something like:
>
> ⚠️  Your previous ~/.zshrc has been backed up to ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
>     If you had custom configurations (PATH exports, aliases, functions, etc.),
>     you may want to migrate them to your new ~/.zshrc.
>
>     You can compare the two files with:
>       diff ~/.zshrc ~/.zshrc.pre-oh-my-zsh
>
> This could be a simple addition to the post-install output — no changes to
> the actual install logic needed.
> Describe alternatives you've considered
>
>    - Automatically appending old custom configurations to the new .zshrc
>    — but this could introduce conflicts, so I understand why it's not done.

author: matheusfelipeog

body:
Hi, I stumbled across this ticket while diagnosing a similar issue and I'm afraid the diagnosis that you created with AI is misleading.

The agent is presenting it as if it's OK and doesn't matter that your fpath is getting reordered or has duplicates, and we can just normalize it, but that's not the case. The order in fpath matters, because depending on the order a different completion function might get picked.

So fix the root cause instead:

This will let you see the diff from one shell run to a next (usually, when the env carries over from one to a sub-shell which is also a login or interactive shell, however that'd happen, things will get invalidated like described):
```sh
diff -u <(echo $fpath | tr ' ' '\n') <(zsh -l -c 'echo $fpath; exit 0;' | tr ' ' '\n')
```

For each item you see, find what sets it, and gate it appropriately. For example, with homebrew, you can gate all of your init code with a `if [ -z "$HOMEBREW_PREFIX" ]` to avoid duplicating it in your .zshenv, etc.

If oh-my-zsh were to do anything about it, then I'd probably suggest a per-fpath-hashed compdump path in some cache dir, not normalization.
author: mologie

body:
Thanks @mologie — this is a fair catch and it genuinely improves the proposal. You're right that fpath order matters: the first dir in `$fpath` that ships a file with a given basename wins the `compdef` registration. I confirmed this by re-reading `Completion/compinit` upstream:

```zsh
typeset -A _i_test
for _i_dir in $fpath; do
  ...
  for _i_file in $_i_dir/^([^_]*|...)(N); do
    _i_name="${_i_file:t}"
    (( $+_i_test[$_i_name] + ... )) && continue   # first occurrence wins
    _i_test[$_i_name]=yes
```

So `${${(@onu)fpath}}` (sort + dedup) was overclaiming — two fpaths with the same set of entries in different order can resolve a colliding `_foo` to different functions, and sort-normalizing would mask that.

**Narrowing the fix to `${${(@u)fpath}}` (dedup only, no sort):** this preserves first-occurrence order, so the first dir that wins a collision under the live fpath is the same first dir that wins in the stored metadata. It's strictly safe under zsh's actual semantics:

```zsh
% fpath=(/b /a /c /a /b /d)
% echo "${${(@u)fpath}}"
/b /a /c /d            # first-occurrence order preserved, duplicates removed
```

### Empirical validation

I wrote a second reproduction specifically for the collision-reorder case you raised — two directories each shipping a `_git` completion file, with their order flipping between runs. Running each of {master, `(@u)`, `(@onu)`} through both reproductions gives:

| Variant | duplicate-injection case | collision-reorder case |
|---|---|---|
| `master` (unpatched) | ✗ cache rewritten every run | ✓ correctly invalidates |
| `${${(@u)fpath}}` (dedup only) | ✓ cache reused | ✓ correctly invalidates |
| `${${(@onu)fpath}}` (sort+dedup, rejected) | ✓ cache reused | ✗ **stale dump retained** |

`(@onu)` fails exactly the way you predicted — two fpaths with the same set in different order produce the same sorted string, so the dump is not rewritten and the stale cache serves the wrong `_git`. `(@u)` catches that case because the raw string still differs by first element.

### Performance numbers

With the narrower `${${(@u)fpath}}` form on a macOS arm64 box running zsh 5.9, benchmarked with `hyperfine --warmup 3 --min-runs 15`, and a `.zprofile` that alternates between injecting 1 and 2 copies of the same fpath entry to mimic real-world `brew shellenv` / pyenv / conda drift:

| Setup | master | `${${(@u)fpath}}` | speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 plugin (`git` only) | 2104 ms ± 144 | 279 ms ± 25 | 7.5× |
| 13 plugins (realistic) | 3442 ms ± 190 | 348 ms ± 23 | 9.9× |

For the record, the rejected `${${(@onu)fpath}}` form benched at 308 ms ± 25 at 1 plugin — marginally slower than `(@u)` because `(onu)` does more work, so the narrower form is also the faster one.

### Reproduction scripts

Two self-contained scripts in a `mktemp -d` sandbox (no contact with real `$HOME`):

- `reproduce-duplicates.sh` — tests the duplicate-injection case (the dominant real-world cause; alternates 1/2 copies of an fpath entry)
- `reproduce-collision-reorder.sh` — tests your case (two dirs both shipping `_git`, flipping order between runs)

Both are attached to this issue via the updated gist at https://gist.github.com/rndjams/c7a3e85348ecb348f2d937c81628e49a.

### On the hashed-compdump suggestion

I like it as a follow-up. Hashing the live `$fpath` into the dump filename gives each distinct fpath its own cache, so reorders of colliding non-duplicate entries would produce distinct caches automatically — strictly more correct than either normalization approach, and it auto-recovers if a user toggles between two fpath configurations. The trade-offs (cache-dir layout, cleanup policy for old hashes, transition from existing dumps, XDG compliance bikeshed) feel like enough complexity that it's better as a separate PR once the immediate bleeding is stopped. Happy to draft that as a follow-up if there's maintainer appetite; it's captured as "Option D" in the draft PR description.

I'll update the proposed solutions block on this issue and the draft PR to reflect the narrower `(@u)` form and credit your order-sensitivity point. Thanks again for catching this before it shipped.

author: BigBrainAFK

body:
How would you configure this? As an ordered list of files for specific folders, or for any and skip any not found? 

The first is hard, the second's easy but probably would have edge cases preventing from rollout to multiple users.

Also, just FYI that there's the option of writing a custom function that loads the files in order. You just need to call `parse_dotenv` with the path to the env file, once per file and in the order you want.

Ideally we'd avoid overcomplicating, but if it's a new feature that is opt-in only there's less risk.
author: mcornella

body:
> How would you configure this? As an ordered list of files for specific folders, or for any and skip any not found?
> 
> The first is hard, the second's easy but probably would have edge cases preventing from rollout to multiple users.

i would have imagined just treating the configurable file env var as a list. loop over each list entry, if file doesnt exist skip to next. otherwise just load like it already does. any edge cases would be more a user configuration thing since the default should just be only `.env` as it already is thus not changing anything really about current load results. This should also keep the result the same for people who already configured the env var with a custom filename to load.

This would obviously be the dream solution (at least for me) since it just means configuring an env var with the file list in my env before omz loads and then its up to me to not mess up.


> Also, just FYI that there's the option of writing a custom function that loads the files in order. You just need to call `parse_dotenv` with the path to the env file, once per file and in the order you want.

Hm that could work but also might get a bit ugly. Also unsure how this works when `cd` to another project.

> Ideally we'd avoid overcomplicating, but if it's a new feature that is opt-in only there's less risk.

Agreed. Thats why i thought one could leave the default to basically behave as it already does basically.
